tasks.title: Harden find_self_reading_test_fns scanner and cover attribute-gap edge case (follow-up to task 1059)
tasks.description: tree-sitter-reify/tests/build_logic_tests.rs — tighten test_self_read_paths_use_manifest_dir assertions and add one missing scanner edge case.
tasks.details:
From esc-1059-785 review triage (4 suggestions, 1 skipped):

(1) test_self_read_paths_use_manifest_dir is effectively tautological: find_self_reading_test_fns discovers tests whose body contains (THIS_FILE), then the loop asserts each body contains (THIS_FILE) — can never fail by construction. Only the `>= 3` count is a real guard, and it's stale: if a 4th self-reading test is added and one original regresses, the count still passes.
Fix: broaden the scanner's discovery criterion so the loop becomes a genuine cross-property check. For example, scan for functions that contain read_to_string/File::open applied to any path argument, then assert they specifically use (THIS_FILE) rather than a bare relative path. Alternatively, add a complementary negative scan for bare path strings like "tests/build_logic_tests.rs" outside of comments, asserting none are used as function arguments.

(2) Add edge-case coverage to test_find_self_reading_test_fns_discovers_dynamically for a `#[test]` function with an intermediate attribute (e.g. `#[test]\n#[allow(unused)]\nfn test_with_attr() { let _ = read(THIS_FILE); }`). find_cfg_unix_test_fns's test covers this, but the new scanner's test doesn't — the code handles it via the starts_with('#') branch, so the test is straightforward to add.

Skipped: state-machine duplication between find_self_reading_test_fns and find_cfg_unix_test_fns — reviewer self-marked "no action required" unless a third scanner is ever needed.

Source: esc-1059-785.

tasks.title: Strengthen bit-identity test edge cases in value.rs
tasks.description:
In `crates/reify-types/src/value.rs`, the bit-identity test functions for Scalar, Complex, and Orientation have several coverage gaps identified during Task 913 review:

1. **Missing ordering assertion for Complex `im` neg-zero** (~line 3222): The neg-zero-in-`im` sub-case asserts `assert_ne` and antisymmetry but omits `assert!(neg_im < pos_im)`, unlike every other neg-zero sub-case (Scalar, Complex `re`, Orientation `w`). Add this one-liner.

2. **Both-component edge cases for Complex** (~line 3149-3224): NaN and neg-zero are tested in `re` and `im` independently, but not simultaneously. Add a sub-case like `re: f64::NAN, im: -0.0` vs `re: f64::NAN, im: 0.0` to verify lexicographic chaining when the first component compares Equal.

3. **Orientation neg-zero in non-`w` component** (~line 3577-3643): The `nan_z` spot-check covers NaN in a non-w component, but neg-zero is only tested in `w`. Add a spot-check with neg-zero in `z` (or `x`/`y`) where `w` is identical, to exercise the lexicographic fallthrough path.

4. **Distinct NaN bit patterns** (~line 2258-2303): All NaN tests use `f64::NAN` for both values (identical bit patterns). Add a sub-case with two distinct NaN payloads (e.g., `f64::from_bits(0x7ff8000000000001)` vs `f64::NAN`) and verify `assert_ne` for both PartialEq and Ord, strengthening the to_bits() contract.

tasks.title: portable_timeout: post-review polish (readability, docs, test robustness)
tasks.description:
Five small improvements to portable_timeout code and tests from task 1088 review (esc-1088-777):

## S1 — Fix Test 18 comment tense (test_portable_timeout.sh:343-345)
The comment says "Current code unconditionally runs set +m..." in present tense, but the patch already fixed this. Reword to past tense: "Before this fix, the code unconditionally ran set +m..." to make it a regression-guard comment.

## S2 — Replace `&&`/`||` idiom with explicit `if` (lib_portable.sh:112, 120)
`[ "$_pt_had_monitor" -eq 0 ] && set +m 2>/dev/null || true` relies on left-associative shell operator precedence. Replace with explicit `if [ ... ]; then set +m 2>/dev/null || true; fi` at both lines 112 and 120.

## S3 — Add caller guidance for SIGTERM-only termination (lib_portable.sh:42-49)
The header documents SIGTERM-only but doesn't explain how callers should detect/handle the case where the command ignores SIGTERM. Add a brief note, e.g. "Callers needing SIGKILL can wrap the command or check process liveness after portable_timeout returns."

## S4 — Scope Test 17 grep to function body (test_portable_timeout.sh:335-336)
Test 17 greps the entire file for `kill -9`/`kill -KILL`. Future functions could legitimately use SIGKILL. Scope the grep to the portable_timeout function body using `sed -n '/^portable_timeout()/,/^}/p'`.

## S5 — Add Test 18b for degraded-path monitor-mode preservation (test_portable_timeout.sh)
Test 18 only exercises the normal path (mktemp succeeds). Add a Test 18b using MKTEMP_FAIL_SETUP to verify monitor-mode preservation on the degraded path (mktemp fails, lines 116-120).

## Verification
Run `tests/infra/test_portable_timeout.sh` and `scripts/test_tree_sitter_generate.sh`.

tasks.title: Unify guard-state hash format across eval() and edit_param()

The guard-state hash format strings differ between eval() and edit_param() in crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs, causing the same logical guard state to produce different topology fingerprints depending on the code path:

- Site 1 (eval(), ~line 1797): `format!("{:?}", gv)` — hash of just the debug representation
- Site 2 (edit_param(), ~line 2092): `format!("guard:{}={:?}", g.guard_cell, val)` — includes cell ID prefix

This inconsistency could cause subtle bugs if fingerprints are ever compared across eval/edit_param boundaries. Currently the test doesn't cross-assert F1==F3 (same guard state computed via different paths).

## Fix
Unify to the more descriptive `guard:{}={:?}` format in both paths (or choose a single canonical form). Then add a test assertion that F1 == F3 to verify cross-path fingerprint consistency.

tasks.title: Harden content_hash tag uniqueness test and add tag registry
tasks.description: Improve the `content_hash_tags_are_unique_across_variants` test and tag documentation in `crates/reify-types/src/value.rs` with three enhancements from the Task 1094 review.
tasks.details:
Three related improvements to the content_hash tag system in value.rs:

1. **Tag registry comment** (S1): Add a comment block or companion constant array at the top of the `content_hash()` method that enumerates all assigned tag bytes (0=Bool, 1=Int, 2=Real, 3=String, 4=Scalar, 5=Undef, 6=Enum, 7=List, 8=Set, 9=Map, 10=Satisfaction(reserved), 11=Option, 12=Lambda, 13=Field, 14=Tensor, 15=Complex, 16=Orientation, 17=Range, 18=Point, 19=Vector, 20=Frame, 21=Transform, 22=Plane, 23=Axis, 24=BoundingBox, 25=Matrix). This makes it easy for future contributors to see what's taken and avoid accidental reuse.

2. **Cross-type Satisfaction coverage** (S2): Extend the `content_hash_tags_are_unique_across_variants` test to also include `Satisfaction::Satisfied`, `Satisfaction::Violated`, and `Satisfaction::Indeterminate` hashes in the `seen` set before iterating Value variants. Satisfaction uses tag 10 in the shared namespace; the existing test only checks Value variants.

3. **Better collision error messages** (S3): Replace the `HashSet<ContentHash>` with a `HashMap<ContentHash, &str>` so the assertion can report both colliding variant names: `"content_hash collision: Value::{name} collides with Value::{previous_name}"`. Currently only the second variant name is reported.

tasks.title: Add keyword assertions to task-1045 meta error path tests
tasks.description:
In `crates/reify-eval/tests/e2e_meta.rs`, the two task-1045 regression tests (`e2e_meta_access_missing_key` at ~line 536 and `e2e_meta_access_no_meta_block` at ~line 574) only assert `!errors.is_empty()`. This means any unrelated compile error (e.g., a type mismatch) would satisfy the assertion, masking a regression in the meta-specific error path.

Add a secondary loose keyword assertion to each test checking that at least one error message contains `"meta"`, e.g.:
```rust
assert!(
    errors.iter().any(|d| d.message.contains("meta")),
    "expected a meta-related error, got: {:?}",
    errors
);
```

This keeps the tests wording-resilient (they intentionally avoid exact message checks like the task-213 tests do) while preventing false-positive passes from unrelated errors.

tasks.title: Harden byte_offset_to_line_col: fix stale docs, add release-mode/mid-codepoint/expected-value tests
tasks.description:
Follow-up improvements from Task 1097 review (4 suggestions grouped):

1. **Fix stale doc comment (engine.rs:786-788):** The doc bullet "Offset beyond source.len()" still says "silent clamping — no panic or error", but the new `debug_assert!(offset <= source.len())` panics in debug builds. Update to: "**Offset beyond `source.len()`**: panics in debug builds (`debug_assert`); in release builds the loop exhausts all characters and returns the position after the last character (silent clamping)."

2. **Add release-mode clamping test (engine_tests.rs):** The `#[cfg(debug_assertions)]` guard on `byte_offset_to_line_col_offset_beyond_len` means release-profile runs (`cargo test --release`) have no coverage for beyond-len behavior. Add a complementary `#[cfg(not(debug_assertions))]` test asserting `byte_offset_to_line_col("ab", 100) == (1, 3)`.

3. **Add mid-codepoint byte offset test (engine_tests.rs):** In `byte_offset_to_line_col_multibyte_chars`, add an assertion for offset 1 in "αβ\nγ" (mid-codepoint, inside the 2-byte α). Expected: `(1, 2)` — the `char_indices` loop skips past non-boundary offsets, landing on the next codepoint. Add a comment explaining this behavior.

4. **Add expected absolute coordinates to empty-span test (engine_tests.rs:1217-1265):** `get_diagnostics_empty_span_has_identical_start_end` cross-validates but never checks that the actual line/col values are correct. Compute expected line/col from `bracket_source` for the 'width' offset and assert `d.line == expected_line` and `d.column == expected_col`.

tasks.title: Add descriptive labels to parameterized URI normalization test in gotoDefinition.test.ts
tasks.description: Improve test output readability by adding descriptive labels to the `it.each` parameterized URI normalization symmetry test. Currently the test description renders verbose full URIs in failure output. Use labeled tuples with a descriptive tag instead.
tasks.details:
In `gui/src/__tests__/gotoDefinition.test.ts` around line 236, change the `it.each` from:

```js
it.each([
  ['/project/src/foo.ri', 'file:///project/src/foo.ri'],
  ['file:///project/src/foo.ri', '/project/src/foo.ri'],
])(
  '%s currentUri with %s location.uri for same file dispatches cursor movement',
  async (currentUri, locationUri) => { … }
)
```

To labeled tuples:

```js
it.each([
  ['bare-path → file://', '/project/src/foo.ri', 'file:///project/src/foo.ri'],
  ['file:// → bare-path', 'file:///project/src/foo.ri', '/project/src/foo.ri'],
])(
  '%s: same file dispatches cursor movement',
  async (_label, currentUri, locationUri) => { … }
)
```

This makes failure output more scannable by showing labels like 'bare-path → file://' instead of full URIs in the test name.

tasks.title: Guard collection subs in self-resolution paths
tasks.description:
The `self.member` branch (lib.rs:2669) and `self.sub.member` branch (lib.rs:2710) check `sub_component_types` without excluding collection subs. For a collection sub like `sub items : List<Bolt>`, `self.items` returns `Type::StructureRef("Bolt")` (single-entity ref) instead of being rejected or producing a collection type, causing silently wrong evaluation results at runtime.

Fix: Add `&& !scope.collection_sub_names.contains(member.as_str())` guard at line 2669 and `&& !scope.collection_sub_names.contains(sub_name.as_str())` guard at line 2710. Optionally emit a diagnostic directing users to indexed access for collection subs.

Also add test coverage for `self.collection_sub` and `self.collection_sub.member` in self_keyword_tests.rs to assert correct behavior.

tasks.title: Add negative test pinning dimensioned-domain gradient assertion skip
tasks.description:
In crates/reify-expr/tests/gradient_tests.rs, add a test that verifies a gradient field with a dimensioned domain (e.g., domain=Scalar[LENGTH]) and a mismatched codomain (e.g., declared Scalar[MASS] but lambda returns Scalar[LENGTH]) does NOT panic, even though the codomain is wrong.

This pins the intentional design decision in lib.rs (~line 854): the debug assertion only fires for dimensionless domains (domain_dim.is_none()). With dimensioned domains, `make_arg` passes Scalar<domain_dim> which causes the lambda to naturally return a dimensioned value regardless of the declared codomain — so the assertion is skipped.

The test should:
- Create a field with domain=Scalar[LENGTH], codomain=Scalar[MASS], lambda |x| 2*x
- Compute gradient, sample at a point
- Assert it returns a value (not Undef) and does NOT panic

tasks.title: Fix silent unwrap_or(0.0) in solver uniqueness check
tasks.description:
In `crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs:763,767`, the uniqueness verification code uses `.unwrap_or(0.0)` when extracting solved parameter values. If either the original or perturbed solve returns Undef or a non-numeric value for a param, both default to 0.0 — making diff=0.0 and incorrectly reporting the solution as unique.

This violates the "errors must be noisy" convention. A solver that returns Undef/missing values for auto params is a bug that should be detected, not silently absorbed.

Fix: if either value is None (missing) or non-numeric, either (a) return false (non-unique / cannot verify), or (b) skip that param with a tracing::warn!. Do NOT default to 0.0.

memory_hints: search "solver uniqueness verification silent default", get_entity "DimensionalSolver"

tasks.title: Log warning on delta_to_events serialization failure instead of silent skip
tasks.description:
In `gui/src-tauri/src/diff.rs:148-161`, delta_to_events() silently skips any mesh/value/constraint that fails serde_json::to_value(). If serialization fails (e.g., unexpected NaN in a field), the frontend never receives the update event and its state becomes permanently stale for that item — with no indication of failure.

Fix: replace `if let Ok(val) = serde_json::to_value(mesh)` with a match that logs `tracing::warn!("failed to serialize {}: {}", item_id, err)` on the Err branch. Consider also emitting an error event so the frontend can show a diagnostic.

tasks.title: Log error when sidecar tool-result write fails instead of .ok() swallow
tasks.description:
In `gui/src-tauri/src/claude_bridge.rs:292`, the tool result write to the sidecar is swallowed with `.ok()`:
```rust
write_to_sidecar(&mut *writer, &response).await.ok();
```
If this write fails, the sidecar hangs forever waiting for a ToolResult that never arrives. The Claude session becomes stuck with no error reporting.

Fix: replace `.ok()` with error handling that (a) logs `tracing::error!("failed to send tool result to sidecar: {err}")` and (b) emits a `claude-error` event to the frontend so the user sees the failure.

tasks.title: Clean up poison-recovery test helpers and reduce duplication in concurrent_eval.rs
tasks.description:

Either remove it, or wire it into `tracing_warn_emitted_on_poison_evaluate` (line 2524) which does manual warn capture instead of using a helper. 4. **Reduce duplication in `tracing_warn_emitted_on_poison_into_result*` tests (lines 2162-2210):** The three tests (values, snapshot_values, results) are nearly identical — each creates simple_setup, from_setup, eval_set, poisons one lock, and calls `assert_poison_recovers`. tasks.title: Extract guard_state_fingerprint helper in reify-eval
tasks.description:
Eliminate 3-site duplication of guard fingerprint computation in crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs: eval() lines ~1508-1517, edit_param() Site 1 lines ~1823-1831, edit_param() Site 2 lines ~2111-2125. All three iterate guarded_groups, hash each guard value with format string 'guard:{}={:?}', call ContentHash::combine_all, then combine with topology_fingerprint(). This format-string drift across sites is exactly what caused the bug task 1112 fixed.

Extract a helper: `fn guard_state_fingerprint(groups: &[GuardedGroup], values: &HashMap<ValueCellId, Value>, strict: bool) -> ContentHash`. Sites 1 and eval() pass strict=false (unwrap_or(Value::Undef)); Site 2 passes strict=true (.expect). Preserve existing comments explaining strict-vs-lenient rationale at the helper level.

All existing guard tests must continue to pass. Source: review suggestion on task 1112, escalation esc-1112-816.

tasks.title: Improve guard fingerprint tests: multi-guard + rationale comment
tasks.description:
Two related test improvements in crates/reify-eval/tests/guard_eval.rs from review of task 1112:

1. **Multi-guarded-group test for ID disambiguation.** The Site 1 comment in crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs (around line 1822) explicitly calls out "two guards holding the same value produce different hashes" as a critical correctness property, but no existing test exercises 2+ guarded groups. Add a regression test: build a template with two guarded groups whose guard cells both evaluate to Bool(true), capture fingerprint F_tt; then edit one guard to false (F_tf) and the other to false (F_ft). Assert F_tt != F_tf, F_tt != F_ft, F_tf != F_ft — proving the fingerprint is sensitive to which specific guard cell holds which value, not just the multiset of guard values. This protects the guard_cell ID disambiguation property across future refactors.

2. **Rationale comment on cross-path test.** The test eval_edit_param_guard_fingerprint_cross_path_consistency (around line 1387) overlaps significantly with the updated edit_param_guard_fingerprint_round_trips (lines 1258-1374), which now asserts F1==F3 cross-path equality at line 1347. Add a brief doc comment explaining why both tests exist: round_trips covers F2==F4 and value assertions; this focused test is a minimal regression reproducer for the cross-path bug fixed in task 1112.

Source: review suggestions on task 1112, escalation esc-1112-816.

On heavily loaded CI, this could be flaky. Replace with a retry loop (e.g., poll `ps` up to 5 times with 200ms intervals). 3. **Add rescue_dir cleanup in Test 18b** (from review S6): Test 18b (line ~448) creates a `rescue_dir` via `mktemp -d` but never cleans it up (intentionally omits EXIT trap to avoid timer subshell inheritance). Add `rm -rf "$rescue_dir"` at the end of the `bash -c` block (after the ps/grep check) to avoid accumulating temp dirs in CI loops.

tasks.title: Add tracing::warn for unwrap_or(mid) fallback in verify_uniqueness
tasks.description:
In `crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs:790`, the `verify_uniqueness` function uses `.unwrap_or(mid)` when extracting the solved value for perturbation starting point selection. While less dangerous than the comparison bug (it only affects where perturbation starts, not agreement detection), it is inconsistent with the defensive approach added to `solutions_agree` in Task 1132.

tasks.title: Merge finite-check and serialize loops in serialize_finite_f32_vec

tasks.title: Bundle: pragma validation consolidation + content_hash + test coverage
tasks.description: Consolidates pragma validation consolidation with context-aware diagnostics, module-level pragma inclusion in CompiledModule content_hash, and missing pragma test coverage (occurrences and #no_prelude storage).
tasks.details:
**BUNDLED TASK** (bundle consolidation 2026-04-11). This task consolidates task 1163 plus 2 sibling polish task(s) that share a module footprint. Original per-task content preserved verbatim below; implement all sections in one PR.


### Original task 1163: Consolidate pragma validation and add context-aware diagnostics


Two related improvements to pragma validation in the compiler:

1. **Use `_context` parameter in `validate_pragmas`** (lib.rs:4732-4746): The `_context` parameter is accepted but never read. Unlike `validate_annotations` which uses context for context-specific warnings, `validate_pragmas` only does name-lookup. Use the context to provide better diagnostics (e.g., "#no_prelude is only valid at module level" when found on a block).

2. **Consolidate known pragma name lists** (lib.rs:3905 and 4737): `KNOWN_MODULE_PRAGMAS` (5 entries) and `KNOWN_BLOCK_PRAGMAS` (3 entries, a subset) are defined independently with no compiler-enforced link. Extract to a shared location or add cross-references.

Memory hints: search for "pragma validation" and "known pragma names" in reify-compiler.

### Absorbed from task 1164: Include module-level pragmas in CompiledModule content hash
The content hash computation for CompiledModule (lib.rs:4530-4552) does not include module-level pragmas. If pragmas like `#precision(value=64)` are semantically meaningful for downstream compilation (solver config, numeric precision), a change to pragmas alone won't invalidate cached content hashes, risking stale results in incremental compilation.

Either:
- Hash the pragmas vector into `all_hashes` if they affect semantics, OR
- Document that pragmas are intentionally excluded from the content hash if they are purely advisory/metadata.

Memory hints: search for "content hash" and "CompiledModule" in reify-compiler. Check task 12 for content-hash caching context.

### Absorbed from task 1165: Add missing pragma test coverage: occurrences and #no_prelude storage
Two test coverage gaps in pragma_compile_tests.rs:

1. **Occurrence-level pragmas untested**: Tests cover pragma propagation for structures, traits, and purposes, but not occurrences. The `From<&'a OccurrenceDef>` → `EntityDefRef` conversion path that carries pragmas is untested. Add a test like `occurrence_pragma_propagated_to_topology_template`.

Add an assertion like `module.pragmas.iter().any(|p| p.name == "no_prelude")` to `no_prelude_simple_structure_compiles_clean` or a new test.

Both are low-risk (shared code paths / behavioral coverage exists) but worth closing.

tasks.title: Subscribe to serialization-error events on frontend
tasks.description:
The backend (gui/src-tauri/src/diff.rs lines 154, 170, 189) emits `serialization-error` Tauri events when mesh/value/constraint serialization fails in `delta_to_events()`. However, the frontend has no listener for this event — the error is silently dropped.

**What's wrong:** When a mesh, value, or constraint delta fails JSON serialization (e.g., NaN in a float32 mesh, or a Value variant the serializer can't handle), the backend correctly detects the error, logs a tracing::warn, and emits a `serialization-error` event. But `gui/src/bridge.ts` does not export an `onSerializationError()` listener, and neither `gui/src/stores/engineStore.ts` nor `gui/src/App.tsx` subscribe to the event. The user sees no indication that data was lost.

**Suggested fix:**
1. Add `onSerializationError()` listener export to `gui/src/bridge.ts` (matching the pattern of `onMeshUpdate`, `onValueUpdate`, etc.)
2. Subscribe in `gui/src/App.tsx` initApp() (after the evaluation-status subscription)
3. Show a toast notification with the error details: "Failed to serialize {item_type} '{item_id}': {error}"
4. Add test coverage in `gui/src/__tests__/` verifying the event handler processes payloads correctly

Memory hints: search "serialization-error event" "diff.rs delta_to_events"

tasks.title: Replace geometry eval_arg unwrap_or(Value::Undef) with diagnostic
tasks.description:
In crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs, the `eval_arg` closures at lines 3354-3364 and 3410-3420 use `unwrap_or(reify_types::Value::Undef)` when looking up named geometry operation arguments. This violates the project convention: "errors must be noisy — never silently swallow logic errors with unwrap_or(Value::Undef)".

**What's wrong:** When `evaluate_geometry_op()` looks up a required argument (like "width" for a Box primitive), a missing argument silently returns `Value::Undef`. This masks potential compiler bugs — if the compiler fails to emit a required argument, the geometry operation receives Undef and produces invalid geometry without any diagnostic.

**Context:** The argument names are statically known per geometry op kind (e.g., Box needs width/height/depth). A missing named argument indicates a compiler bug (the compiler should always emit all required args), NOT a user error. Returning Undef here is incorrect.

**Suggested fix:** Replace `unwrap_or(Value::Undef)` with a diagnostic emission or `tracing::warn!` + `Value::Undef` at minimum. Better: emit a diagnostic saying "missing required geometry argument '{name}' for {kind}" and return Undef. This follows the pattern established by dd5e10dbe (which replaced guard-site unwrap_or with expect) and a300a6f6c (which replaced solutions_agree unwrap_or(0.0)).

**Two sites to fix:**
- Line ~3363: eval_arg in Primitive handling
- Line ~3419: eval_arg in Modify handling

Memory hints: search "silent defaults convention" "eval_arg geometry" entity "evaluate_geometry_op"

In tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh (lines 301-312), the behavioral test for `assert_sync_ref_exists` sources the entire `sync_comments_test.sh` file to get access to the helper function. This executes all top-level code (real assertions, `set -euo pipefail`, variable assignments) as a side effect, which is fragile.

Two improvements:
1. **Isolate the function extraction**: Instead of `source '${SYNC_FILE}'`, extract just the `assert_sync_ref_exists` function definition (e.g., via `sed -n '/^assert_sync_ref_exists/,/^}/p'`) or move the function to a shared helper that can be sourced independently. This prevents future top-level code additions from breaking the test.
2. **Use trap-based cleanup**: Replace the explicit `rm -f` at the end with `trap 'rm -f "$tmp_src" "$tmp_tgt"' EXIT` immediately after the mktemp calls, so temp files are cleaned up even on early exit (e.g., if `set -euo pipefail` from the sourced file triggers unexpectedly).

tasks.title: Rate-limit serialization error toasts to prevent UI flooding
tasks.description:
The serialization error handler in App.tsx calls showToast for every error event with no deduplication or rate-limiting. If a model triggers many non-finite values in a single evaluation cycle, the backend emits one serialization-error per failed mesh/value/constraint, potentially flooding the UI with dozens of identical or near-identical error toasts.

Suggested fix: Debounce or coalesce serialization error toasts — accumulate errors over a short window (e.g. 500ms) and show a single summary toast like "N items failed to serialize", or deduplicate by (item_type, item_id) so repeated errors for the same item don't stack.

Memory hints: search "serialization-error event" "showToast toast flooding"

tasks.title: Add max-wait cap to serialization error coalescer debounce
tasks.description:
The trailing-edge debounce in `createSerializationErrorCoalescer` resets the 500ms window on every new error. If errors arrive continuously at <500ms intervals (e.g. a tight loop emitting serialization failures), the toast is deferred indefinitely and the `buffer` Map grows without bound.

Add a max-wait ceiling (e.g. 3–5 seconds) that forces a flush regardless of whether new errors keep arriving. Track the timestamp of the first buffered error and force-flush when `Date.now() - firstArrival > maxWaitMs`.

2. **add-after-cleanup test:** No test covers calling `add()` after `cleanup()` has been called. The current implementation handles this gracefully (cleanup clears state, a subsequent add starts a fresh timer), but this edge case should be asserted since `App.tsx`'s `onCleanup` could race with a late-arriving event. tasks.title: Replace silent if-let fallthrough with unreachable!() and fix doc contract in eval_perturbed_point
tasks.description:
In eval_perturbed_point (crates/reify-expr/src/lib.rs ~line 731), the work_point recovery uses `if let Some(Value::Point(inner)) = work_args.pop()` which silently falls through on mismatch, leaving work_point empty. The debug_assert_eq! on line 735 only fires in debug builds; in release a failed match causes an opaque index-out-of-bounds panic on the next iteration.

Two changes:
1. Replace the `if let` (lines 731-734) with a `match` that has an `unreachable!()` arm:
```rust
match work_args.pop() {
    Some(Value::Point(inner)) => *work_point = inner,
    other => unreachable!("expected Value::Point after apply_lambda, got {other:?}"),
}
```
This makes the infallibility contract explicit at runtime in both debug and release builds.

2. Fix the doc comment for `work_point` (line 694) which says "Must be empty and unused in the decomposed path" but this contract is unenforced. Either add `debug_assert!(work_point.is_empty() || single_point_param)` at the function top, or soften the doc to "ignored in the decomposed path".

tasks.title: Add trap-based temp dir cleanup and remove dead variable in tests/infra/test_run_all.sh
tasks.description:
Follow-up from Task 1068 review (esc-1068-904, suggestions S1 and S2). Both suggestions target tests/infra/test_run_all.sh.

S1 (resource_cleanup, lines 41-55): Temp dirs (TMPDIR_T2, TMPDIR_T3, TMPDIR_PASS, TMPDIR_FAIL, TMPDIR_EMPTY) are cleaned up inline via `rm -rf`, but there is no trap-based cleanup. Under `set -euo pipefail`, an unexpected early exit leaks them.

Fix: Add a trap at the top of the script that tracks and removes all temp dirs on EXIT. Pattern:

    _TMPDIRS=()
    cleanup() { for d in "${_TMPDIRS[@]+${_TMPDIRS[@]}}"; do rm -rf "$d"; done; }
    trap cleanup EXIT

Then replace each `rm -rf "$TMPDIR_*"` with `_TMPDIRS+=("$TMPDIR_*")` (or keep the inline rm and just register the dir for safety-net cleanup).

S2 (dead_code, line 43): `t2_rc` is captured via `&& t2_rc=0 || t2_rc=$?` but never referenced — Test 2 only checks output text. Either drop the rc capture (`t2_output="$(...)" || true`) or add an assertion that t2_rc is 0 (the empty/excluded suite should succeed).

Verify by running `bash tests/infra/test_run_all.sh` and `bash tests/infra/run_all.sh` after the change.

tasks.title: Extend missing-geometry-arg diagnostic to Transform/Pattern/Sweep branches via shared helper
tasks.description:
In crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs `compile_geometry_op` (lines ~3346-3700), the new "missing required geometry argument" diagnostic only fires for Primitive and Modify branches (Box/Cylinder/Sphere/Fillet/Chamfer/Shell/Draft/Thicken). The Transform, Pattern, and Sweep branches still silently `?`-short-circuit on a missing arg via `eval_arg_f64` closures (lines ~3490, 3548-3563, 3659), so users only get the generic `failed to compile geometry operation` error for Translate/Rotate/Scale/RotateAround/LinearPattern/CircularPattern/Mirror/Loft/Extrude/Revolve. Same root cause, inconsistent UX.

**Fix:** Extract a single shared helper, e.g.

```rust
fn eval_named_arg(
    name: &str,
    kind_label: &str,
    args: &[(String, CompiledExpr)],
    values: &ValueMap,
    functions: &[CompiledFunction],
    meta_map: &HashMap<String, HashMap<String, String>>,
    diagnostics: &mut Vec<Diagnostic>,
) -> Option<reify_types::Value>
```

(or a similar `_f64` variant) that performs the lookup, emits the warning on miss, and returns `None`/`Value::Undef` consistently. Use it in all five match arms (Primitive, Modify, Transform, Pattern, Sweep) so the warning text and behavior are uniform across op kinds. This also dedupes the two near-identical closure bodies created in Task 1167.

**Source review suggestions:** S1 (design_inconsistency) + S7 (code_duplication) from Task 1167 review.

tasks.title: Avoid duplicate diagnostic when geometry op missing required arg
tasks.description:
In crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs `compile_geometry_op`, the Primitive and Modify branches (lines ~3357-3388, 3413-3484) emit a `Severity::Warning` for a missing required arg, then still build a `GeometryOp { ..., field: Value::Undef }` and return `Some(...)`. The op then flows to `kernel.execute(...)`, which (almost certainly) fails with `Err`, and the caller pushes a second diagnostic (`geometry error: ...`). The user gets a Warning + a downstream Error for the same root cause with no obvious linkage between them. The other branches (Transform/Pattern/Sweep) sidestep this by short-circuiting via `?` so only one error appears.

**Two viable fixes — pick one, document rationale in commit:**

1. Make the missing-arg helper `Option`-returning so the closure call short-circuits the whole match arm (`compile_geometry_op` returns `None`). Aligns with the existing Transform/Pattern/Sweep convention. The caller will then emit the single generic `failed to compile geometry operation` error — consider whether the warning text alone is enough context.

2. Raise the warning's severity to `Severity::Error` and have the caller suppress the now-redundant generic error message when this specific diagnostic has already been emitted for the op.

Option (1) is consistent with existing convention; option (2) gives the user a more specific error message but requires de-duplication logic in the caller.

**Source review suggestion:** S2 (robustness) from Task 1167 review.

**Cross-task note:** This change should be coordinated with the shared-helper extraction task (S1+S7 follow-up). Doing them together is cleaner — the helper API determines whether short-circuit semantics are baked in.

tasks.description: Consolidates compile_geometry_op diagnostic quality (Display not Debug for op kind, source span/label on missing-arg), degenerate-guard constants (DEGENERATE_LENGTH_M/ANGLE_RAD/GEOMETRY_EPSILON with scientific notation), ExtrudeSymmetric per-side threshold semantics clarification, missing-arg tests (Circular/Mirror pattern coverage), extrude/revolve degenerate-guard boundary tests, stress_sweep_degenerate tidy, negative-near-zero symmetry pinning, eval_named_arg_f64 coverage broadening, build_modify_missing_arg helper extraction, suppress redundant generic-constraint diagnostic on missing required geometry args, fill-form eval failure + NaN propagation guards, compile_geometry_op resilience (missing-arg/unresolved-ref early-return), and boolean-op compile coverage.
tasks.details:
**BUNDLED TASK** (bundle consolidation 2026-04-11, extended 2026-04-20). This task consolidates task 1188 plus 15 sibling polish task(s) that share a module footprint. Original per-task content preserved verbatim below; implement all sections in one PR.


### Original task 1188: Use Display (not Debug) formatting for geometry op kind in diagnostics


In crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs (lines ~3365-3368, 3425-3428), the new "missing required geometry argument" diagnostic uses `{:?}` (Debug) on `kind`, producing user-visible strings like `for Box` or `for Fillet` that depend on the Rust enum's `Debug` derive. This couples user-visible diagnostics to a `Debug` impl that can change without notice and doesn't degrade gracefully for compound variants. The codebase otherwise prefers Display formatting for user-facing messages (e.g. `Diagnostic::error(format!("geometry error: {}", e))`).

**Fix:** Either:
- Add a small helper `fn primitive_kind_name(kind: &PrimitiveKind) -> &'static str` and `fn modify_kind_name(kind: &ModifyKind) -> &'static str` returning lowercase op names (`"box"`, `"cylinder"`, `"sphere"`, `"fillet"`, `"chamfer"`, `"shell"`, `"draft"`, `"thicken"`), and format the message as `format!("missing required argument '{}' for {}", name, primitive_kind_name(kind))`.
- Or implement `Display` on `PrimitiveKind` and `ModifyKind` in the `reify_compiler` crate, then use `{kind}` in the format string.

Update the existing `compile_geometry_op_*_missing_arg_emits_diagnostic` tests to assert the Display format.

**Files:** crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs (compile_geometry_op), crates/reify-compiler/src/geometry.rs (optional Display impl), crates/reify-eval/tests/geometry_error_handling.rs (test updates)

**Note:** The test changes here may overlap with the assert_compile_rejection helper extraction in sibling task 1213 follow-ups (task 1627/1628) — coordinate if both land in the same cycle.

```rust
const DEGENERATE_LENGTH_M: f64 = 1e-12;  // 1 picometer
const DEGENERATE_ANGLE_RAD: f64 = 1.745_329e-3;  // ~0.1 degrees
const GEOMETRY_EPSILON: f64 = 1e-12;
```
and replace all numeric literals with these names. Use underscores for readability (1.745_329e-3) per Rust convention.

### Absorbed from task 1198: Harden compile_geometry_op resilience: missing-arg and unresolved-ref early-return
In crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs (compile_geometry_op), the function currently emits diagnostics for missing arguments and unresolved geometry references but sometimes continues evaluation, leading to downstream panic or confusing secondary errors. After emitting "missing required argument" or "unresolved geometry reference", immediately `return None;` to halt further processing. Add a test that tries to compile a geometry op with both missing arg and unresolved ref and asserts only the first diagnostic appears (fail-fast).

In crates/reify-eval/tests/geometry_error_handling.rs, the boolean op path (GeometryOp::Boolean) has no missing-arg coverage. Add tests compile_geometry_op_union_missing_tool, compile_geometry_op_intersect_missing_target, compile_geometry_op_subtract_missing_tool that construct BooleanOp with target or tool as None/invalid GeomRef, call compile_geometry_op, and assert no kernel operations + diagnostic with "missing" or "unresolved". ### NEW from candidate (spawned_from task 322): Clarify ExtrudeSymmetric degenerate-threshold semantics (per-side vs total)

The ExtrudeSymmetric degeneracy check in crates/reify-eval/src/geometry_ops.rs:631-640 rejects `|distance| < 1e-12 m`, matching the Extrude arm. But the symmetric variant extrudes distance/2 each way, so the per-side extrusion is 0.5e-12 m when distance is at the threshold. If downstream OCCT (or the mock kernel) rejects per-side lengths below 1e-12, a boundary distance that passes this check would still fail at the kernel.

**Fix:**
1. Determine the correct threshold semantics: should ExtrudeSymmetric reject `|distance| < 1e-12` (current, total-distance check) or `|distance/2| < 1e-12` (per-side check, i.e. `|distance| < 2e-12`)?
2. If per-side is correct, update the threshold constant to `2.0 * DEGENERATE_LENGTH_M` in the ExtrudeSymmetric arm.
3. Add a boundary test in crates/reify-eval/tests/geometry_error_handling.rs: `build_extrude_symmetric_per_side_threshold` that tests distance=1.5e-12 (per-side 0.75e-12, below threshold if per-side semantics apply) and asserts diagnostic or success depending on chosen semantics.
4. Document the chosen semantics in a comment at the threshold check.

**Files:** crates/reify-eval/src/geometry_ops.rs:631-640, crates/reify-eval/tests/geometry_error_handling.rs

tasks.title: Harden test_build_rs_reads_use_manifest_dir meta-test detection
tasks.description:
Strengthen the meta-test in tree-sitter-reify/tests/build_logic_tests.rs that guards against bare-relative `read_to_string("build.rs")` calls. Three small improvements were suggested in review of Task 1040:

1. **Inline comments not stripped (line 1281-1284)**: The current filter only skips lines whose trimmed text starts with `//`. A trailing inline comment like `let x = 0; // example: read_to_string("build.rs")` would be misflagged. Fix: strip everything after `//` before doing `contains`, e.g.:
   ```rust
   let code = line.split("//").next().unwrap_or(line);
   code.contains(bare_pattern)
   ```
   Also consider `/* ... */` block comments.

2. **Detection too narrow (line 1276)**: Only matches the literal `read_to_string("build.rs")`. Variants such as `read_to_string( "build.rs" )`, `read_to_string("./build.rs")`, or `File::open("build.rs")` would silently bypass the guard. Fix: use a regex (the `regex` dev-dep is already used elsewhere in the crate) like `read_to_string\s*\(\s*"\.?\/?build\.rs"\s*\)` or scan for any `"build.rs"` literal outside the BUILD_RS doc string.

3. **Test name awkward (line 1259)**: Rename `test_build_rs_reads_use_manifest_dir` → `test_no_bare_relative_build_rs_reads` (or `test_build_rs_reads_use_const_path`) to match the descriptive style of nearby tests (`test_no_redundant_rerun_if_changed`, `test_out_dir_no_silent_fallback`). Update the doc comment accordingly.

All three are quality-of-life hardening. None are urgent — the meta-test currently catches the actual regression it was created for. Source: review_suggestions on Task 1040 by reviewer_comprehensive.

tasks.title: Expand test coverage for compile_geometry_op missing-arg diagnostics + add e2e plumbing test
tasks.description:
The unit tests added in Task 1167 (crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs lines ~4863-5054) cover only `Primitive::Box` (1 of 3 primitive variants) and `Modify::Fillet` (1 of 5 modify variants). The shared `eval_arg` closures are exercised but the per-kind formatting and other variant code paths are not validated. There is also no end-to-end test confirming that the warning actually reaches `BuildResult.diagnostics` / `TessellateResult.diagnostics` via `Engine::build()`, `Engine::build_snapshot()`, or `Engine::tessellate_realizations()` (call sites at lib.rs lines ~2899-2906, 2979-2986, 3099-3107). Future refactors that break the `&mut diagnostics` plumbing would not be caught by the new unit tests.

**Add the following tests:**

1. `compile_geometry_op_primitive_cylinder_missing_radius_emits_diagnostic` — `Primitive::Cylinder` missing `radius`.
2. `compile_geometry_op_primitive_sphere_missing_radius_emits_diagnostic` — `Primitive::Sphere` missing `radius`.
3. `compile_geometry_op_modify_chamfer_missing_distance_emits_diagnostic` — `Modify::Chamfer` missing `distance`.
4. `compile_geometry_op_modify_shell_missing_thickness_emits_diagnostic` — `Modify::Shell` missing `thickness` while a `face_0` arg is present (confirms only the required-arg path warns, not the optional `face_*` collection).
5. `compile_geometry_op_modify_draft_missing_angle_emits_diagnostic` — `Modify::Draft` missing `angle`.
6. `compile_geometry_op_modify_thicken_missing_offset_emits_diagnostic` — `Modify::Thicken` missing `offset`.
7. `compile_geometry_op_box_two_missing_args_emits_two_diagnostics_in_order` — `Primitive::Box` missing both `width` and `height`; assert `diagnostics.len() == 2` and verify message order matches caller iteration order (`width` before `height`).
8. **End-to-end plumbing test** in the same module: construct a `CompiledModule` containing a `Primitive::Box` op with an intentionally missing required arg, run `engine.build(...)`, and assert the resulting `BuildResult.diagnostics` contains a `Severity::Warning` mentioning the missing arg name. Doing this for one of the three call sites is sufficient to lock the wiring in place.

Tests (1)–(7) can reuse the helpers in the existing module; (8) needs an `Engine` fixture — look at sibling tests for the right setup.

**Source review suggestions:** S5 (test_coverage) + S6 (test_coverage) from Task 1167 review.

tasks.title: Pin EngineSession invariant: doc + positive resolve_source test (Task 899 review)
tasks.description:
Two related follow-ups from the Task 899 review covering the new `resolve_source` precondition contract.

**Background:** Task 899 changed `resolve_source` from `Option<(String, &str)>` (graceful) to `(&str, &str)` with `debug_assert!(self.compiled.is_some())` and inner `expect()`s. `get_diagnostics` and `get_source_location` previously returned empty/None on failure; they now panic in release if the invariant breaks (compiled set without matching `module_name` / `source_map` entry). The invariant is currently airtight inside `load_from_source`/`update_source` (atomic mutation), but worth pinning explicitly.

**Item A — Document the invariant (gui/src-tauri/src/engine.rs ~lines 22-29 / 300-345):**
Add a doc comment on `EngineSession` (or its fields) noting that `compiled`, `module_name`, and the matching `source_map` entry **must always be mutated together** — any future code path that touches one without the others is a crash hazard for `get_diagnostics`/`get_source_location`. Current safe sites: `load_from_source`, `update_source`. If a future caller acquires partial state through serialization, snapshotting, or test injection, the invariant must be re-established before the next `get_*` call.

**Item B — Positive test for resolve_source (gui/src-tauri/src/tests/engine_tests.rs ~line 1648):**
Add a `#[cfg(test)]` test that:
1. Constructs `EngineSession::new(Box::new(SimpleConstraintChecker), None)`,
2. Calls `load_from_source(bracket_source(), "bracket")`,
3. Asserts `session.resolve_source_for_test() == ("bracket.ri", bracket_source())`.

This locks in the borrowing contract (no allocation on the return path) and the key derivation jointly. Currently exercised only indirectly via `get_source_location`/`get_diagnostics`.

**Optional Item C — Invariant regression test:** A `#[cfg(test)]` test that mutates only one of the three fields (e.g. via a test-only setter, or by `mem::take`-ing `module_name` after a load) and confirms `get_diagnostics()` panics — pinning that the panic is intentional and expected.

tasks.title: Harden extract_fn helper in sync_comments_test.sh (non-empty assert, anchored regex, comment rewrite)
tasks.description:
Three related hardening fixes to the extract_fn helper added in task 864 at tests/sync_comments_test.sh:46-59.

S3 (false_negative_in_test) — lines 46-59: extract_fn returns empty if 'fn sanitize_value' is not found in a file. If a future rename removes the signature from both files simultaneously, diff <(empty) <(empty) returns 0 and the assertion silently PASSes — a false-negative that defeats the body-diff guard. The existing assert_sync_ref_exists check uses a different (grep-based) pattern, so the two checks are not coupled. Fix: capture extract_fn output to a variable, fail the test if either is empty (or assert that the first line starts with 'fn sanitize_value'), then diff.

S4 (regex_robustness) — lines 49-52: The awk pattern '/^fn '"$fn_name"'/' is unanchored at the end. Since extract_fn takes fn_name as a parameter (signaling intent for reuse), a future call with a name that is a prefix of another function (e.g. fn_name='sanitize' matching both 'sanitize' and 'sanitize_value') would extract from whichever appears first. For the current sanitize_value call this is harmless, but the helper is more brittle than its docstring implies. Anchor the trailing context, e.g. /^fn '"$fn_name"'[(<]/ — requiring the next character to be '(' (non-generic fn) or '<' (generic fn). This matches the conventions already encoded in tests/infra/test_sync_comments_grep.sh.

S5 (misleading_comment) — lines 46-48: The helper comment says 'Excludes doc comments and SYNC markers, which may legitimately differ between the two copies.' extract_fn doesn't actively exclude them — they're simply above the '^fn' anchor and so naturally not captured. Reword as: 'Extracts from the fn signature line to the next line that begins with } at column 0. Doc comments and SYNC markers above the signature are naturally excluded; legitimate per-copy differences in those sections do not trigger a body diff.'

After making the helper more robust, consider whether tests/infra/test_sync_comments_grep.sh should grow a meta-test section that exercises extract_fn fixtures (analogous to its existing accept/reject section for the SYNC pattern).

Source: review of task 864 (esc-864-918).

tasks.title: Document throttled-tab sync-flush invariant in serialization error coalescer (JSDoc + inline + test guard)
tasks.description:
Three small documentation/robustness follow-ups from the Task 1183 review of `createSerializationErrorCoalescer`. All reinforce why the synchronous-flush branch exists and why `Date.now()` must be captured once — i.e. the throttled-timer-in-backgrounded-tab production case.

1. **JSDoc — add throttled-tab scenario** (`gui/src/hooks/useSerializationErrorCoalescer.ts:20-22`). The current block mentions "continuous arrivals keep resetting the debounce indefinitely" but not the actual production reason for the sync-flush branch: throttled `setTimeout` delivery in backgrounded browser tabs. Append a sentence such as: "When timers are throttled (e.g. a backgrounded tab) and the elapsed wall-clock time on a new arrival has already exceeded `maxWaitMs`, `add()` flushes synchronously rather than waiting for the throttled timer."

2. **Inline comment — explain the single `Date.now()` read** (`gui/src/hooks/useSerializationErrorCoalescer.ts:52-55`). Add a one-line comment above the `const now = Date.now(); firstArrival ??= now;` block such as: "// Read Date.now() once: keeps firstArrival and elapsed consistent under throttled timers (backgrounded tabs)." This prevents a future "simplification" back to two `Date.now()` calls (which would yield `elapsed = 0` on first arrival even after a clock jump and silently break the throttled-tab case).

3. **Test invariant guard** (`gui/src/__tests__/useSerializationErrorCoalescer.test.ts:311-350`). The sync-flush test relies on `vi.setSystemTime()` advancing `Date.now()` without firing pending fake timers — documented vitest behavior, but a subtle invariant a future vitest upgrade could change. Before the second `coalescer.add()` (the one that triggers the sync flush), assert `expect(vi.getTimerCount()).toBe(1)` so that if vitest ever changes `setSystemTime` to fire pending timers, the test fails loudly rather than silently going green via the timer path.

Source task: 1183. Source review: reviewer_comprehensive (3 suggestions). All three changes touch the same hook + its test; expect a single small commit.

tasks.title: Polish conjugate sanitization tests and comment in reify-expr
tasks.description:
Follow-up to task 903 (NaN/Inf guard for conjugate in reify-expr). Review surfaced four small polish items in `crates/reify-expr/src/lib.rs`, all in the conjugate method (~line 1444) and its tests (~lines 4459-4609). Apply all four:

1. **Duplicate test (lines 4574-4608, `conjugate_finite_dimensioned_correct`)** — The test asserts `Complex{re:3.0, im:4.0, LENGTH}.conjugate == Complex{re:3.0, im:-4.0, LENGTH}`, which is byte-identical to the existing `tests/complex_eval_tests.rs::method_conjugate` (lines 418-426). The justification comment claims it adds coverage "beyond the dimensionless path" but the existing test ALSO uses LENGTH dimension — the comment is factually wrong. Either delete this test as redundant, or repurpose it with genuinely new coverage (dimensionless Complex, pure-imaginary `re=0.0`, or negative `re`) and update the comment to match.

2. **+Inf im coverage gap (lines 4459-4570)** — The five Inf/NaN tests cover {NaN re, NaN im, +Inf re, -Inf re, -Inf im} but not +Inf im. Since conjugate flips the sign of im, +Inf→-Inf is the most natural input to demonstrate the guard. Add `conjugate_pos_inf_im_returns_undef` using `Complex{re:1.0, im:+Inf, DIMENSIONLESS}`.

3. **Comment overstates benefit (lines 1450-1456)** — The current comment says the pre-guard is "more robust than sanitize_value". For conjugate (unlike phase, where atan2 transforms Inf→finite), sanitize_value's Complex arm fully detects the poisoning, so the two approaches are equivalent. Update the comment to acknowledge this and explain the pre-guard is chosen for stylistic mirroring with phase / forward-compat with potential future removal of the Complex arm in sanitize_value (see task 860 history). Do NOT switch to sanitize_value wrapping — the pre-guard remains preferable for forward-compatibility.

4. **Hardcoded line number (line 1451)** — Drop the `(line ~1423)` parenthetical from the comment; reference the phase method by name only ("mirroring the phase-method pattern above"). Line numbers drift.

After the changes run `cargo test -p reify-expr --lib conjugate` and `cargo test -p reify-expr --test complex_eval_tests method_conjugate` to verify.

tasks.title: Doc cleanup for warn counter helpers in tracing_support.rs (Task 1182 follow-ups)
tasks.description:
Three small documentation/comment fixes in `crates/reify-test-support/src/tracing_support.rs`, all surfaced by review of Task 1182. Pure docs/comment changes — no behavior change, no new tests required beyond running existing ones.

S1 — Stale docstring on `assert_warn_count_delta_panics_when_counter_went_backwards` (lines 1247-1250):
The docstring still describes the OLD `saturating_sub` behavior ("this test will not panic … the test fails — confirming the gap that step-2 closes"). The same diff replaced `saturating_sub` with the panicking subtraction, so the prose now contradicts the actual behavior — the test does panic and pass. Leftover scaffolding from a TDD step-1/step-2 split that was collapsed before merge. Rewrite to describe only the post-fix invariant, e.g.:
"Verifies `assert_warn_count_delta` panics with the `warn counter went backwards` message when `before` exceeds the current counter, which catches stale-snapshot bugs that the old `saturating_sub` implementation silently swallowed."

S2 — Non-obvious `_subscriber` binding in the same test (line 1259):
`let (_subscriber, counter) = warn_counting_subscriber();` constructs a subscriber but never installs it via `with_default`. Casual readers may worry the test forgot the install. Add a one-line inline comment on the binding itself stating that the subscriber is intentionally unused — we only need the zero-valued counter handle. (A `fresh_warn_counter()` test helper is an alternative, but a comment is the smaller change.)

S3 — Document `before` parameter contract on `assert_warn_count_delta` (lines 30-47, doc lines 19-20):
Tightening from `saturating_sub` to a hard panic on `before > after` is a stricter contract change. Today all call sites use `before=0`, but a future caller passing a `before` snapshot from another thread without an `Acquire` fence could legitimately observe `after < before` due to load reordering. Extend the `before` parameter doc with a sentence such as:
"Must be a snapshot of `counter` taken earlier in happens-before order (e.g., via `counter.load(Ordering::Acquire)` on the current thread). Passing a value greater than the counter's current load — including a stale snapshot from a reordered or unrelated read — will panic."

tasks.title: Tighten shutdown bridge tests: stable anchors, post-handshake matrix, isolated instances, ref params
tasks.description:
Four small follow-up improvements to `crates/reify-lsp/tests/in_process_bridge.rs`, all flagged in the Task 1196 review. They are tightly localized to the helper extracted in 1196 (`assert_shutdown_returns_null`, lines 28-46) and the `shutdown_ignores_unexpected_params` test (lines 652-671), so they are bundled into one task.

1. **Documentation brittleness (line 657).** The doc comment for `shutdown_ignores_unexpected_params` references `bridge.rs lines 177-183` by absolute line number — these will rot the moment a new arm is inserted above shutdown. Replace with a stable anchor such as `the "shutdown" arm in bridge::InProcessLsp::handle_request`, or quote the relevant snippet inline. Sweep the rest of the file for any other absolute line-number references and apply the same fix.

2. **Test coverage gap (line 665).** `shutdown_ignores_unexpected_params` only exercises the pre-handshake path (`InProcessLsp::new()`). The doc justification ("the shutdown arm does not consult initialization state") is exactly the invariant the test should be guarding — relying on it means a regression that conditionally validates params only after `initialize` would slip through. Add a `shutdown_ignores_unexpected_params_after_initialize` mirror that exercises both payloads against `initialized_lsp().await`. (Alternative: parameterise the helper to run both states; the explicit named tests match the existing four-shutdown-test pattern in this file better.)

3. **Test isolation (line 665).** `shutdown_ignores_unexpected_params` calls `assert_shutdown_returns_null` twice on the same `InProcessLsp` instance. This works today only because `LspServer::shutdown` is a no-op `Ok(())` (server.rs:354). If shutdown ever becomes stateful (e.g. flips an `is_shutdown` flag and rejects subsequent calls, which the LSP spec actually permits), the second assertion will fail for a reason unrelated to params handling, masking the real signal. Construct a fresh `InProcessLsp::new()` for each payload (or split into two `#[tokio::test]` functions). Apply the same isolation to the new post-handshake variant from item 2.

4. **API ergonomics (line 36).** `assert_shutdown_returns_null` takes `params: serde_json::Value` by value and then clones it inside (`params.clone()`) so the original can be reused in the panic/assertion messages. Change the signature to `async fn assert_shutdown_returns_null(lsp: &InProcessLsp, params: &serde_json::Value)` and clone once when forwarding to `handle_request`. This makes the helper's intent (it does not consume params) clearer and matches the by-reference style of the sibling `assert_malformed_params_returns_error` helper directly above it. Update all call sites in the four shutdown tests + the new post-handshake variant to pass `&json!(...)`.

Verify with `cargo test -p reify-lsp --test in_process_bridge`.

tasks.title: Soften assert_warn_count_delta `before` param docstring — drop happens-before jargon
tasks.description:
In `crates/reify-test-support/src/tracing_support.rs` lines 19–24, the `before` parameter docstring uses "happens-before order on the current thread" language. The function only checks `before > after` at runtime — it doesn't verify happens-before ordering. This overpromises a contract the code doesn't enforce and may confuse readers unfamiliar with memory-model vocabulary.

Reword to something like: "Must be a snapshot taken from the same counter before the code under test ran; passing a value greater than the counter's current load (for example, a stale snapshot from an unrelated counter or a reordered read) will panic."

Also consider dropping the redundant trailing inline comment on line 1259 (`// subscriber intentionally unused …`) since the block comment on lines 1257–1258 already explains the same thing and the `_` prefix is the idiomatic Rust signal.

tasks.description:
Four validation checks in `compile_geometry_op` (crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs) return `None` without pushing a specific diagnostic. The caller only pushes a generic "failed to compile geometry operation" error, making it impossible for users to know WHY the operation failed.

1. **Line ~3539** — Transform::Scale negative factor: `if factor < 0.0 { return None; }` → push "negative scale factor not allowed — OCCT SetScale produces inside-out geometry, not mirroring"
2. **Line ~3647** — Sweep::Extrude: `_distance_f64 = distance.as_f64().filter(|v| v.is_finite() && *v != 0.0)?` → push diagnostic for non-finite or zero extrude distance
3. **Line ~3663** — Sweep::Revolve degenerate axis: `if !mag.is_finite() || mag < 1e-12 { return None; }` → push "revolve axis is degenerate (zero-length or non-finite)"
4. **Line ~3668** — Sweep::Revolve zero angle: `if angle_rad == 0.0 { return None; }` → push "revolve angle is zero — operation would produce no geometry"

**Pattern to follow:** `eval_named_arg` (same file, line ~3349) pushes a `Diagnostic::warning(...)` before returning `None`. Use the same pattern here with `Diagnostic::warning(...)` before the early return.

tasks.title: Harden portable_timeout POSIX fallback: SIGKILL orphan cleanup, quoting, DRY grace period
tasks.description:
Three small robustness improvements to the portable_timeout POSIX fallback in scripts/lib_portable.sh, from review of Task 1185:

**S2 (most impactful): Post-wait orphan cleanup should use SIGKILL, not SIGTERM.**
Line 149: `kill -- -$cmd_pid` sends SIGTERM to clean up orphaned children after the timer has already proven SIGTERM was ineffective (the whole reason SIGKILL escalation fired). Change to `kill -9 -- -$cmd_pid 2>/dev/null || true` for consistency with the timer's escalation logic. The PID-reuse safety argument still holds: cmd_pid was just reaped, but the PGID remains valid as long as any member process exists.

**S1: Quote `$cmd_pid` in process-group kill for consistency.**
Lines 123 and 133: `kill -9 -- -$cmd_pid` leaves `$cmd_pid` unquoted, while the SIGTERM line above uses `kill "$cmd_pid"`. Change to `kill -9 -- "-$cmd_pid"` on both lines for defensive consistency (the value is always numeric from `$!`, so this is cosmetic).

**S4: Extract the 2-second SIGKILL grace period to a local variable.**
Lines 122 and 132 both hardcode `sleep 2`. Add `local _pt_kill_grace=2` near line 82 and reference `sleep "$_pt_kill_grace"` in both timer subshells to keep the value DRY.

Memory hints: search "portable_timeout SIGKILL escalation process-group" for context on the timer design; get_entity "lib_portable.sh" for file relationships.

tasks.description:
The non-empty guards on lines 61-62 of sync_comments_test.sh use `[ -z "$expr_body" ] && assert ... false` which records a FAIL but does not short-circuit execution. If both bodies are empty (e.g. function renamed), the subsequent `diff <(printf '%s' "") <(printf '%s' "")` succeeds, recording a spurious PASS for 'sanitize_value body is identical'. The overall test still fails (FAIL count > 0), but the misleading PASS in the output could confuse debugging.

Fix: convert the guards to early returns that also record the failure, either by calling `test_summary` after the assert (which exits non-zero due to FAIL count), or by wrapping the diff in a conditional that only runs when both bodies are non-empty.

tasks.title: Fix dimensional correctness in divergence/laplacian codomain type metadata
tasks.description:
compute_divergence (line 806) sets codomain_type to Type::Real unconditionally. For a dimensioned vector field (e.g., velocity: Point{3, Length} → Vector{3, Velocity}), the divergence has dimension Velocity/Length, not dimensionless Real. By contrast, compute_gradient correctly computes gradient_quantity = codomain_dim / domain_dim.

Similarly, compute_laplacian (line 962) preserves the original codomain_type unchanged, but the Laplacian has dimension codomain_dim / domain_dim².

Fix:
1. In compute_divergence: compute the result codomain dimension as codomain_component_dim / domain_dim (matching gradient's pattern)
2. In compute_laplacian: compute the result codomain dimension as codomain_dim / domain_dim²
3. This only affects type metadata — runtime SI values are already correct — but it matters for dimensional analysis downstream

   - divergence(gradient_field) → Undef (wrong source kind)

2. **Curl of irrotational field**: curl of a conservative field F=grad(φ) (e.g., F=[x,y,z]) should return approximately [0,0,0]. Other operators have "near zero" tests (divergence_constant_field_near_zero, laplacian_linear_field_near_zero) but curl doesn't.

3. **1D laplacian path**: laplacian(x²) at x=3.0 should equal approximately 2.0. The laplacian supports both 1D scalar domains and nD Point domains, but only the 3D case is tested. The 1D path takes a different branch in point extraction (Value::Real) with single_point_param=false and n=1.

tasks.title: Refine verify_uniqueness warn-emitting tests (robustness + return value)
tasks.description: Two small test-quality improvements for the warn-emitting tests added in Task 1143 (`crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs:1060-1107`): (1) Replace the brittle `assert_count_and_any_message_contains(2, ...)` — which couples the new tests to a secondary WARN from `solutions_agree` — with a filtered assertion that counts only messages containing "falling back to mid" (target: 1). This isolates the assertion to the new `verify_uniqueness` branch and is robust to refactors of the downstream pipeline. (2) Bind the return value of `verify_uniqueness` instead of `let _ = ...` and assert the expected uniqueness outcome end-to-end, so a regression where the fallback silently masks the bug is caught.
tasks.details:
Locations:
- crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs:1070 (missing-value test)
- crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs:1106 (non-numeric test)
- crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs:1066, 1102 (discarded return values)

Suggested shape:
```rust
let unique = verify_uniqueness(&problem, &solved_values);
let n_matching = capture
    .messages()
    .iter()
    .filter(|m| m.contains("falling back to mid"))
    .count();
assert_eq!(n_matching, 1, "expected exactly one fallback warn from verify_uniqueness");
assert!(!unique, "missing/non-numeric solved value should cause uniqueness check to fail");
```

Note: Before adding the `!unique` assertion, verify empirically that the empty-constraints problem + missing/Undef value actually results in `verify_uniqueness == false`. `solutions_agree` may also short-circuit on the None branch; the return-value assertion should match observed behavior rather than the reviewer's assumption.

tasks.title: Polish verify_uniqueness fallback warn: message clarity + aggregation
tasks.description: Two polish improvements to the `verify_uniqueness` fallback warn added in Task 1143 (`crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs:787-798`): (1) **Log message clarity**: The current message "falling back to mid for perturbation start" is misleading — `mid` is not used as the perturbation start, it is the comparison threshold that decides whether to start at the upper or lower half. Since `solution_val == mid` in the fallback path, the code takes the else branch and starts at `lo + 0.1*(hi-lo)`. Reword to e.g. "verify_uniqueness: solved value missing or non-numeric, using midpoint as comparison anchor; perturbation start defaults to lower-half side", or log the actual chosen perturbation start as a field after the if/else branch. (2) **Aggregation**: If multiple `auto_params` are simultaneously missing/non-numeric, the current per-param `tracing::warn!` inside the `.map()` iterator emits one WARN per param — a noisy burst at warn level for what is fundamentally a single internal-state bug. Preferred fix: collect the names of missing params during the iterator pass, then emit a single aggregated warn after the `perturbed` Vec is built. Alternative: keep first at warn level and demote subsequent occurrences to debug.

tasks.title: Task 1224 follow-up: clean up mcp_context_tests diagnostic assertions
tasks.description:
Address 5 review suggestions from Task 1224 on gui/src-tauri/src/tests/mcp_context_tests.rs::get_diagnostics_maps_warning_fields_to_diagnostic_info. All changes are confined to that one test function (~lines 538-618).

1. **Stale doc comment (lines 538-544):** The doc comment says "span arithmetic is already covered by engine_get_diagnostics_returns_populated_warning" but Task 1224 just added column/end_column/same-line-ordering assertions that contradict this. Update the doc comment to explain: (a) this test is the thin wrapping-path smoke test for TauriToolContext, (b) mcp_context.rs::get_diagnostics delegates straight to engine.rs via Ok(session.get_diagnostics()), so the mapping closure lives in engine.rs, (c) the same-line end_column >= column check is unique here; the rest of the span assertions intentionally duplicate engine_tests coverage or should be dropped (see #2).

2. **Duplicate range checks (lines 600-605):** `first.column >= 1` and `first.end_column >= 1` duplicate engine_tests.rs:884-887 and 896-900, which already cover the same mapping closure via EngineSession. Drop the bare `>= 1` checks here. Keep only the genuinely new same-line `end_column >= column` check (see #3). Optionally, move the same-line check into engine_tests.rs::engine_get_diagnostics_returns_populated_warning where the rest of the span coverage lives — that keeps mcp_context_tests focused on Result-wrapping.

3. **Conditional same-line check (lines 606-613):** The `if first.end_line == first.line { ... }` guard makes the `end_column >= column` assertion a silent no-op if the diagnostic ever becomes multi-line. For the `port mount : NonExistentTrait` fixture the diagnostic is always single-line, so assert `first.end_line == first.line` unconditionally (so the ordering check always runs), OR pin exact coordinates with assert_eq! against the known offset of `NonExistentTrait` in the source string. Pinning is strictly better at catching field-swap bugs.

4. **Bare TODO (line 616):** `// TODO: update when code extraction is implemented` has no task reference. Repo convention (seen in crates/reify-lsp/src/completion.rs:759,787,821,...) is `TODO(task-N): ...`. Either drop the TODO entirely (the line-615 comment already documents the invariant: "the mapping closure hardcodes code: None") or file a task for DiagnosticInfo.code extraction and reference it here. Preferred: drop the TODO, since it restates the line-615 comment.

5. **Comment accuracy (line 615):** The comment "the mapping closure hardcodes code: None (engine.rs)" is correct but understates the delegation: this test reaches engine.rs only through `Ok(session.get_diagnostics())` in mcp_context.rs:127-133. Fold the delegation note into the updated doc comment from #1 rather than leaving it at line 615.

tasks.title: Refactor field-calculus Vector sample-point tests and add dimension-guard coverage
tasks.description:
The three new tests added in Task 1220 (`divergence_accepts_vector_sample_point`, `curl_accepts_vector_sample_point`, `laplacian_accepts_vector_sample_point` in crates/reify-expr/tests/field_calculus_tests.rs around lines 354, 536, 730) are ~85-line near-clones of their existing Point counterparts (S2). They also declare the point-literal type as `Type::point3(Type::Real)` while the value is `Value::Vector(...)` — an inconsistent type/value pairing that would silently break if the literal type checker is strengthened (S3). Happy path is the only covered case for the new Vector variant (S6).

Fix:
1. Extract a parameterized helper per operator — e.g. `fn run_divergence_identity_test(point: Value, label: &str)` — or loop a single `#[test]` body over `[Value::Point(..), Value::Vector(..)]`. Collapse ~240 lines of duplicate boilerplate.
2. Either change the literal type on the Vector-sample-point tests to `Type::vec3(Type::Real)` (and verify runtime still accepts it), or add an inline comment documenting the intentional type/value mismatch and that these tests only exercise the runtime helper, not surface-syntax type checking.
3. Add a negative case for curl on a 2-element `Value::Vector` to lock in the `items.len() == 3` dimension guard: `assert!(matches!(eval_expr(&sample_expr, &ctx), Value::Undef));`.

tasks.title: Extract warning_source() helper to deduplicate the 6 NonExistentTrait literals in engine_tests.rs
test_portable_timeout.sh: helper polish (cleanup, self-test, dedup, naming, doc fixes)
tasks.description:
Seven small follow-up improvements to the SETUP_POSIX_FALLBACK_ENV helper added in Task 1142 (tests/infra/test_portable_timeout.sh). All seven items are from the post-merge review of esc-1142-1006.

## S1 — Fix helper docstring call-site list (lines 207-211, documentation_accuracy)
The docstring says "(16b, 18b)" and "all 4 tests (16a/b, 18a/b)" but the helper is actually used by Tests 16a/b and 21a/b — never by Test 18 (Test 18 uses POSIX_FALLBACK_SETUP for monitor-mode preservation). Replace `(16b, 18b)` with `(16b, 21b)` and `all 4 tests (16a/b, 18a/b)` with `all 4 tests (16a/b, 21a/b)`.

## S2 — Fix rescue_dir cleanup contract violation (lines 234, 333, 370, 518; contract_violation)
The helper docstring says "Callers must clean up rescue_dir explicitly" but only Test 21b (line 549) actually does. The other 4 call sites — self-test (~234), Test 16a (~333), Test 16b (~370), Test 21a (~518) — leak a temp dir per run, bloating /tmp under repeated CI runs and making the contract unenforceable.

Preferred fix: bake cleanup into the helper itself by exposing a sibling `CLEANUP_POSIX_FALLBACK_ENV='rm -rf "$rescue_dir"'` (or a single combined wrapper) so future call sites cannot forget. Alternative: add explicit `rm -rf "$rescue_dir"` to each of the 4 leaking sites mirroring the Test 21b pattern. If S4 is taken at the same time, fold into the unified helper instead.

## S3 — Strengthen helper self-test with load-bearing assertions (lines 233-239, test_coverage)
Self-test currently only verifies that timeout/gtimeout are absent from PATH and rescue_dir was created. It does NOT assert the helper's two most load-bearing properties:
1. `declare -f portable_timeout >/dev/null` — proves LIB_PORTABLE was actually sourced.
2. `[ -z "$(trap -p EXIT)" ]` — proves no EXIT trap is installed (the entire reason this helper exists; a regression that re-adds an EXIT trap would silently reintroduce the false-positive bug in 16b/21b).

Both are one-liners and would catch the exact regressions the helper guards against.

## S4 — Deduplicate the three setup blobs (lines 188-205 vs 212-228 vs MKTEMP_FAIL_SETUP, code_duplication)
SETUP_POSIX_FALLBACK_ENV, POSIX_FALLBACK_SETUP, and MKTEMP_FAIL_SETUP are now three near-identical ~15-line PATH-stripping blobs. Differences are: (a) symlink command list (POSIX_FALLBACK_SETUP omits `ps`/`bash`), (b) presence of EXIT trap, (c) TMPDIR override (MKTEMP_FAIL_SETUP). It has already drifted: `bash` was added to the new helper but not to POSIX_FALLBACK_SETUP.

Refactor: define a helper function `_build_posix_fallback_env()` taking the symlink command list (and trap/TMPDIR knobs) as args, and have all three SETUP variables call it. Or collapse to a single setup with environment knobs (`_PT_RESCUE_EXTRA_CMDS`, `_PT_INSTALL_EXIT_TRAP`, `_PT_OVERRIDE_TMPDIR`). This is the substantive refactor — S2 falls out of it naturally if cleanup is centralized.

## S5 — Fix Test 16a polling-loop comment arithmetic (lines 319-321, documentation_accuracy)
Comment says "polls up to 5×200ms (1s total)" but the loop body sleeps AFTER each ps check, so the 5th iteration's sleep is wasted (loop exits next). Total realized worst-case wait is 4×0.2s = 0.8s, not 1s. Either reword the comment to "5 checks separated by 4×200ms sleeps (~0.8s worst case)" or move the sleep to the start of the loop (skipped on first iteration via a guard) so the worst case actually equals 1s.

## S6 — Test 16a safety-net cleanup robustness (lines 361-364, robustness)
Two minor concerns with the safety-net `kill` block:
1. Uses `grep -E` not `grep -qE` — match would write to stdout (harmless under assert's `>/dev/null` redirect, but inconsistent with the `-qE` form used in the polling loop above and noisy if anyone debugs by removing the redirect).
2. Pattern matches system-wide on `[[:space:]]sleep 31337$`, so if Test 16a runs in parallel with itself (CI matrix on shared host, developer running suite twice), each instance terminates the other's sentinel and produces flaky positive-spawn checks.

Fix: switch to `grep -qE` for consistency, and at minimum document the "no-parallel-runs" assumption next to the safety-net. Per-run sentinel tagging is harder (the integer sleep duration can't easily encode `$$`) and probably not worth the complexity.

## S7 — Rename SETUP_POSIX_FALLBACK_ENV to match `*_SETUP` convention (line 212, naming_consistency)
Existing helpers use suffix `_SETUP` (`MKTEMP_FAIL_SETUP`, `POSIX_FALLBACK_SETUP`) but the new helper uses prefix `SETUP_` (`SETUP_POSIX_FALLBACK_ENV`). Rename to `POSIX_FALLBACK_SETUP_NO_TRAP` to (a) match the existing `*_SETUP` pattern and (b) telegraph the distinguishing property ("no EXIT trap") at every call site. Update all 5 call sites and the env-passing wrappers.

## Memory hints
- search: "SETUP_POSIX_FALLBACK_ENV helper duplication test_portable_timeout"
- search: "Task 1142 portable_timeout helper extraction"
- entity: "test_portable_timeout.sh"

## Notes
- All seven suggestions live in `tests/infra/test_portable_timeout.sh` only. No production code changes (`scripts/lib_portable.sh`) are needed.
- S2 and S4 are coupled: a unified helper (S4) with built-in cleanup naturally satisfies S2.
- S1, S5, S7 are pure docstring/naming fixes and could ship as a single trivial commit.
- S3 strengthens the existing self-test added in Task 1142 — keep it minimal (two extra one-liners).
- Existing pending Task 1214 covers `scripts/lib_portable.sh` hardening; it does NOT overlap with this test-file polish task.

tasks.title: Strengthen verify_uniqueness happy-path test + document solve_core dependency
tasks.description:
Two small follow-up improvements to the verify_uniqueness tests in `crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs` from Task 1227's review (reviewer_comprehensive S2 + S3).

(S2 — symmetry) The happy-path test `verify_uniqueness_no_warn_when_param_has_valid_f64` (~line 1164) still discards the return value with `let _ = verify_uniqueness(...)`. To match the strengthened contract on the warn-emitting tests, capture the return value and add `assert!(unique, "valid f64 should produce a unique solution");` — or document why the unique outcome is intentionally not asserted there.

(S3 — robustness comment) The new `assert!(!unique, ...)` assertions in `verify_uniqueness_warns_when_param_missing_from_solved_values` (~line 1114) and `verify_uniqueness_warns_when_param_is_non_numeric` (~line 1161) implicitly depend on `solve_core` converging on the perturbed point so that `solutions_agree` runs and returns false. If `solve_core` ever returned `Infeasible`/`NoProgress` for these trivial no-constraint problems, `verify_uniqueness` would conservatively return `true` via the early-return branch (~line 826) and the new assertion would flip to a misleading failure. Add a brief comment near each assertion noting this dependency so a future maintainer understands why the assertion is true.

Both changes are tiny, single-file, and out-of-scope for Task 1227 itself.
