tasks.title: Fix metadata-vs-runtime dimension drift in numerical divergence/laplacian handlers
tasks.description:
Task 1221 updated compute_divergence (lib.rs:803-841) and compute_laplacian (lib.rs:999-1035) to stamp the result Field's codomain_type with a dimensioned Type::Scalar, but the corresponding runtime sample handlers compute_numerical_divergence_at_point (lib.rs ~1536) and compute_numerical_laplacian_at_point (lib.rs ~1855) still return Value::Real(divergence) / Value::Real(laplacian) — i.e. dimensionless. This breaks the metadata-vs-runtime contract that was established for gradient: compute_gradient AND compute_numerical_gradient_at_point were updated together (see lib.rs:1206-1213 and 1368-1375 which build Value::Scalar { dimension: result_dim, ... } when result_dim != DIMENSIONLESS). Any downstream consumer that does `sample(div_field, p) * other_velocity` will silently treat the divergence as dimensionless because Value::Real propagates through arithmetic, whereas the type system thinks it's Scalar<1/Time>. This is the same class of bug the gradient fix closed.

Fix:
1. Compute the result dimension inside compute_numerical_divergence_at_point and compute_numerical_laplacian_at_point (mirroring the result_dim derivation in compute_numerical_gradient_at_point at lib.rs:1206-1213). This requires the dispatcher to forward the result codomain type — currently the sample handler dispatch at lib.rs:158/182 only forwards grad_codomain_type for the Gradient case. Add equivalent forwarding for Divergence/Laplacian, or compute the result_dim from domain_type alone (laplacian: cd / dd^2; divergence: cd / dd) inside the handler.
2. At the return site, wrap in Value::Scalar { si_value: ..., dimension: result_dim } when result_dim != DIMENSIONLESS, mirroring lib.rs:1368-1375.
3. Add at least one sample()-level test per operator: build a concrete dimensioned analytical lambda (e.g. f(x,y,z) = vec3(x, y, z) with x,y,z in meters and components in m/s), then assert sample(divergence(field), origin) returns a Value::Scalar with dimension == 1/Time, not Value::Real. The current new tests in field_calculus_tests.rs:1126-1438 only inspect metadata via divergence()/laplacian() and never call sample(), so they would not catch this drift.

Memory hints:
- search "gradient codomain_type result_dim Value::Scalar" — pattern from compute_numerical_gradient_at_point
- search "grad_codomain_type forwarded sample handler dispatch" — see lib.rs:158/182 for how dispatch carries codomain
- get_entity "Task 1221" — the partial fix this task completes

tasks.title: Unify fallback semantics across compute_gradient/divergence/laplacian (mixed-dim and Int handling)
tasks.description:
After Task 1221 the three field-calculus operators have three different fallback strategies for the dimension-extraction-and-divide logic, despite a comment claiming compute_divergence "Mirrors the gradient_quantity block":
- compute_gradient (lib.rs:650-690): falls back to Type::Real for dimensionless Scalar codomain, otherwise codomain_type.clone()
- compute_divergence (lib.rs:803-841): unconditionally falls back to Type::Real
- compute_laplacian (lib.rs:999-1035): unconditionally falls back to codomain_type.clone()

This creates corner-case asymmetries. Examples missing or wrong:
- (S4) Point{n, Real} → Vector{n, Scalar<Length>} should yield divergence codomain Length (Length/1), but compute_divergence returns Real
- (S4) Point{n, Scalar<Length>} → Vector{n, Real} should yield divergence codomain 1/Length, but returns Real
- (S8) compute_laplacian's domain validation accepts Type::Real, Type::Int, Type::Scalar, and Point{quantity ∈ {Real,Int,Scalar}}, but the new dim-extraction at lib.rs:1003-1010 only handles Type::Scalar and Point{Scalar} — Type::Real, Type::Int, Point{Real}, and Point{Int} all collapse to None. Same issue for codomain extraction at lib.rs:1013-1016.

Fix:
1. Pick one strategy and apply it to all three operators. The gradient strategy is most defensible: preserve codomain when one side is dimensionless, downgrade to Real only for explicitly-dimensionless Scalar. For divergence, this means extracting the Vector's component type and falling back to it.
2. Treat Type::Real and Type::Int as Some(DimensionVector::DIMENSIONLESS) in the extraction match arms, so the existing `dd != DIMENSIONLESS` guard handles them correctly (rather than silently missing the guard via None fallback).
3. Update the misleading comment at compute_divergence:804 to be accurate.
4. Add tests for the mixed-dimensional cases enumerated above (Real domain × Length codomain, Length domain × Real codomain, Int domain × dimensioned codomain) for all three operators.

Likely subsumed if the helper-extraction task lands first (the helper would force consistency). If both tasks proceed in parallel, coordinate via dependency.

Memory hints:
- search "compute_gradient gradient_quantity dimensionless fallback" for the canonical pattern
- get_entity "DimensionVector::DIMENSIONLESS" for the dim algebra

tasks.title: Extract scalar_field_codomain helper to deduplicate dim-extraction across gradient/divergence/laplacian
tasks.description:
After Task 1221, the dimension-extraction-and-divide block is copy-pasted three times across compute_gradient (lib.rs:650-690), compute_divergence (lib.rs:803-841), and compute_laplacian (lib.rs:999-1035). Each copy reimplements the same Type→Option<DimensionVector> projection and the same DIMENSIONLESS guard, with subtle drift between them (see the "unify fallback semantics" follow-up task).

1. Hoist a helper, e.g.

   ```
       domain_type: &Type,
       codomain_dim: Option<DimensionVector>,
       fallback: &Type,
       exponent: u32, // 1 for grad/div, 2 for laplacian
   ) -> Type
   ```
   or a pair of `extract_domain_dim` / `extract_codomain_dim` helpers.
2. Use it from all three operators. Collapses ~90 lines.
3. Bonus (S7): replace the absolute line references in the new comments at lib.rs:804 ("see line 650-690") and lib.rs:1000 with relative references like "see compute_gradient" — or eliminate the comments entirely once the helper exists. Stale absolute line references already caused issues elsewhere (Task 1174 stale-comment finding).

Coordinate with the "unify fallback semantics" task — landing this first would force consistency and obviate that task partially.

tasks.title: Expand field_calculus dimensional-correctness test coverage (1D/2D/Int/Composed/dimensionless-result)
tasks.description:
Task 1221's new tests in crates/reify-expr/tests/field_calculus_tests.rs:1132-1438 only cover the happy path (3D Point → Vector{3,Velocity} for divergence; 3D Point → Scalar<Temperature> for laplacian) plus one dimensionless regression. Missing branches that the new dim-extraction code traverses:

(a) 1D and 2D fields — laplacian validation accepts both, none are tested.
(b) Type::Int domains and codomains — laplacian validation explicitly accepts Int, but the new dim-extraction returns None for Int and falls through. No test exercises this path.
(c) The result-is-dimensionless-from-dimensioned-inputs branch — e.g. laplacian of Point{Length} → Scalar<Length²> should produce Real via the inner `if result_dim != DIMENSIONLESS … else Type::Real` branch. The smallest case is Length²/Length².
(d) Composed source kinds — laplacian validation accepts Composed but no test uses one.
(e) The cross-dimensional mixed cases tracked in the "unify fallback semantics" follow-up.

Fix: add parameterized cases for (a)-(d). Defer (e) to the unify-fallback task. Each test should mirror the existing dimensional_correctness pattern: build a Field with the target domain/codomain, call divergence()/laplacian(), assert the metadata. Once the metadata-vs-runtime drift task lands, also add sample() calls to these tests.

tasks.title: Task 1229 follow-up: refine doc/comments in get_diagnostics_maps_warning_fields_to_diagnostic_info
tasks.description:
Address 3 review suggestions on Task 1229's update to gui/src-tauri/src/tests/mcp_context_tests.rs::get_diagnostics_maps_warning_fields_to_diagnostic_info (current lines ~538-612). All changes are doc/comment-only and confined to that one test function.

1. **Doc comment accuracy (lines 541-545):** The comment currently says the `DiagnosticData → DiagnosticInfo` mapping closure "is already covered by `engine_get_diagnostics_returns_populated_warning` in `engine_tests.rs`." Confirmed against engine_tests.rs:881-900 — that test only does `first.line >= 1`, `first.column >= 1`, `first.end_line >= first.line`, `first.end_column >= 1` range checks. It does NOT validate exact span arithmetic, and would not catch a `(line, end_line)` or `(column, end_column)` transposition. After Task 1229, the new mcp_context test is in fact the ONLY place where exact span coordinates are pinned — the opposite of what the comment implies. Reword to be precise, e.g.: "The mapping closure is exercised by `engine_get_diagnostics_returns_populated_warning`, but that test only range-checks the span fields. The exact-coordinate assertions in this test are intentionally the only span-arithmetic regression guard for the diagnostics path."

2. **Exclusive-end clarity (lines 601-608):** The comments say "closing `}` of port body ends at line 4 of the fixture" / "ends at column 6 (1-indexed)." Ambiguous: the `}` itself is at L4:C5 (4 leading spaces + `}`). Column 6 is the position immediately AFTER the `}`, because tree-sitter's `end_byte` is exclusive and `offset_to_line_col_fast` computes `chars().count() + 1` from the line start. A future reader counting characters in the fixture will get column 5 for the `}` and may think the assertion is wrong. Clarify the exclusive-end convention, e.g.: "The span end is the byte one-past the closing `}`: the `}` itself sits at L4:C5, so the exclusive end position is L4:C6."

3. **Brittleness note in doc comment (lines 538-554):** Pinning exact coordinates couples this test to (a) the exact whitespace of the inline fixture, (b) which AST node tree-sitter reports as the label span for "unknown port type" warnings, and (c) the grammar's port_decl boundary definition. The current doc comment justifies the trade-off for `code: None` but doesn't warn that a more useful diagnostic — e.g. one that points at just `NonExistentTrait` instead of the whole port_decl — would also break this test. Add a sentence such as: "If the diagnostic span is later narrowed (e.g. to point at just `NonExistentTrait`), update the pinned coordinates rather than reverting to range checks — the exact pinning is the regression guard."

tasks.title: Polish robustness suite for sync_comments_test structural checks
tasks.description:
Apply four quality improvements to the robustness section of tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh (lines ~261-327) added by Task 1154:

1. **Stable check descriptions (lines 312-316).** Both branches of the non-underscore ref-guard test currently use different `check` descriptions ("...should FAIL" vs "...guard present → false"), which makes log grepping inconsistent and the "should FAIL" phrasing is misleading inside a runner that prints `FAIL:` for genuine failures. Use a single stable description and let `check` decide PASS/FAIL from a boolean — e.g. `if _check_has_no_ref_guard "$fixture_guard"; then ok=false; else ok=true; fi; check "_check_has_no_ref_guard rejects non-underscore ref variable" "$ok"`.

2. **Trap-based fixture cleanup (lines 295-327).** Three temp fixtures (`fixture`, `fixture_guard`, `fixture_clean`) are removed only on the linear success path, so under `set -euo pipefail` an early failure leaks them. Register cleanup once with `trap`, e.g. `_robust_fixtures=(); cleanup_robust() { rm -f "${_robust_fixtures[@]}"; }; trap cleanup_robust EXIT` plus a small `mk_fixture` helper that appends to the array. Collapses three `mktemp`/`rm` blocks and guarantees cleanup on early exit.

3. **Parameterize whitespace-tolerance fixture (line 296).** The `assert_sync_ref_exists` whitespace fixture only covers a single space. Loop over realistic variants the relaxed `\s*` is meant to admit — at minimum `''`, `' '`, `'  '`, `$'\t'` — so the relaxed contract is locked in.

4. **Symmetric helper names (lines 261-262).** Rename the file-local helpers from asymmetric `_check_defines_assert_sync_ref_exists` (positive) / `_check_has_no_ref_guard` (negative) to consistent positive form, e.g. `_has_assert_sync_ref_exists` and `_has_ref_guard`, and invert at the call site (`if ! _has_ref_guard "$SYNC_FILE"; then ...`). Robustness tests then read more uniformly.

All four are localized polish in the same robustness section; they should be bundled.

Verification: run `bash tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh` and confirm 53+ tests pass.

tasks.title: Pin historical guard regression and clarify scope of _check_has_no_ref_guard
tasks.description:
The relaxed `_check_has_no_ref_guard` pattern in tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh:262 (`grep -qE 'if \[ -n.*ref'`) and its robustness fixture have two related gaps:

1. **Missing literal historical fixture (line 262 robustness suite).** The robustness suite verifies the relaxed pattern catches `$ref_fn` (non-underscore), but it does NOT include a fixture matching the EXACT historical pre-refactor guard pattern that this work was meant to defend against — `if [ -n "$_expr_ref_fn" ]` and `if [ -n "$_stdlib_ref_fn" ]` (removed in commit ff0880bfe). Without that fixture, a future tightening of the regex could silently lose coverage of the actual historical regression while the new robustness test still passes. Add a third fixture mimicking the historical guard literally — e.g. `printf 'if [ -n "$_expr_ref_fn" ]; then echo skip; fi\n'` — and assert `_check_has_no_ref_guard` returns non-zero. This pins the regression case the helper exists to prevent.

2. **Pattern scope clarity (line 262).** The pattern `if \[ -n.*ref` only catches defensive guards whose tested variable name contains the literal substring `ref`. A future guard reintroduced as `if [ -n "$marker" ]` or `if [ -n "$fn_name" ]` would slip through. The commit message says "relax if-guard pattern beyond underscore prefix", but the relaxation is bounded to a different — equally narrow — keyword. Decide between:
   - (a) Tighten the comment/check description on lines 262/274 to make explicit that the helper only catches guards on `ref`-named variables (the historical regression class), OR
   - (b) Broaden the regex to catch any defensive existence guard around an extracted-name local — e.g. `if \[ -n .*\$.*_fn` or `if \[ -n.*_(ref_)?fn`.

These are tightly coupled: deciding the pattern scope (item 2) determines what fixtures should be pinned (item 1). Resolve them together.

Verification: run `bash tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh` and confirm both new fixtures behave as asserted, total tests still pass.

tasks.title: verify_uniqueness: early-return when missing params detected
tasks.description:
Optimize verify_uniqueness in crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs:781-816 to return false immediately after the aggregated WARN if `missing` is non-empty. The current code runs a full perturbed solve_core + solutions_agree cycle even though solutions_agree is guaranteed to return false on its first iteration (because the original solved_values map is missing the same param the perturbation closure already detected). This wastes a Nelder-Mead solve. Returning false directly both saves the work and produces a single, clean WARN per call.</description>
<parameter name="details">Source: review_comprehensive S5 from Task 1228 review (esc-1228-1017).

Implementation:
- After the `if !missing.is_empty()` warn block at solver.rs:807-816, add `return false;`.
- Update or add a test asserting that verify_uniqueness returns false WITHOUT calling solve_core when an auto_param is missing from solved_values. (One way: count solve_core invocations via a probe; simpler: rely on the existing `assert!(!unique, ...)` assertions in verify_uniqueness_warns_when_param_missing_from_solved_values and verify_uniqueness_warns_when_param_is_non_numeric, plus a runtime/timing observation in CI.)
- Confirm no other call site of verify_uniqueness depends on the side effects of the perturbed solve when missing != empty.

Reviewer note: "Outside the scope of [Task 1228] (which is purely a logging cleanup), but worth flagging because the new aggregation makes the fact that we already know the answer more obvious in the code."

tasks.title: verify_uniqueness warn tests: restore solutions_agree cross-coverage and extract helper
tasks.description:
Two related improvements to the three verify_uniqueness warn tests in crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs (lines ~1077-1277): (1) Restore the previously-tested invariant that solutions_agree also emits its WARN on the missing/non-numeric path. The current single-param tests (verify_uniqueness_warns_when_param_missing_from_solved_values at ~1086, verify_uniqueness_warns_when_param_is_non_numeric at ~1132) filter on the verify_uniqueness substring and assert == 1, dropping the prior `count == 2` assertion that covered solutions_agree's WARN — if solutions_agree's warn is later removed or downgraded, these tests will not catch it. (2) Reduce ~80% duplication across the three verify_uniqueness warn tests (missing-from-map, Value::Undef, two-missing aggregation) by extracting a small helper.</description>
<parameter name="details">Source: review_comprehensive S3 + S4 from Task 1228 review (esc-1228-1017).

S3 (lost_test_coverage at ~1086, ~1132):
- Add a second filter (e.g. count messages containing "cannot verify uniqueness" or whatever solutions_agree's WARN substring is) and assert it equals 1 in the single-missing-param tests.
- Alternative: assert `msgs.len() >= 2` plus the substring filter.
- Note: this assertion may need to be skipped if Task 1228-S5 (early-return when missing is non-empty) lands first, because early-return would prevent solve_core/solutions_agree from running. Coordinate sequencing — if the early-return task lands first, S3 becomes obsolete (instead, document that the test no longer exercises solutions_agree because the function short-circuits).

S4 (test_duplication at ~1044-1240):
- Extract a helper like `assert_verify_uniqueness_aggregated_warn(problem, solved_values, expected_param_substrings: &[&str])` that builds the WarnCapturingSubscriber, runs verify_uniqueness, applies the count/substring assertions, and returns the unique flag for the caller to assert against.
- Each test then becomes a few lines of setup.

Coordinate with S5 sibling task (early-return optimization) — if that lands first, the cross-coverage assertion in S3 becomes moot and the helper from S4 should be designed accordingly.

tasks.title: verify_uniqueness aggregated WARN: remove redundant missing field and fix test comment
tasks.description:
Cosmetic cleanup of the aggregated warn! macro in crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs:807-816 and its companion test comment: (1) The warn! macro references `missing` three times — as the structured field `missing_params = ?missing`, as `missing.len()`, and as `{:?}` interpolated into the message body — duplicating identical data. Drop one of the two redundant references. (2) The test comment at ~1265 in `verify_uniqueness_aggregates_warn_for_multiple_missing_params` is misleading: it claims param names appear "via ?missing_params field", but WarnCapturingSubscriber's MessageVisitor only captures the `message` field and ignores all structured fields. The test actually passes because of the `{:?}` placeholder in the format-string body. Fix the comment to reflect the truth.</description>
<parameter name="details">Source: review_comprehensive S1 + S2 from Task 1228 review (esc-1228-1017).

S2 (redundant_data at solver.rs:807-816): Choose one option:
  Option A (simpler, no test churn): Drop `missing_params = ?missing` from the structured fields, keep the body's `{}` and `{:?}` placeholders. The test continues to assert against the message body unchanged.
  Option B (richer, requires test_support enhancement): Drop the `{:?}` placeholder from the body, keep `missing_params = ?missing` as the canonical structured carrier, then enhance MessageVisitor in crates/reify-test-support/src/tracing_support.rs (or add a new visitor) to capture structured fields, and update tests to match against the structured rendering.

Recommendation: Option A is the smaller change. Option B is preferable architecturally (structured logs are best practice) but pulls in test_support changes.

S1 (test_comment_misleading at ~1265): Update the comment to reflect Option A or B. For Option A: `// Both param names must appear in the formatted message body (via the {:?} placeholder); WarnCapturingSubscriber's MessageVisitor does not capture structured fields.` Optionally add a brief note pointing at MessageVisitor in tracing_support.rs.

These two suggestions are coupled and should be addressed in one commit.

tasks.title: Strengthen divergence_gradient_field_returns_undef to isolate source-kind whitelist check
tasks.description:
Review suggestion from Task 1222. The test divergence_gradient_field_returns_undef in crates/reify-expr/tests/field_calculus_tests.rs (lines ~1802-1865) currently builds a 1D Field<Real, Real>, takes its gradient (producing Field<Real, Real, source=Gradient>), then passes that to divergence. The doc comment claims the test exercises compute_divergence's source-kind whitelist (lib.rs:742-747), but because the gradient field's domain is Type::Real (not Type::Point), the divergence call would still return Undef via the "Domain must be Point{n}" guard at lib.rs:764-771 even if the source-kind check were removed or reordered. The test therefore cannot uniquely catch a regression in the source-kind whitelist — another guard would shadow it.

Fix: Replace the 1D scalar input with a 3D scalar field (Point3<Real>->Real, lambda |x,y,z| x*x+y*y+z*z), so the inner gradient produces a Field<Point3, Vec3, source=Gradient> which passes domain/codomain/dim-match guards in compute_divergence. With those guards out of the way, the source-kind whitelist becomes the only thing that can return Undef, and the test isolates the behaviour the doc comment promises. Mirrors gradient_of_gradient_field_returns_undef in gradient_tests.rs, which uses the same 3D pattern.

Optional fold-ins (same test cluster, trivial cost):
- Align assertion style across the four Step 9 Undef-guard tests at field_calculus_tests.rs lines ~1718, 1739, 1802, 1873. divergence_non_field_returns_undef uses assert_eq!(result, Value::Undef, ...); the other three use matches!(&result, Value::Undef, ...). Pick one — either switch divergence_non_field_returns_undef to matches! to match the surrounding three (existing file pattern), or switch all four to assert_eq! for the better failure message.
- Optionally drop the intermediate `assert!(matches!(&grad_result, Value::Field { .. }), ...)` sanity check at line ~1839 in divergence_gradient_field_returns_undef. It re-tests behaviour already covered by gradient_1d_quadratic_accuracy and gradient_of_gradient_field_returns_undef, and adds noise to a test whose stated purpose is the divergence-of-gradient guard. (Keep if you prefer the failure breadcrumb.)

tasks.title: Replace warning_source fixture substring tests with real compile-and-assert-warning checks
tasks.description:
In `crates/reify-test-support/src/fixtures.rs:1247-1262`, the new tests `warning_source_is_well_formed` and `warning_source_with_width_has_width_param` only assert substrings of the literal they were just defined from. They don't verify the contract documented in the docstrings — namely that the source `reliably produces an "unknown port type" warning (not error)`.

If a future edit accidentally broke that contract (typo in `NonExistentTrait`, change in spacing the parser cares about, or a compiler severity reclassification), these tests would still pass while every dependent test in `engine_tests.rs` and `mcp_context_tests.rs` would explode. The crate already depends on `reify-compiler`, so the contract is testable here at fixture-definition time rather than waiting for the dependent suites to fail.

**Suggested fix:** Replace (or augment) the substring checks with a real compile assertion:
- Parse the source via `reify_syntax::parse`
- Run `reify_compiler::compile`
- Assert exactly one warning whose message contains both `"unknown port type"` and `"NonExistentTrait"`
- Assert no errors

For the `warning_source_with_width_has_width_param` variant, additionally assert that a `width` member is present in the compiled module.

Reference for the compile-and-assert pattern: `crates/reify-compiler/tests/port_compile_tests.rs:101-124` (already cited in the fixture's doc comment at fixtures.rs:41 as the validating test).

Source: review_comprehensive suggestion S2 on task 1232 (esc-1232-1020).

tasks.title: Tighten verify_uniqueness warn tests: drop verdict assertions, fix comment refs, revert misleading binding
tasks.description:
Three small follow-up improvements to the verify_uniqueness tracing tests in `crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs` from Task 1234's review (reviewer_comprehensive S1 + S2 + S3). All three address the same diff and should be done together.

(S1 — drop verdict assertions) The `assert!(!unique, ...)` lines added in Task 1234 at ~line 1126 (`verify_uniqueness_warns_when_param_missing_from_solved_values`) and ~line 1179 (`verify_uniqueness_warns_when_param_is_non_numeric`) are implicitly coupled to `solve_core` converging on the perturbed point so `solutions_agree` runs and returns false. Each test's stated contract (per its name and the `vu_warn_count` assertion) is the *fallback warn count*, not the verdict — the verdict assertion is redundant noise that introduces a real failure mode if `solve_core` is ever changed. Delete both `assert!(!unique, ...)` lines so each test focuses on the single behavior its name advertises. The warn-count assertion is sufficient to cover the None branch in `verify_uniqueness`.

(S2 — comment brittleness, becomes moot if S1 is done) The two NB comments at ~lines 1121-1125 and ~1174-1178 reference `(~line 826)` for the conservative early-return branch. Line-number references rot. If S1 is applied, both NB blocks should be deleted entirely (no verdict assertion → no fragility to document). If for any reason S1 is *not* applied, replace `(~line 826)` with a symbolic reference such as "the `_ => true` arm of the `match solve_core(...)` in `verify_uniqueness`" and de-duplicate by extracting the shared NB block.

(S3 — revert misleading binding) In `verify_uniqueness_no_warn_when_param_has_valid_f64` (~line 1222), revert `let _unique = tracing::subscriber::with_default(...)` back to `let _ = tracing::subscriber::with_default(...)`. The binding name `_unique` falsely suggests the value matters, then the comment has to walk that suggestion back. Idiomatic Rust uses `let _ = expr;` for explicit discard. Keep the explanatory comment above the line (it's the rationale; the variable name shouldn't carry it).

All changes are tiny, single-file, test-only.

tasks.title: Refactor verify_uniqueness fallback-warn tests to test the None-branch in isolation
tasks.description:
Follow-up from Task 1234's review (reviewer_comprehensive S4, marked explicitly out of scope for the comments-only diff).

The three `verify_uniqueness_*` tracing tests in `crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs` (~lines 1078-1227) all exercise `verify_uniqueness` end-to-end, transitively invoking `solve_core` and `solutions_agree`, just to assert behavior of the local None-branch warn at `verify_uniqueness` lines ~792-799 (`unwrap_or_else(|| { missing.push(...); mid })` followed by the `tracing::warn!(...)` block). This end-to-end coupling is what produced the implicit-coupling problem Task 1234 documented and Task [S1 follow-up] cleaned up.

Two refactor options to consider:
(a) Construct a problem whose perturbed solve is provably stable so the implicit coupling becomes intentional/asserted (e.g. a single-param problem with a tight bound that reliably converges).
(b) Factor the `as_f64()` / mid-fallback / `missing` collection logic out of `verify_uniqueness` into a tiny private helper (e.g. `build_perturbation_anchors` returning `(Vec<f64>, Vec<String>)`) so the missing-collection + warn-emission can be unit-tested in isolation, leaving `verify_uniqueness` as a thin orchestration layer.

`solutions_agree` already has its own dedicated tests, and `verify_uniqueness` is module-private, so both options are open. Option (b) is preferred because it eliminates the coupling rather than working around it.

tasks.title: Bundle: CompiledModule filter iterators + has_test_annotation + @test semantics
tasks.description: Consolidates CompiledModule filter helper API slimming (return iterators), has_test_annotation helper extraction (eliminate three-site duplication), is_test encapsulation on TopologyTemplate with asymmetry docs, and @test-on-function half-support resolution.
tasks.details:
**BUNDLED TASK** (bundle consolidation 2026-04-11). This task consolidates task 1251 plus 3 sibling polish task(s) that share a module footprint. Original per-task content preserved verbatim below; implement all sections in one PR.


### Original task 1251: Return iterators and slim API surface of CompiledModule filter helpers


In `crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs` lines 178-199, change `test_templates`, `non_test_templates`, `test_constraint_defs`, and `non_test_constraint_defs` to return `impl Iterator<Item = &TopologyTemplate>` / `&ConstraintDef` instead of `Vec`. While doing so, evaluate whether the four methods can be replaced by two (`test_templates_iter`, `test_constraint_defs_iter`) and let callers negate with `.filter(|t| !t.is_test)` — eliminating the API-surface doubling identified in suggestion [1].

### Absorbed from task 1252: Extract has_test_annotation helper to eliminate three-site duplication
The expression `annotations.iter().any(|a| a.name == "test")` is inlined verbatim in three places: `crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs:6384`, `crates/reify-syntax/src/lib.rs:465`, and `crates/reify-test-support/src/builders/topology.rs:356`. Extract a single helper (e.g. `fn has_test_annotation(anns: &[impl HasName]) -> bool` or one helper per annotation type) and replace all three call sites. Document that `"test"` is the canonical lowercase spelling.

### Absorbed from task 1253: Encapsulate is_test on TopologyTemplate and document is_test asymmetry
In `crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs` around line 248: (a) make `is_test` a private field and expose it via a `pub fn is_test(&self) -> bool` getter, with a doc note that mutating `annotations` post-construction is unsupported. This is symmetric with `ConstraintDef::is_test()`. (b) Add TODO comments on `test_constraint_defs`/`non_test_constraint_defs` (lib.rs ~192-199) and on `ConstraintDef::is_test()` in `crates/reify-syntax/src/lib.rs:465` noting that the method will move to a `CompiledConstraintDef::is_test` field once constraint-def lowering lands.

### Absorbed from task 1254: Resolve @test-on-function half-support
In `crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs:4728`, `function` is listed as a valid context for `@test`, but `CompiledFunction` (re-exported from `reify_types`) has no `is_test` field and `CompiledModule` has no `test_functions()` helper. Either (a) remove `function` from the valid context match arm if test-functions are out of scope for Task 267, or (b) add `CompiledFunction::is_test()` in `reify_types` and `CompiledModule::test_functions()` / `non_test_functions()` in `crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs` to make the API coherent.

tasks.title: Polish warning_source fixture validation helpers (style + width type assertion)
tasks.description:
Two small improvements to the validation helpers added in Task 1247 to `crates/reify-test-support/src/fixtures.rs`:

**S1 — style_imports (line 1252, 1264, 1272):** The helper return type uses the fully-qualified `reify_compiler::CompiledModule`, but `CompiledModule` is already brought into scope at line 1 (`use reify_compiler::{CompiledModule, RequirementKind};`). Same for `reify_types::Severity` references at lines 1264 and 1272.

Fix:
- Change the return type of `assert_warning_source_compiles_with_unknown_port_warning` to bare `CompiledModule`.
- Add `use reify_types::Severity;` either to the test mod or to the existing top-level use list, then use `Severity::Error` / `Severity::Warning` directly in the predicates.

**S2 — test_coverage (lines 1293-1310):** `warning_source_with_width_compiles_and_has_width_value_cell` only asserts that some value cell whose `id.member == "width"` exists. The fixture's stated purpose (lines 52-53) is to provide a `width` field for `get_source_location` lookup, which depends on the cell having the right *type* (`Length`) and a real source span. A regression that silently swapped `param width : Length = 80mm` to e.g. `Int = 0` would still pass.

Fix:
- After locating the `width` value cell, also assert it is the expected `Length`/`Scalar` shape (inspect `vc.cell_type` against the actual enum variants in `reify_types`).
- And/or assert that its source span is non-empty, mirroring how `get_source_location` will use it.

Source: review_comprehensive S1 + S2 on Task 1247 (esc-1247-1023).

tasks.title: Task 1239 follow-up: soften "ONLY" claim and reorder exclusive-end comment in get_diagnostics_maps_warning_fields_to_diagnostic_info
tasks.description:
Doc/comment-only refinements to gui/src-tauri/src/tests/mcp_context_tests.rs::get_diagnostics_maps_warning_fields_to_diagnostic_info from a second-round review of Task 1239's doc updates. Both changes are confined to that one test function (current lines ~538-622).

1. **Soften "ONLY span-arithmetic regression guard" claim (lines ~550-552).** The current doc comment from Task 1239 says the pinned-coordinate assertions are "the ONLY span-arithmetic regression guard in the test suite." Verified against `gui/src-tauri/src/tests/engine_tests.rs::get_diagnostics_multi_diagnostic_stress_matches_reference` at lines 1499-1575: that test pins exact line/column/end_line/end_column for THREE injected diagnostics by comparing against a `byte_offset_to_line_col` oracle for each span. It does catch any line↔column transposition bug in the engine.rs mapping closure (engine.rs:354-365). The wrapping path in mcp_context.rs:127-133 is a pure passthrough with no field reassignment, so the actual span-arithmetic logic the doc is worried about is also covered by that engine stress test. The mcp_context test still has unique value (it uses a real compiler-emitted span on a real port_decl AST node and exercises the wrapping path's `Ok` return), but the "ONLY" claim mis-describes coverage redundancy. Reword to something like: "the only test that pins literal exact-coordinate values for a real compiler-emitted span — the engine multi-diagnostic stress test (`get_diagnostics_multi_diagnostic_stress_matches_reference`) pins coordinates against a `byte_offset_to_line_col` reference for synthetic injected spans, so this test provides complementary coverage on a real port_decl span shape."

2. **Reorder the exclusive-end clarity comment (lines ~607-612).** The new explanatory block from Task 1239 currently arrives at column 5 first ("four leading spaces → prefix chars().count() = 4, 1-indexed = 5") then pivots to column 6 via the exclusive end byte. Since the assertion is `end_column == 6`, leading with the end-byte semantics and treating the glyph column as a sanity check reads more linearly. Rephrase along these lines: "// Tree-sitter end_byte is exclusive — for the closing `}` of the port body on line 4 (4 spaces + `}`), end_byte points one past `}` (at the trailing `\\n`). offset_to_line_col_fast computes chars().count() of source[line_start..end_byte] + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6. (The `}` glyph itself sits at column 5; end_column 6 is the exclusive one-past position.)"

Both edits are pure rewording — no test logic, no assertions, no fixture changes. Verify the test still compiles and runs after the comment edits (`cargo test -p reify-tauri get_diagnostics_maps_warning_fields_to_diagnostic_info` from gui/src-tauri).

tasks.title: Harden assert_sync_ref_exists test isolation via sourceable helper
tasks.description: Follow-up to Task 1168. Reviewer flagged 6 related concerns about the behavioral/robustness tests for assert_sync_ref_exists in tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh. The primary refactor addresses the root cause; the remaining items are smaller hardenings in the same region.
tasks.details:
Six suggestions from reviewer_comprehensive (esc-1168-1030), grouped because they all target the same test region and most become moot once the primary refactor lands.

**S1 — Fragile sed extraction (primary fix)**
Location: tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh:364,422
The sed range `/^assert_sync_ref_exists()/,/^}/p` extracts to the first `^}` line. A future heredoc terminator, nested function, or JSON/shell formatted with `}` in column 0 inside the body would silently truncate mid-body; the extracted snippet may parse but be semantically broken, causing confusing false FAILs.
Fix: Move `assert_sync_ref_exists` out of `tests/sync_comments_test.sh` into a sourceable helper (e.g., `tests/infra/sync_ref_helpers.sh`) so tests can `source` it directly without text extraction. Update `sync_comments_test.sh` to source the helper. As a fallback, place `# region:assert_sync_ref_exists` / `# endregion:assert_sync_ref_exists` markers around the function and narrow the sed range to the sentinels.

**S2 — Structural meta-tests duplicate the robustness test**
Location: tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh:376-403 (behavioral_test_block isolation meta-tests)
The three checks (sed -n present, no bare source, trap registered) duplicate what the robustness test at lines 405-431 already proves behaviorally (the `exit 99` injection strictly proves full-file sourcing is not happening). They also couple to a specific implementation (exact regex, single-quoted source, literal trap-with-rm-f-EXIT), so any future cleanup breaks them without a real regression. Per simplify principle: test overengineering.
Fix: Drop the three structural meta-tests at 380-403 and rely on the robustness test plus the existing structural check at line 268 ("defines assert_sync_ref_exists"). Collapse to a single comment explaining why the robustness test exists.

**S3 — Negative-source regex incomplete**
Location: tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh:392
Regex `source[[:space:]]+'[^<]*SYNC_FILE` only catches single-quoted `source '${SYNC_FILE}'`. Double-quoted, unquoted variable, or `$SYNC_FILE` would slip past. Addressed as a byproduct of S2 (deletion). If meta-test is retained for any reason, broaden to `source[[:space:]]+["'\$][^<]*SYNC_FILE`.

**S4 — Trap-detection regex loose**
Location: tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh:399
Regex `trap[[:space:]]+.*rm[[:space:]]*-f.*EXIT` — `[[:space:]]*` allows `rm-f` (invalid), and `.*EXIT` is unanchored (would match EXIT appearing anywhere later). Addressed as a byproduct of S2 (deletion). Otherwise tighten to `trap[[:space:]]+'[^']*rm[[:space:]]+-f[^']*'[[:space:]]+EXIT`.

**S5 — Robustness test FAIL check is weak**
Location: tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh:427
Currently only greps for word `FAIL` in `_rob_out`. Doesn't check subshell exit code — could spuriously pass if `FAIL` appears in unrelated error output (e.g., parse error mentioning "FAILED"). Stronger form: capture rc separately and assert rc != 99 AND grep for the anchored `^  FAIL:` form that the assert helper actually emits.
Fix: `_rob_out=$(bash -c "..." 2>&1); _rob_rc=$?; [ "$_rob_rc" -ne 99 ] && echo "$_rob_out" | grep -q '^  FAIL:'`.
Apply the same tightening to the sibling behavioral test at line 370.

**S6 — Duplication between behavioral test and robustness test**
Location: tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh:356-367, 412-425
The two subshells share ~80% scaffolding (mktemp pair, trap, SYNC marker file, stub target, source HELPER_FILE, source extracted function, reset PASS/FAIL, call assert_sync_ref_exists). Only the SYNC source differs (original vs. altered-with-exit-99).
Fix: Extract a helper function in the test file that takes the sync file path to extract from, runs the scenario, and echoes output. Both tests then call it with different file arguments.

**Suggested order**
1. S1 (sourceable helper) — removes the extraction text-munging entirely
2. S2/S3/S4 (delete structural meta-tests) — eliminated by S1
3. S6 (dedupe scaffolding into helper function)
4. S5 (harden FAIL assertion with exit code + anchored match)

tasks.title: Cleanup: refactor div/lap sample handlers (rename binding, extract helper, debug_assert, stable comments)
tasks.description:
Follow-up from Task 1235 review (esc-1235-1032). Four related cleanups in crates/reify-expr/src/lib.rs around the div/lap sample-handler dispatch:

S1 (naming, lib.rs:126,149,162,187): The destructure `codomain_type: grad_codomain_type` is now forwarded to compute_numerical_divergence_at_point and compute_numerical_laplacian_at_point as well as the gradient arm. The `grad_` prefix is misleading. Rename to `outer_codomain_type` (or drop the rename-on-destructure to use `codomain_type`) and update all three forwarding call sites.

S2 (duplication, lib.rs ~1150-1395, ~1397-1563, ~1728-1908): The result_dim extraction (`match codomain_type { Type::Scalar { dimension } => *dimension, _ => DIMENSIONLESS }`) and the Value::Scalar/Value::Real return-site wrap are now triplicated across gradient, divergence, and laplacian. Divergence and laplacian blocks are identical word-for-word; gradient's variant additionally matches Type::Vector. Extract a helper like `fn wrap_scalar_result(value: f64, codomain_type: &Type) -> Value` returning Value::Scalar for Type::Scalar and Value::Real otherwise. Use from divergence/laplacian return sites; gradient can reuse per-component (Vector arm handled separately).

S3 (doc_drift, lib.rs:1448, 1554, 1784, 1896): The new comments reference gradient-handler line numbers (`lib.rs:1206-1213`, `lib.rs:1368-1375`) which are already off by a couple of lines. Replace with stable descriptors like "Mirrors the result_dim extraction in compute_numerical_gradient_at_point" and "Mirrors the gradient handler's return-site wrapping (result_dim != DIMENSIONLESS branch)".

S7 (robustness, lib.rs:1445-1452, 1781-1788): Add `debug_assert!(matches!(codomain_type, Type::Real | Type::Scalar { .. }), "divergence/laplacian codomain must be scalar, got {:?}", codomain_type);` at the top of compute_numerical_divergence_at_point and compute_numerical_laplacian_at_point. Guards against future stamping drift in compute_divergence/compute_laplacian without changing release behavior.

tasks.title: Propagate dimensions through curl (compute_curl + compute_numerical_curl_at_point)
tasks.description:
Follow-up from Task 1235 review (esc-1235-1032, S4, consistency). Curl was not updated by Tasks 1221 or 1235, creating an inconsistent story where div/lap/grad propagate dimensions under the type system but curl silently does not.

Locations:
- crates/reify-expr/src/lib.rs compute_curl (lines ~863-933): currently preserves the input codomain verbatim (`codomain_type: codomain_type.clone()`) rather than dividing the vector-component dimension by domain_dim.
- crates/reify-expr/src/lib.rs compute_numerical_curl_at_point (~lines 1717-1725): unconditionally wraps components in Value::Real.
- crates/reify-expr/src/lib.rs sample-handler dispatch (~lib.rs:170-176): currently does NOT forward codomain_type to compute_numerical_curl_at_point. Will need the equivalent plumbing the Divergence/Laplacian arms already have.

Expected behavior: a curl of Point{3,Length} → Vector{3,Velocity} should produce per-component dimension 1/Time (same as divergence). compute_curl should stamp Vector{3, Scalar{dim = input_component_dim / domain_dim}} and compute_numerical_curl_at_point should wrap components in Value::Scalar when result_dim != DIMENSIONLESS.

Tests: mirror the div/lap sample-level dimensional-correctness tests in field_calculus_tests.rs — build a concrete dimensioned analytical lambda, call sample(curl(field), point), assert per-component Value::Scalar with the expected derived dimension.

tasks.title: Strengthen div/lap sample tests: numeric asserts + mixed-case coverage
tasks.description:
Follow-up from Task 1235 review (esc-1235-1032), two test gaps in crates/reify-expr/tests/field_calculus_tests.rs.

S5 (test_quality, lines 1723-1734, 2040-2051): `divergence_sample_dimensionless_returns_real` and `laplacian_sample_dimensionless_returns_real` only assert on the Value variant, not the numeric value. Both test lambdas have a known analytical answer (divergence of vec3(1,1,1) ≡ 0, laplacian of x+2y+3z ≡ 0). Destructure the Value::Real(v) arm and assert `v.abs() < 1e-4` against the analytical value. Zero fixture cost, catches numerics regressions as well.

S6 (test_coverage_gap, lines 1746-1878, 2063-2206): The two new dimensional-correctness tests only cover both-sides-dimensioned cases (Length→Velocity, Length→Temperature). The mixed cases — dimensioned domain + dimensionless codomain (Point{3,Length}→Vector{3,Real}), and dimensionless domain + dimensioned codomain (Point{3,Real}→Vector{3,Velocity}) — are never exercised at the sample level. Both currently hit compute_divergence/compute_laplacian's `_ => Type::Real` fallback and end up in the dimensionless runtime branch. A divergence of Length→Real field should physically be 1/Length but no test documents this. Either add `#[test] #[ignore]` placeholders or TODO tests for the two mixed cases, or add a code comment on compute_divergence/compute_laplacian explaining the fallback intentionally loses dimensional information, so future readers don't "fix" the fallback without also updating the numerical call sites.

tasks.title: Consolidate Task 272 @deprecated tests with pre-existing coverage
tasks.description: Refactor crates/reify-compiler/tests/deprecated_use_tests.rs to eliminate the duplication introduced by the "Task 272: five dedicated regression tests" block. Four of the five new scenarios re-use identical source strings with pre-existing tests and add only exact-count and weak-substring assertions. The parallel "Task 272" section header also competes with the existing Step 1 / Step 3 / Step 5 organization, creating two organization schemes for the same behaviors.
tasks.details:
Source: reviewer_comprehensive (8 suggestions) on commit that added tests around lines 333-555.

Apply these changes to crates/reify-compiler/tests/deprecated_use_tests.rs:

1. Scenario (1) `task_272_deprecated_entity_emits_warning_on_use` (lines 337-383) vs. `deprecated_structure_used_as_sub_emits_warning` (lines 49-76): fold the new `assert_eq!(warns.len(), 1, ...)` and the use-site span check into the existing test, then delete the task_272 duplicate. Identical source strings will drift.

2. Scenario (2) `task_272_no_warning_on_definition_alone` (lines 388-400): drop part (a) (structure) — it is already covered by `defining_deprecated_entity_without_using_produces_no_use_warning` (lines 231-252) AND `annotation_compile_tests_no_regression` (lines 316-331). Keep only parts (b) (fn-only) and (c) (trait-only), and rename to reflect that narrower scope (e.g. `deprecated_fn_and_trait_definition_alone_produce_no_warning`).

3. Scenario (4) `task_272_deprecated_trait_emits_warning_when_implemented` (lines 485-517) vs. `deprecated_trait_used_as_trait_bound_emits_warning` (lines 144-170): fold the exact-count assertion into the existing test and delete the duplicate. The `msg.contains("trait")` check is a no-op because `deprecation_warnings` already filters by the substring `OldTrait`.

4. Also rename: the phrase `when_implemented` is misleading — in Reify terminology `structure S : OldTrait` is a trait bound, not a trait implementation (see lib.rs ~5381, ~5396). If any variant survives, use `when_used_as_trait_bound`, matching `deprecated_trait_used_as_trait_bound_emits_warning`.

5. Scenario (5) `task_272_deprecated_fn_emits_warning_when_called` (lines 520-555) vs. `deprecated_function_called_emits_warning` (lines 111-140): fold the exact-count assertion into the existing test and delete the duplicate. `msg.contains("function")` is a no-op for the same reason.

6. Scenario (1) use-site span assertion (lines 373-382) is too loose — it only checks `span.start >= use_site_offset`, accepting any span that starts at or after `sub b`, including spans at EOF. Tighten to also assert `label.span.end as usize <= source.len()` and that `&source[label.span.start as usize .. label.span.end as usize]` contains `"OldBolt"`. (After consolidation, these assertions should live in the now-enriched `deprecated_structure_used_as_sub_emits_warning`.)

7. Scenario (3) `task_272_message_includes_annotation_argument` (lines 477-481): the individual `contains` checks are redundant with the final `assert_eq!` on the exact message. Pick one style. Recommend keeping `assert_eq!` (contract test) and dropping the `contains` checks, plus add a one-line comment linking to `emit_deprecation_warning` in crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs (~line 4800) so future readers know where the contract is defined. This scenario is the only genuinely new coverage worth keeping under a `task_272_` name — or fold it into the file's "message format" section.

8. Remove the `// ── Task 272: five dedicated regression tests ─────` section header (line 333). After consolidation, group tests by behavior (structure use, fn call, trait bound, trait refinement, definition-only, message format, span correctness) matching the existing Step 1 / Step 3 / Step 5 scheme — not by task number.

Verify with `cargo test -p reify-compiler --test deprecated_use_tests` and `cargo fmt --check`.

Context: Task 272 is the @deprecated test work. Dependencies: 271 (implementation).

tasks.title: Polish warning_source_with_width_produces_length_typed_width_cell_with_span test
tasks.description:
Three small polish items on the `warning_source_with_width_produces_length_typed_width_cell_with_span` test in `crates/reify-test-support/src/fixtures.rs` (lines ~1294-1346), flagged by reviewer_comprehensive on Task 1259 (esc-1259-1035). All three target the same small test block and can be a single commit.

**S1 — test_maintainability (lines 1329-1334):** The assertion failure message hard-codes the byte range `(bytes 22..49 in warning_source_with_width())`. The test itself is resilient (it derives `span_text` dynamically from `width_cell.span.start/end`), but this diagnostic comment will silently go stale if `warning_source_with_width()` is ever reformatted. The assertion already prints `{width_cell.span:?}` and the covered text, so the literal byte offsets add little value. Fix: drop the `(bytes 22..49 in warning_source_with_width())` parenthetical from the assertion message.

**S2 — test_naming (line 1295):** The test is named `warning_source_with_width_produces_length_typed_width_cell_with_span`, but now also asserts `ValueCellKind::Param` and `default_expr.is_some()`. The name undersells the extra behaviors covered, making it harder to locate this test when scanning failures for kind/default_expr regressions. Fix: rename to reflect all four assertions, e.g. `warning_source_with_width_param_cell_has_length_type_span_kind_and_default`, OR split into two focused tests (one for span, one for kind/default) if the single name gets unwieldy.

**S3 — robustness (lines 1324-1325, optional):** `&source[width_cell.span.start as usize..width_cell.span.end as usize]` will panic with an opaque out-of-bounds / non-char-boundary message if the compiler ever produces a span outside the source or straddling a multibyte boundary. The fixture is ASCII and the compiler-produced span is in-range today, so this is fine in practice. Optional fix:
```rust
let (start, end) = (width_cell.span.start as usize, width_cell.span.end as usize);
let span_text = source.get(start..end).unwrap_or_else(|| {
    panic!("width_cell.span {:?} out of bounds for source of len {}", width_cell.span, source.len())
});
```

Verify with `cargo test -p reify-test-support`.

tasks.title: verify_uniqueness test helper: doc/naming polish (S1/S3/S4 from Task 1243 review)
tasks.description: Three small doc/naming polish items on the `assert_verify_uniqueness_aggregated_warn` helper and surrounding section comment in crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs, from the reviewer_comprehensive review of Task 1243. Non-blocking; purely readability.
tasks.details:
Source: reviewer_comprehensive review of Task 1243 (esc-1243-1036). Three suggestions:

S1 (solver.rs:942-944, doc forward reference):
- The helper doc comment at ~L942 says "See the section comment at `// ---- verify_uniqueness tracing tests ----`" but that section comment lives ~185 lines later (~L1130).
- Fix: either (a) inline a 2-line tl;dr of the S3/early-return rationale directly in the helper doc, or (b) say "see the section comment below, above the `verify_uniqueness_warns_*` tests" so the pointer is self-locating.

S3 (solver.rs:948, parameter naming):
- `expected_param_substrings: &[&str]` is slightly misleading — the helper only checks substring presence in the joined WARN text, but all 3 call sites pass `ValueCellId`-style strings (e.g. "Part.x").
- Fix: rename to `expected_missing_param_ids` (matches what callers pass) OR `expected_warn_substrings` (matches what the helper actually checks). Either is fine; pick one.

S4 (solver.rs:1130-1147, section comment load-bearing):
- The section comment asserts that a `solutions_agree` WARN count on these tests "would always be 0" because of the Task 1242 early-return. This is load-bearing — if the early-return invariant ever changes, the comment becomes stale guidance.
- Fix: add a one-line cross-reference at the end of the section comment to the existing test `verify_uniqueness_skips_solve_core_when_param_missing` at solver.rs:1307, which ALREADY asserts 0 DEBUG events from the `reify_constraints` target (proving solve_core is not invoked). This makes the invariant test-enforced and the section comment self-witnessed.
- Suggested wording: `// NB: the early-return invariant is directly asserted by `verify_uniqueness_skips_solve_core_when_param_missing` below.`

Dismissed from the same review:
- S2 (test_coverage/strictness at solver.rs:959-968) — reviewer explicitly said "No change needed if the decoupling is intentional." It is intentional per the section comment at L1130-1147, and the S3 substring check already ties each match to a specific missing_params payload.

tasks.title: Tighten _check_has_no_if_n_guard regex scope and false-positive robustness
tasks.description:
The `_check_has_no_if_n_guard` helper in tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh:265 uses the regex `if \[ -n`, which has two related gaps flagged by reviewer_comprehensive on Task 1241:

1. **Alternate guard forms slip through.** The regex only matches the single-bracket single-line `if [ -n` form. Equivalent defensive guards that a future author might (re)introduce without realizing they're prohibited are not caught:
   - `if [[ -n "$x" ]]`
   - `if test -n "$x"`
   - `if [ ! -z "$x" ]`
   - split-line variants with a newline after `[`
   The stated intent of the new comment ("any `if [ -n` conditional regardless of the variable name") is narrower than the broader goal of banning defensive guards.

2. **False-positive risk from comments and string literals.** Because `grep` inspects every line of the file (including comments and heredocs), a future comment or string literal in `tests/sync_comments_test.sh` containing the literal text `if [ -n` (e.g. a comment explaining *why* the guard was removed) would cause structural check (b) to fail even though no actual guard exists.

Resolution: pick one of these approaches and apply it consistently.
- (a) Extend the regex to e.g. `(if|&&|\|\|)[[:space:]]*(\[\[?|test)[[:space:]]+-n` to cover `[[` / `test` forms; and anchor to start-of-line (`^[[:space:]]*`) OR strip comment lines before matching (`grep -v '^[[:space:]]*#' | grep -qE ...`) to kill the comment false-positive.
- (b) Document the limitation explicitly adjacent to `_check_has_no_if_n_guard` so a future contributor debugging a surprising failure finds the explanation, AND add an explicit comment noting that `[[` / `test` forms are intentionally out of scope and must be banned by reviewers rather than the automated check.

If you choose (a), add fixtures exercising each newly-supported form (`[[`, `test`, `!-z`) in the robustness suite, plus a negative fixture proving a comment line containing `if [ -n` does NOT trip the helper.

Verification: `bash tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh` passes with the new fixtures.

tasks.title: Add positive -z fixture to pin _check_has_no_if_n_guard n/z boundary
tasks.description:
In tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh:409-417, the current "clean file" fixture (`printf '# no guards here\necho hello\n'`) only proves `_check_has_no_if_n_guard` tolerates a file with *nothing* that looks like a guard. It does NOT prove the helper tolerates the legitimate early-fail `-z` guards that actually exist in `tests/sync_comments_test.sh` — specifically `[ -z "$ref_fn" ]` and `[ -z "$expr_body" ]`.

Since the whole point of broadening the regex from `if \[ -n.*ref` to `if \[ -n` was to keep `-z` guards allowed while banning `-n` guards, a positive fixture that asserts `_check_has_no_if_n_guard` returns 0 (true) for a file containing `if [ -z "$ref_fn" ]; then ...` would directly pin that contract and catch a future over-tightening such as someone broadening the regex to `\[ -[nz]`.

Fix (mirror of the historical-pin test, but in the positive direction):
```bash
fixture_z=$(mktemp)
printf 'if [ -z "$ref_fn" ]; then echo fail; fi\n' > "$fixture_z"
if _check_has_no_if_n_guard "$fixture_z" 2>/dev/null; then
    check "if-guard pattern tolerates legitimate -z early-fail guard (should PASS)" "true"
else
    check "if-guard pattern tolerates legitimate -z early-fail guard" "false"
fi
rm -f "$fixture_z"
```

Verification: `bash tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh` passes; the new fixture asserts TRUE.

Flagged by reviewer_comprehensive on Task 1241.

tasks.title: Disambiguate 'should FAIL' labels in test_test_helpers.sh guard-detection fixtures
tasks.description:
In tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh, the four guard-detection fixtures at lines 402-405, 412-415, 424-427, and 439-442 use the phrasing `(should FAIL)` on the `check "..." "false"` branches. But `check` with `"false"` always records a FAIL in test output regardless — the phrasing reads as an instruction when it is actually a branch-taken annotation.

A reader scanning `FAIL: ... (should FAIL)` lines in CI output is likely to misread them as "expected-to-fail" tests rather than "this is the wrong-outcome branch I hope is never taken." The new `$marker` and `$_expr_ref_fn` historical-pin tests added in Task 1241 inherit and perpetuate this confusing convention.

Fix: rename the wrong-outcome branch labels to be self-explanatory, e.g.:
- `(wrong: helper said no guard, but guard is present)`
- `(wrong: helper reported guard in clean file)`

Apply consistently to all four guard-detection fixtures. Pre-existing stylistic issue, but since the Task 1241 diff is the last code that added new fixtures inheriting this convention, it's the right place to fix it.

Verification: `bash tests/infra/test_test_helpers.sh` still passes and CI output FAIL lines (if triggered) read unambiguously.

Flagged by reviewer_comprehensive on Task 1241.

tasks.title: Consolidate field-calculus test helpers and add dimension-guard symmetry
tasks.description:
Follow-up from Task 1230 review (esc-1230-1040). Four related suggestions on crates/reify-expr/tests/field_calculus_tests.rs:

S1 (code_duplication, lines 596-675): `curl_two_element_vector_sample_point_returns_undef` reintroduces ~55 lines of field/lambda/curl-expr construction that Task 1230's refactor had just eliminated. Extract a shared builder — e.g. `build_curl_rotation_field() -> (Value curl_result, Type curl_field_type)` — or let `run_curl_rotation_test` take an `ExpectedCurl { Vec3([f64;3]), Undef }` enum, and have both the happy-path helper and the new regression test call it.

S2 (test_coverage_symmetry, line 595): Only `curl` has a 2-element dimension-guard regression test. `compute_numerical_divergence_at_point` and `compute_numerical_laplacian_at_point` have the same `items.len() == 3` guard and were fixed in the same Task 1220 pass. Add `divergence_two_element_vector_sample_point_returns_undef` and `laplacian_two_element_vector_sample_point_returns_undef` using the corresponding run_*_test helper (ideally via the shared sampler helper from S1).

S3 (test_clarity, lines 651-663): The curl regression test declares the sample point literal's static type as `Type::vec2(Type::Real)` while the curl field's domain is `Type::point3(Type::Real)`. Runtime dispatch only inspects the `Value`, so it still exercises the dimension guard, but the static-type mismatch is subtle. Add a short inline comment above the `CompiledExpr::literal(point, point_literal_type)` call noting that `vec2` is intentional because the declared type here is not used by the curl sample handler — the guard matches on `Value` shape at runtime.

S4 (api_design, line 84): The three helpers take `(point: Value, point_literal_type: Type, label: &str)`, but `point_literal_type` is mechanically `Type::point3(Type::Real)` for `Value::Point` and `Type::vec3(Type::Real)` for `Value::Vector` at every call site. That coupling is implicit and easy to desynchronize. Either derive `point_literal_type` from the `Value` variant inside each helper, or replace the `(Value, Type)` pair with a small `SamplePoint::{Point([f64;3]), Vector([f64;3])}` enum so callers can't desync.

Verify: `cargo test -p reify-expr --test field_calculus_tests` passes after the refactor.

memory_hints: parent task 1220 + review esc-1230-1040; helper signatures live around lines 84, 354, 536, 730; dimension guards are in `compute_numerical_*_at_point` in crates/reify-expr/src/lib.rs around lines 1325-1720.

tasks.title: Harden run_tests isolation + clarify TestResult.diagnostics semantics
tasks.description:
Two related robustness improvements in `crates/reify-eval/src/tests_runner.rs` from Task 268 review:

S1 (lines 48-68): `build_isolated_module` only strips `t.constraints`, `guarded_groups.{constraints,else_constraints}`, and `t.objective` from non-test templates. It does NOT strip `CompiledPort.constraints` or connection-attached constraints (`compatibility_constraint`, `frame_constraint`). This is currently safe only because `Engine::collect_active_constraints()` (crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs ~line 2743) gathers exclusively from `template.constraints` + `template.guarded_groups`. The coupling is implicit and fragile — if the check pipeline ever extends to port/connection constraints, tests will silently pick up unrelated non-test failures.

Action: (a) add a doc comment in both `build_isolated_module` and `collect_active_constraints` pointing at each other as the shared invariant; and/or (b) defensively clear `t.ports[*].constraints` and any connection-embedded constraint IDs in the strip step.

S2 (lines 115-117): `TestResult.diagnostics` is populated from `check_result.diagnostics`, which includes `eval_result.diagnostics` computed over the entire isolated module. Any eval warning/error from an unrelated non-test template (e.g. a faulty default_expr) therefore leaks into a passing test's diagnostics. The docstring ("Diagnostics emitted by constraint checking during the test run") is misleading.

Action: pick one — either filter diagnostics to those originating from the target test template (and its sub-component closure), or update the docstring to state that diagnostics may include eval diagnostics from the whole isolated module. Lock the chosen behavior in with a dedicated test (faulty default_expr in a non-test fixture, passing test, assert leak-or-no-leak).

queries: resolution for: Harden run_tests isolation + clarify TestResult.diagnostics semantics

tasks.title: Optimize run_tests to avoid per-test full CompiledModule clone
tasks.description:
Performance suggestion from Task 268 review (crates/reify-eval/src/tests_runner.rs:78-93).

`run_tests` currently calls `module.clone()` once per test via `build_isolated_module`, then mutates template lists/constraints and constructs a fresh `Engine`. Cost is O(tests × module_size) just for the clones, plus fresh engine re-eval of everything each iteration. Fine for small test suites but will scale poorly as suite/module sizes grow.

Proposed approach: build a single shared "base" `CompiledModule` with all non-test templates pre-stripped once, then per-test replace just the one retained test template slot (or wrap in a lightweight borrowing newtype). Reduces per-test overhead from a full deep clone to one template swap.

Low priority — defer until there is evidence of a slow test suite. Confirm no behavioral change via the existing integration tests in crates/reify-eval/tests/test_runner.rs.

Additionally, integrate memory hints for execution context: entities include `CompiledModule` and `CompiledUnit`. Queries should focus on `CompiledModule` structure fields, units Vec `CompiledUnit`, and `run_tests` test execution performance clone optimization.
tasks.details: Integrate memory hints to optimize execution context for `CompiledModule` and `CompiledUnit`. Focus on reducing memory overhead during test execution by optimizing clone operations.
tasks.testStrategy: Use existing integration tests in crates/reify-eval/tests/test_runner.rs to confirm no behavioral changes. Monitor performance improvements with memory profiling tools to ensure optimization effectiveness.

queries: resolution for: Optimize run_tests to avoid per-test full CompiledModule clone

tasks.title: Fix tautological unit tests in tests_runner.rs
tasks.description:
Test quality cleanup from Task 268 review (crates/reify-eval/src/tests_runner.rs).

S4 (lines 267-279): `build_isolated_module_preserves_shared_infrastructure` asserts `isolated.X.len() == module.X.len()` for `functions`, `fields`, `type_aliases`, `enum_defs`, `trait_defs` — but the inline source only declares a `constraint def` and one `@test structure`. All of those collections are empty in both modules, so assertions reduce to `0 == 0` and prove nothing. Fix: use a source that actually populates each collection (a `fn`, a module-level `field`, a `type` alias, an `enum def`, a `trait def`), or split into per-field tests each with a minimal populating snippet.

S5 (lines 96-103): `test_status_variants_exist_and_are_distinct` is tautological — the variants are distinct by construction via `PartialEq` derive and separate discriminants. The test cannot fail unless a variant is deleted, in which case the file won't compile anyway. Fix: delete the test. The behavior-carrying `compute_status_*` tests already cover all three variants.

tasks.title: Rename tests_runner module to test_runner
tasks.description:
Naming consistency fix from Task 268 review.

The source module is `crates/reify-eval/src/tests_runner.rs` (plural) while the integration test file is `crates/reify-eval/tests/test_runner.rs` (singular), and the public items (`run_tests`, `TestResult`, `TestStatus`) use singular `test`. The `tests_runner` module name is inconsistent with both the integration test and the public API, and `tests` awkwardly shadows the common unit-test module convention.

Action:
1. Rename `crates/reify-eval/src/tests_runner.rs` → `crates/reify-eval/src/test_runner.rs`.
2. Update `pub mod tests_runner;` → `pub mod test_runner;` and any `pub use tests_runner::…` in `crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs`.
3. Run `cargo build -p reify-eval` and `cargo test -p reify-eval` to verify.

No external API changes — the re-exported symbols stay the same.

tasks.title: Strengthen run_tests_propagates_violation_diagnostics assertion
tasks.description:
Test quality improvement from Task 268 review (crates/reify-eval/tests/test_runner.rs:138-151).

`run_tests_propagates_violation_diagnostics` currently only asserts that `diagnostics` is non-empty when a test fails. Because diagnostics flow through `eval_result.diagnostics` over the whole isolated module, an unrelated eval warning could satisfy this assertion — so a regression that zeroed out constraint diagnostics but left an unrelated eval warning would still pass.

Action: assert that at least one diagnostic references the violated constraint — e.g. `id.entity == "TestA"` on the constraint result, or that a diagnostic mentions the `Positive` constraint id / text / label. Exact form depends on which Diagnostic fields are populated for constraint violations; inspect `reify_types::Diagnostic` structure.

Note: coordinate with the task that clarifies `TestResult.diagnostics` semantics (S2 follow-up) — if that task decides to filter diagnostics to target-template only, this assertion becomes trivially enforceable.

tasks.title: Add run_tests_simple convenience wrapper for stateless checkers
tasks.description:
Ergonomic suggestion from Task 268 review (crates/reify-eval/src/tests_runner.rs:78-93).

`run_tests` takes `make_checker: FnMut() -> Box<dyn ConstraintChecker>`, called once per test to produce a fresh checker. For stateless checkers (the common case), every call site writes `|| Box::new(SimpleConstraintChecker)` — seven test call sites in `crates/reify-eval/tests/test_runner.rs` already repeat this boilerplate.

Action: add a thin wrapper — `pub fn run_tests_simple(module: &CompiledModule) -> Vec<TestResult> { run_tests(module, || Box::new(SimpleConstraintChecker)) }` — gated on whether `reify-eval` is allowed to depend on `reify-constraints`. If the crate dependency direction forbids that, instead: document the closure idiom at the item level on `run_tests` and leave callers as-is.

Update all seven integration test call sites to use the new wrapper once added.

tasks.title: Fix Rust grammar qualifier order in sync_comments_test.sh fn-existence regex
tasks.description:
Follow-up from Task 1174 review (esc-1174-1043, S1). The fn-existence regex at tests/sync_comments_test.sh:39 hardcodes the alternation order `(unsafe)? (const)? (async)? fn`, but Rust's actual grammar order is `const? async? unsafe? fn`. Combinations like `const unsafe fn foo(...)` and `async unsafe fn foo(...)` are legal Rust but would silently fail to match the current regex.

Current regex:
```
^[[:space:]]*(pub(\([^)]*\))?[[:space:]]+)?(unsafe[[:space:]]+)?(const[[:space:]]+)?(async[[:space:]]+)?fn[[:space:]]+
```

Not currently a bug because the sanitize_value definitions in reify-expr/reify-stdlib don't use those combinations, but it's a latent false-negative that silently weakens the SYNC comment checker.

Fix options:
1. Reorder alternation groups to match Rust grammar: `(const)? (async)? (unsafe)? fn`.
2. Switch to an orderless match: `(pub(\([^)]*\))?|unsafe|const|async)([[:space:]]+(pub(\([^)]*\))?|unsafe|const|async))*[[:space:]]+fn`.

Option 2 is more robust but harder to read; option 1 is a minimal change. Pick one and apply consistently.

Required meta-test additions in tests/infra/test_sync_comments_grep.sh:
- accept: `const unsafe fn sanitize_value(`
- accept: `async unsafe fn sanitize_value(`

Verify with: `bash tests/infra/test_sync_comments_grep.sh`

Source: esc-1174-1043 (steward-triage from Task 1174 post-merge review)

tasks.title: Add pub+modifier accept tests and PATTERN extraction sanity check in test_sync_comments_grep.sh
tasks.description:
Follow-up from Task 1174 review (esc-1174-1043, S2 + S3). Two small meta-test improvements to tests/infra/test_sync_comments_grep.sh:

**S2 — Additional pub+modifier accept cases (after line 93):**
Task 1174 added `pub(crate) const fn` and `pub(crate) unsafe fn` accept assertions, but the equally important `pub(crate) async fn` and `pub(super) const fn` combinations are still untested. Add:
- `accepts: pub(crate) async fn sanitize_value(`
- `accepts: pub(super) const fn sanitize_value(`
These close the remaining symmetric holes in the visibility+modifier cross-product.

**S3 — PATTERN extraction sanity check (after line 44):**
The PATTERN extraction sed pipeline is coupled to the exact literal form `'"${ref_fn}"'` in sync_comments_test.sh. If the source file is ever refactored (e.g. different variable name, or unquoted `${ref_fn}`), the substitution `s/'"[^"]*"'/sanitize_value/` will silently fail to replace, leaving literal `${ref_fn}` in `$PATTERN` and producing a wall of confusing fixture-failure noise. The existing early-exit guard on line 40 only catches an empty PATTERN, not a malformed one.

Add after the existing `pattern extraction ... succeeded` assertion:
```bash
assert "extracted pattern contains expected fn name" \
    bash -c "[[ \"$PATTERN\" == *sanitize_value* ]]"
```

Verify with: `bash tests/infra/test_sync_comments_grep.sh`

Source: esc-1174-1043 (steward-triage from Task 1174 post-merge review)

tasks.title: Reconcile self.items vs bare items asymmetry in entity scope
tasks.description:
In crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs, the bare-identifier path (ExprKind::Ident, lines ~2229-2247) resolves a collection sub name like `items` to a List<T> value cell (`__list_{name}__{first_member}`), so `let x = items` is valid inside a structure. Task 1125 made the equivalent `self.items` (MemberAccess branch at lines ~2737-2750) an error telling users to switch to indexed access. Since `self.member` is otherwise equivalent to bare `member` in entity scope (see the step-11 self.param equivalence test), this inconsistency is surprising: a refactor from `items` to `self.items` will break with a message that tells users to change form entirely.

Decide and implement one of:
  (a) Make `self.items` resolve to the same List<T> value cell as bare `items` (mirror the bare-ident logic inside the `self.member` branch at ~line 2737: look up collection_sub_member_types[member], return a ValueRef to `__list_{member}__{first_member}` with type List<T>).
  (b) Also error on bare `items` so the rule is uniform, and document the rationale.

Add a test pinning whichever direction is chosen, and keep parity with the existing step-11 self.param equivalence test.

tasks.title: Polish error branches in self-collection-sub resolution
tasks.description:
Follow-up cleanup for the Task 1125 guards in crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs:

1. **Error-recovery type fallbacks (lines ~2749 and ~2829)** — both error branches return `CompiledExpr::literal(Value::Undef, Type::Real)`, which cascades type-mismatch diagnostics into downstream expressions expecting the collection element type or List<T>. Mirror the lookup already used at ~line 2860-2877:
   - For the nested `self.items.diameter` case: `scope.collection_sub_member_types.get(sub_name).and_then(|m| m.get(member)).cloned().unwrap_or(Type::Real)`.
   - For the direct `self.items` case: `Type::List(Box::new(Type::StructureRef(...)))` using the collection's declared element structure.

2. **Misleading error text for aggregation members (lines ~2818-2823)** — For `self.items.count` / `.sum` / `.keys` / `.values`, the current message reads `use items[i].count for a specific instance`, but these are aggregations, not per-instance fields. Special-case `member` ∈ {"count","sum","keys","values"}: recommend `{sub_name}.{member}` (drop the `self.`). Alternative: emit both options in the message.

3. **Deduplicate pattern matching (lines ~2776-2830)** — the new error branch re-destructures the same `MemberAccess { MemberAccess { Ident("self"), sub_name }, member }` pattern as the preceding success branch, and 2783 also has a redundant `!collection_sub_names.contains` guard alongside the separate collection-sub error branch. Collapse into a single if-let that branches internally: `if collection_sub_names.contains(sub_name) { emit error } else if sub_component_types.contains_key(sub_name) { resolve member }`. Apply the same simplification to the `self.member` branch at ~2723-2750.

4. **Comment consolidation (lines ~2720-2724)** — the pre-existing comment about "Check for self.sub.member chaining (handled at outer level)" now sits awkwardly with the added collection-sub guard comment. Rewrite as a single comment: `// self.sub — for single-instance subs only, return a StructureRef so outer chaining works. Collection subs are handled by the error branch below (use indexed access instead).`

Add/extend unit tests so diagnostics for `self.items.count` and `self.items` assert the new (more precise) fallback types propagate without cascading noise.
