tasks.details:
Scope:
1. Re-apply the two-pass pre-register split in `crates/reify-compiler/src/conformance.rs` — see reverted commit aa4ef1245 for the exact patch. Pass 1 registers every annotated default (Param + Let with `cell_type = Some(ty)`); Pass 2 compiles unannotated-let expressions against the fully-populated annotated scope and registers the inferred type. The remaining mutual-unannotated limitation stays documented by `mutual_unannotated_lets_documented_limitation`.

2. Add regression guard `unannotated_let_resolves_forward_reference_to_annotated_let` to `crates/reify-compiler/tests/let_type_disambiguation_tests.rs` — scenario: `let a = b + 1mm` followed by `let b : Length = 2mm` must compile without "unresolved name: b" diagnostic.

3. Update `cross_kind_pre_registration_order_sensitivity_reversed_order_produces_error` in `crates/reify-compiler/tests/trait_merge_tests.rs:1094` — flip the assertion from `!errors.is_empty()` (expects error) to clean compilation. Rename accordingly (e.g. drop `_produces_error` suffix, rename to reflect that cross-kind order-sensitivity is resolved when one trait provides annotated Param and another provides unannotated Let). Update the accompanying doc comment that describes this as a "known design limitation" — it is now a resolved limitation. The test was introduced in commit adbb43007 as an intentional document-via-test of the single-pass scope ordering.

Verify: `cargo test -p reify-compiler` green; `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` clean; `cargo fmt --check`.

Origin: reviewer_comprehensive suggestion 1 (behavior_regression) on task 1834 amendment round 2. Reverted in ccb87a247 due to scope rule (trait_merge_tests.rs outside 1834's locked modules). Implementation was already validated on task 1834's worktree before revert.

tasks.title: Tessellation diagnostics: eliminate source clone, derive Severity serialize, align log format
tasks.description: Post-merge polish for the tessellation-diagnostics wiring added in task 1764. Covers performance, API-contract consistency, and doc clarification in engine.rs and diff.rs.
tasks.details:
From reviewer_comprehensive suggestions on task 1764:

1. **Avoid cloning source on every diagnostic-producing tessellation** (engine.rs:433-438).
   - `resolve_source()` result is currently `.map(|(f, s)| (f.to_owned(), s.to_owned()))`, cloning the entire source file each time diagnostics are produced.
   - `diagnostics_to_info` only needs `&str`. Rework to keep borrows, only allocating the fallback `("<unknown>", "")` strings when `resolve_source()` returns `None`.
   - Suggested form: `let (file_path, source): (&str, &str) = self.resolve_source().unwrap_or(("<unknown>", "")); let unresolved = file_path == "<unknown>";`

2. **Derive Serialize on Severity to centralize PascalCase formatting** (engine.rs:1370-1383, diagnostics_to_info).
   - The change from lowercase (`Severity`'s Display impl) to PascalCase (hand-rolled match in `diagnostics_to_info`) is an observable wire-format break for MCP `get_diagnostics` consumers.
   - Preferred: derive `Serialize` on `Severity` with `#[serde(rename_all = "PascalCase")]` (or equivalent) and use that everywhere instead of duplicating the mapping. Keeps `DiagnosticInfo.severity` string and other serialized Severity sites in lock-step.
   - Alternative (if derive is infeasible): add a release/migration note documenting the PascalCase change.

3. **Align log and wire format for diagnostic severity** (engine.rs:427).
   - `warn!(severity = ?diag.severity, ...)` uses Debug format while the emitted wire `DiagnosticInfo.severity` uses the PascalCase match. They happen to look similar today but will drift.
   - Use `%diag.severity` (Display) or the same mapping used by `diagnostics_to_info` so log and wire formats stay pinned together.

4. **Clarify StateDelta::full tessellation_diagnostics asymmetry comment** (diff.rs:33-49).
   - Current comment (line ~41) only explains why the initial full-emission collapses empty→None. It does not mention that `diff_gui_state` still emits `Some(vec![])` when clearing a previously non-empty list.
   - Expand comment to: "Initial full emission: skip when empty (the frontend default is already []). Subsequent diffs (diff_gui_state) still emit Some(vec![]) when clearing a previously non-empty list so subscribers can clear their view."

Verification: run `cargo test -p gui` (or the workspace equivalent) to ensure MCP and GUI tests still pass; also spot-check any serde tests that cover Severity/DiagnosticInfo serialization.

queries: resolution for: Tessellation diagnostics: eliminate source clone, derive Severity serialize, align log format

done_provenance.note: All four reviewer_comprehensive items already implemented and merged to main prior to task being set in-progress. Implementing commits (all ancestors of task/1908 HEAD bf30b9d45 and on main): 129270919 (eliminate source clone, engine.rs:433-446), 70328125d (derive Serialize on Severity + as_wire_str, diagnostics.rs:98-132), 31276b042 (align log+wire via as_wire_str, engine.rs:427 and diagnostics_to_info), 1ac468aa3 (expand StateDelta::full comment, diff.rs:41-43). Pinning tests: cfb6d6073 (unresolved-source fallback), 824a4f5aa (non-empty->empty clearing), and inline get_diagnostics_severity_strings_match_as_wire_str in 31276b042. No code changes needed on branch task/1908.

tasks.title: Tessellation diagnostics: add tests for unresolved-source tag and frontend clear path
tasks.description: Post-merge test-coverage gaps for the tessellation-diagnostics wiring added in task 1764. Two missing tests, one backend and one frontend.
tasks.details:
From reviewer_comprehensive suggestions on task 1764:

1. **Backend: exercise the `code = "unresolved-source"` branch** (engine.rs:440-446).
   - `build_gui_state_captures_tessellation_errors_from_failing_kernel` only covers the resolved-source path because `load_from_source` populates the source map.
   - Add a Rust test that loads a session, uses a `break_source_map_for_test` (or equivalent `break_*_for_test`) helper to invalidate `resolve_source`, triggers tessellation via a failing kernel, and asserts that every produced `DiagnosticInfo` has `code == Some("unresolved-source")` and `file_path == "<unknown>"`.

2. **Frontend: test the diagnostics-clear path in App.tsx end-to-end wiring** (gui/src/__tests__/App.test.tsx:2453-2489).
   - Existing test only covers Error → StatusBar transition.
   - Add a second `it(...)` that fires a diagnostics event with one Error, awaits the badge, then fires an event with `[]` and asserts the badge is gone and the status text reverts to "No geometry" (not "Compile error").
   - This locks in the backend's deliberate `Some(vec![])`-on-clear wire contract at the consumer end.

Verification: `cargo test -p gui` for (1); `npm test` / `vitest` in `gui/` for (2). No production-code changes expected.

queries: resolution for: Tessellation diagnostics: add tests for unresolved-source tag and frontend clear path

tasks.title: Replace duplicated find_node test helper with CompiledExpr::walk & strengthen anti-cascade assertions
tasks.description: In crates/reify-compiler/tests/type_error_propagation_tests.rs, the local `find_node` helper (lines 14-43) re-implements a recursive walk over CompiledExprKind, duplicating the canonical `CompiledExpr::walk` (crates/reify-types/src/expr.rs:255). The duplicated traversal is incomplete (omits ListLiteral, SetLiteral, MapLiteral, Quantifier, Match, OptionSome, RangeConstructor, AdHocSelector, Lambda, UserFunctionCall), so new variants silently break the test (found.is_some() fails). Refactor to collect nodes via `CompiledExpr::walk` then `.find(pred)`. Additionally, the relaxed integration test (lines 158-186) keys on substring `mismatch`/`incompatible` for cascade detection but misses canonical cascade phrases like 'no matching overload', 'expected ... got ...', 'cannot apply'. Either reinstate `errors.len() == 1` exact-count assertion or expand the negative substring list. (Review followup from task 448; bundles S1+S3.)

queries: resolution for: Replace duplicated find_node test helper with CompiledExpr::walk & strengthen anti-cascade assertions

tasks.title: Extend Type::Error anti-cascade contract to UserFunctionCall overload resolution
tasks.description: In crates/reify-compiler/src/expr.rs (UserFunctionCall path, lines ~508-578), `resolve_function_overload` uses exact `param_ty == arg_ty` matching, so a Type::Error-typed argument never matches and falls into `OverloadResolution::NoMatch`, emitting a 'no matching overload for f(<error>, ...)' diagnostic on top of the root-cause error — exactly the cascade behavior task-448 targets. Additionally, the NoMatch / Ambiguous arms still fall back to Type::Real, so enclosing arithmetic re-cascades. Options: (a) short-circuit `resolve_function_overload` (or call site) when any arg is Type::Error, returning a Type::Error-typed UserFunctionCall stub without emitting the no-overload diagnostic; add regression test `let bad = my_fn(self.unsupported)` asserting only the root-cause error is reported; or (b) explicitly document this gap as out-of-scope. (Review followup from task 448; suggestion S2.)

queries: resolution for: Extend Type::Error anti-cascade contract to UserFunctionCall overload resolution

tasks.title: Add unit tests pinning Type::Error wildcard contract in type_compat
tasks.description: In crates/reify-compiler/src/type_compat.rs (lines 9-11 and 94-96), `implicitly_converts_to(Error, X) == true` and `type_compatible(Error, X) == true` make Type::Error a universal wildcard at every consumer (trait conformance, field composition, function-arg checks). This is correct for diagnostics suppression but is not pinned by any test, so a regression removing the wildcard behavior would not surface. Add direct unit tests (in type_compat.rs unit tests or implicit_conversion_tests.rs) asserting: (a) `implicitly_converts_to(Error, Real) == true`, (b) `implicitly_converts_to(Real, Error) == true`, (c) `type_compatible(Error, X) == true` for multiple X (Real, Int, Bool, List, Option, etc.). (Review followup from task 448; suggestion S4.)

queries: resolution for: Add unit tests pinning Type::Error wildcard contract in type_compat

tasks.title: Handle nested Type::Error in is_error or document top-level-only boundary
tasks.description: In crates/reify-types/src/ty.rs (lines 208-214), `is_error()` only matches the top-level `Type::Error` sentinel. Compound types like `Type::List(Box<Type::Error>)`, `Type::Option(Box<Type::Error>)`, or `Type::Map(_, Box<Type::Error>)` are not recognized as poisoned, so e.g. `[self.unsupported, 1.0][0] + 5.0` may still cascade because the list literal's inferred element type is Type::Error but the list type itself is `List<Error>` — the IndexAccess guard at expr.rs:1157 only checks `compiled_obj.result_type.is_error()`. Options: (a) add `contains_error()` recursive check and use at consumer sites, or (b) document top-level-only boundary on `is_error` and add a regression test asserting current nested-error cascade behavior so the next person knows where the line is. (Review followup from task 448; suggestion S6.)

tasks.title: Conformance let-default cache: error-cascade suppression, explicit kind-keyed cache, shared advertised-type helper
tasks.description:
Three design-hardening follow-ups for the `inferred_let_exprs` cache and `available_defaults` map added in task 1834's conformance.rs pre-register pass. All three were surfaced by reviewer_comprehensive on task 1834 amendment round 3 (esc-1834-144) but deferred as non-urgent hardening rather than task 1834 deliverables.

Memory hints: search "task 1834 inferred_let_exprs cache available_defaults AvailableDefaultKind"; entities: DefaultKind, AvailableDefaultKind, Type, compile_expr.
tasks.details:
Three independent changes in `crates/reify-compiler/src/conformance.rs`. Each is small; bundling avoids repeated touch-passes on the same code region.

1. **Error-cascade suppression (robustness)** — conformance.rs:~236-246
   When `compile_expr` fails in the unannotated-let pre-register path (e.g. forward reference to a name not yet registered), `result_type` falls back to `Type::Real` (compile_expr error-recovery type). That `Type::Real` then flows into `inferred_let_exprs`, `available_defaults`, and the injected cell_type — causing a secondary `type mismatch for trait member … available default has Real` diagnostic whose wording leaks an implementation detail instead of the real cause (unresolved identifier).
   Fix: Track a per-default compile-error bit (snapshot `diagnostics.len()` before/after `compile_expr`); when it trips, either skip inserting into `inferred_let_exprs` / return `None` from the `available_defaults` filter_map for that name, OR prefer a "missing required member" diagnostic over "available default has Real". Goal: a single unresolved-identifier error, not a cascade.
   Add regression test: `unannotated_let_with_unresolved_ref_does_not_cascade_type_mismatch` in let_type_disambiguation_tests.rs — input like `trait HasA { let a = b + 1mm } structure S : HasA {}` must produce the unresolved-name diagnostic only, NOT a secondary "available default has Real" type-mismatch.

2. **Kind-keyed cache (architecture)** — conformance.rs:~167-208
   The cache's name-only key (`HashMap<String, CompiledExpr>`) is safe today under three documented invariants (no cross-kind writes; kind-guarded reads; `collect_all_requirements` dedup). Two of those invariants are enforced in a different function 100+ lines away. The symmetric `(String, AvailableDefaultKind)` key already exists on the `available_defaults` map ~70 lines below. Changing the cache key to match eliminates the cross-module invariant dependency at near-zero cost.
   Fix: `HashMap<(String, AvailableDefaultKind), CompiledExpr>`, keyed on `(name.clone(), AvailableDefaultKind::Let)` at insert/read. Delete invariant #1 and #2 from the doc comment; keep #3 (dedup) since that is still load-bearing for the no-double-compile guarantee. Remove the `If any of these ever drift, key the cache on (String, AvailableDefaultKind) for symmetry` note.

3. **Shared advertised-type helper (code_reuse)** — conformance.rs:~293-299 and ~536-538
   Two sites compute "annotation wins, else inferred" for a Let default's advertised type, with slightly different formulations:
   - `available_defaults` builder: `cell_type.clone().unwrap_or_else(|| inferred_let_exprs.get(name).map(|e| e.result_type.clone()).unwrap_or(Type::Real))`
   - injection loop: `cell_type.clone().unwrap_or_else(|| compiled_expr.result_type.clone())`
   Fix: Extract a small helper (module-private fn or closure) like `resolve_let_advertised_type(cell_type: &Option<Type>, inferred: Option<&CompiledExpr>) -> Type` that both sites call, centralizing the "Real fallback on inference failure" rule. Note: suggestion #1 above may remove the `Type::Real` safety net entirely (by dropping failed entries from the cache) — coordinate design order: if #1 lands first, the helper's fallback arm disappears.

Verify: `cargo test -p reify-compiler` green; `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` clean; `cargo fmt --check`.

Origin: esc-1834-144 triage. Reviewer comments captured in that escalation detail. Task 1834 is complete as-is; this follow-up is pure design hardening.

tasks.title: Fix Taskmaster/auto-commit pipeline to emit string task IDs in tasks.json
tasks.description:
The auto-commit pipeline that writes .taskmaster/tasks/tasks.json is emitting JSON numeric IDs for both top-level tasks and subtasks, but tests/infra/test_tasks_json_id_types.sh (added by task 1887) requires JSON string IDs. This breaks post-rebase verify for every task rebased onto current main, causing repeated retry loops (e.g. esc-1754-139, esc-1754-143, esc-1754-157 all failed with identical "Failures: tests failed" for this reason, with the failing branch not touching tasks.json at all). Root cause is in the task-write path: either the Taskmaster CLI, the TaskInterceptor, or the chore(tasks) auto-commit script is serializing numeric IDs. Fix at the write path so IDs are always stringified, not a one-shot file rewrite (which the pipeline would regress on next write). Verify by running `bash tests/infra/test_tasks_json_id_types.sh` after any task-status change — it must pass.

memory_hints: search "taskmaster auto-commit pipeline string IDs", search "test_tasks_json_id_types task 1887"

tasks.title: Auto-install hooks/post-commit and improve normalizer robustness
tasks.description: Follow-up from task 1915 review (esc-1915-178). The post-commit hook only fires when developers have `git config core.hooksPath hooks` set, which is not auto-installed by setup-dev.sh nor documented in CLAUDE.md. Without this, fresh clones bypass the normalizer entirely and depend on test_tasks_json_id_types.sh as the only safety net. Also: the hook silently swallows python3-missing or normalizer crashes (returns 0 with stderr warning), so a broken normalize step surfaces only via the post-rebase verify gate. Improve robustness with defensive isinstance checks in scripts/normalize_tasks_json.py walker (currently crashes on AttributeError if a future tag namespace stores tasks/subtasks as a non-list).
tasks.details:
Three things bundled here because they all concern the post-commit hook actually doing its job in the wild:

1. (suggestion 1) Update scripts/setup-dev.sh to run `git config core.hooksPath hooks` (or symlink into .git/hooks). Add a sentence in CLAUDE.md under "Local Dev Setup" noting the hook convention.

2. (suggestion 2) Make python3-missing a louder failure in hooks/post-commit:41-44. Either fail-loud (post-commit allows non-zero) or write a marker like .git/normalize-failed so it shows up at next `git status`.

3. (suggestion 4) Add `if not isinstance(tasks_list, list): continue` guards in scripts/normalize_tasks_json.py:36-43 around the tasks_list and subtasks iterations.

memory_hints: search "hooks/post-commit normalize tasks.json", search "task 1915 setup-dev hooks"

tasks.title: Extend post-commit hook tests: merge commits and python3-fail stub
tasks.description: Follow-up from task 1915 review (esc-1915-178, suggestion 6). hooks/post-commit explicitly handles --root and -m (merge commits) and explicitly bails out on python3 failure, but tests/infra/test_post_commit_hook.sh exercises none of those paths. A regression that drops `-m` would silently miss merges that introduce numeric IDs — exactly the silent-pass class the surrounding tests are written to defend against.
tasks.details:
Add two checks to tests/infra/test_post_commit_hook.sh:

1. MERGE COMMIT check: create two branches in a fixture repo that both touch tasks.json with numeric IDs, merge them, verify the merge commit's subtask IDs were normalized.

2. PYTHON3-FAIL check: swap in a normalize_tasks_json.py stub that exits 1 (e.g., overwrite the copied script in the fixture). Make a commit that touches tasks.json. Assert the commit succeeded (post-commit hook returned 0 from python3 failure path) and the warning was emitted to stderr.

memory_hints: search "test_post_commit_hook.sh", search "task 1915 hooks post-commit"

queries: resolution for: Extend post-commit hook tests: merge commits and python3-fail stub

tasks.title: Revisit Type::Error wildcard asymmetry in type_compat (producer-side only?)
tasks.description: In crates/reify-compiler/src/type_compat.rs, both `implicitly_converts_to(from, to)` and `type_compatible(param_ty, arg_ty)` short-circuit to `true` when EITHER side is Type::Error. The producer-side guard (`from.is_error()` / `arg_ty.is_error()`) is the anti-cascade path: a poisoned expression feeds into a consumer and must not trigger a second diagnostic. The mirror direction (`to.is_error()` / `param_ty.is_error()`) is conceptually dubious — no legitimate call site should ever pass Type::Error as the *expected* type, so silently returning `true` can mask a bug where Type::Error leaks into a parameter position (e.g. registry lookup returning Error where a concrete type was expected). Evaluate whether the mirror direction can be tightened to only short-circuit on the producer side, with a `debug_assert` (or a `cfg(debug_assertions)` panic) on any call site that legitimately passes Type::Error as `to`/`param_ty`. If the mirror short-circuit stays, document which consumers depend on it. Review followup from task 1912; suggestion S2.

tasks.title: Clarify non-discriminating Type::Error identity-covered pinning test
tasks.description: In crates/reify-compiler/tests/implicit_conversion_tests.rs at line 816, `type_compatible_error_wildcard_error_error` asserts `type_compatible(Type::Error, Type::Error) == true`. This case is also satisfied by the identity arm (`param_ty == arg_ty`), so it does NOT discriminate between guard-present and guard-removed — a future refactor could delete the wildcard guard and this test would still pass. The sibling test `error_wildcard_implicit_error_to_error` (line ~617) was fixed in commit 2b2be6f0b to include an explanatory comment making its non-pinning status explicit; apply the same annotation (or delete the test outright) to `type_compatible_error_wildcard_error_error`. The actual guard-pinning tests for this path are `type_compatible_error_wildcard_real` and `type_compatible_error_wildcard_mirror_real`. Review followup from task 1912; suggestion S3.

tasks.title: Make find_node helper exhaustive over CompiledExprKind in type_error_propagation_tests
tasks.description: In crates/reify-compiler/tests/type_error_propagation_tests.rs (lines 17-42), the `find_node` helper walks `CompiledExprKind` recursively but falls through to `_ => None` for many variants (Quantifier, ListLiteral, StructLiteral, tuple-like nodes, etc.). Current callers only search predicate subtrees so this is not a correctness bug today, but it is a maintainability trap: if a future regression produces a nested `Type::Error` inside an unhandled variant, `find_node` would silently return `None` and turn a real regression into a green test. Either (a) enumerate all CompiledExprKind variants exhaustively (no `_ => None`) so the compiler nags when new variants are added, or (b) panic/log in `cfg(test)` on unhandled kinds so silent miss is impossible. Review followup from task 1912; suggestion S4.

tasks.title: Audit expr.rs error-producing branches for consistent Type::Error sentinel policy
tasks.description: In crates/reify-compiler/src/expr.rs, the "unknown member on self" branch (~lines 731-737) now returns `CompiledExpr::literal(Value::Undef, Type::Error)` to activate the anti-cascade guard (task-448 / task-1912). However, other diagnostic-producing sites in `compile_expr_guarded` may still return `CompiledExpr::literal(Value::Undef, Type::Real)` alongside a `diagnostics.push(Diagnostic::error(...))`. Asymmetric producer coverage means cascades will still fire whenever those other producers run. Action: grep `crates/reify-compiler/src/expr.rs` for `Value::Undef, Type::Real` pairs adjacent to `Diagnostic::error` pushes, and either (a) convert each producer to emit `Type::Error`, or (b) document in the module header which producers intentionally keep `Type::Real` and why. Related: task 448 (parent anti-cascade work), task 936 (trait conformance sentinel). Review followup from task 1912; suggestion S5.

queries: resolution for: Audit expr.rs error-producing branches for consistent Type::Error sentinel policy

done_provenance.note: Squash-rebased after stale-base block; merged via merge queue.

tasks.title: Add Type::Error defensive arms in reify-eval value_type_kind_matches predicates
tasks.description: In crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs (~line 101-143, `value_type_kind_matches`), every value/type predicate (e.g. `Value::Real(_) => matches!(ty, Type::Real | Type::Int)`) enumerates concrete `Type` variants but never accepts `Type::Error`. Since the Type::Error sentinel now propagates further into compiled expressions (task-448 / task-1912), a CompiledExpr carrying `Type::Error` could reach evaluation — particularly in incremental/LSP scenarios where the evaluator may proceed despite compiler errors. In that case, every value/type predicate would report `false` and downstream logic may surface spurious TypeKindMismatch diagnostics or panic. Either: (a) add an explicit `Type::Error` arm in each predicate (likely returning `true` as "don't second-guess a poisoned type"), or (b) guard the evaluator to refuse evaluation of any module whose diagnostics contain errors before walking expressions, and document that contract. Related: task 1867 (value_type_kind_matches TypeParam/StructureRef/Geometry verification), task 1893 (Bool arm unit tests), task 1913 (nested is_error). Review followup from task 1912; suggestion S6.

tasks.title: Add Type::Error defensive arms in reify-eval value_type_kind_matches predicates
tasks.description: In crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs (~line 101-143, `value_type_kind_matches`), every value/type predicate (e.g. `Value::Real(_) => matches!(ty, Type::Real | Type::Int)`) enumerates concrete `Type` variants but never accepts `Type::Error`. Since the Type::Error sentinel now propagates further into compiled expressions (task-448 / task-1912), a CompiledExpr carrying `Type::Error` could reach evaluation — particularly in incremental/LSP scenarios where the evaluator may proceed despite compiler errors. In that case, every value/type predicate would report `false` and downstream logic may surface spurious TypeKindMismatch diagnostics or panic. Either: (a) add an explicit `Type::Error` arm in each predicate (likely returning `true` as "don't second-guess a poisoned type"), or (b) guard the evaluator to refuse evaluation of any module whose diagnostics contain errors before walking expressions, and document that contract. Related: task 1867 (value_type_kind_matches TypeParam/StructureRef/Geometry verification), task 1893 (Bool arm unit tests), task 1913 (nested is_error). Review followup from task 1912; suggestion S6.

tasks.title: Add Type::Error defensive arm in reify-eval value_type_kind_matches
tasks.description: In crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs (~line 101-143, `value_type_kind_matches`), every value/type predicate (e.g. `Value::Real(_) => matches!(ty, Type::Real | Type::Int)`) enumerates concrete Type variants but never accepts Type::Error. Since Type::Error now propagates further into compiled expressions (task-448, task-1912), a CompiledExpr carrying Type::Error could reach evaluation during incremental/LSP scenarios and produce spurious TypeKindMismatch diagnostics or panics. Either add an explicit Type::Error arm in each predicate (returning true as "don't second-guess a poisoned type"), or guard the evaluator to refuse evaluation when diagnostics contain errors, and document the contract. Related: tasks 1867, 1893, 1913. Review followup from task 1912 (S6).

done_provenance.note: Task 1924 premise already satisfied on base commit 8e932317a. The Type::Error defensive arm in value_type_kind_matches (crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs:109-114) was landed under task-1922 via commits f51e51ff0 (failing tests) and a8f5cfcc7 (guard). Docstring at lines 102-108 cross-references reify_compiler::type_compat and task-448/task-1922. Tests include 4 positive cases (Real/Bool/List/Undef vs Type::Error) plus a negative lock (Bool vs Int). Duplicate of task 1922.

tasks.title: Tighten or remove the source-comment regression guard in viewStateStore.test.ts
tasks.description:
The `viewStateStore — source comments` test in gui/src/__tests__/viewStateStore.test.ts (lines 710-726) asserts on the *text* of a code comment rather than any runtime behavior. It has two reviewer-flagged problems:

1. **Typography-only assertion.** The regex `/-{5,}\n\s*\/\/ Mutations\n\s*\/\/ -{5,}/` matches the decorative divider header only; the test still passes even if the mutation functions below it are deleted. It will also spuriously fail if anyone re-styles section headers. Options:
   - Remove the test (trust code review / the commit that added the neutral header).
   - Convert it to an ESLint custom rule (behaves like a lint rule, not a unit test).
   - Anchor the regex to the actual function definition, e.g. `/-{5,}\n\s*\/\/ Mutations\n\s*\/\/ -{5,}\n\s*\n\s*function setVisibility\(/` so a deletion of the mutations block breaks the guard.

2. **Top-level `readFileSync` breaks suite isolation.** `const src = readFileSync(SRC_PATH, 'utf-8');` runs at module load time. If viewStateStore.ts is renamed/moved, the *entire* test file fails to load and every other test in the suite becomes unreported instead of producing a single localized failure on the guard test. Move `SRC_PATH` and `readFileSync` inside the `it(...)` callback so only this one test fails.

Pick option 1.3 (anchor to function) + move readFileSync inside `it`, OR delete the test entirely and accept code review as the guard. Source: review of task 1902 (esc-1902-177).

queries: resolution for: Tighten or remove the source-comment regression guard in viewStateStore.test.ts

tasks.title: Make editor-perf wall-clock guard robust without weakening the threshold
tasks.description:
gui/src/__tests__/editor-perf.test.tsx lines 118-147 — the `Editor wall-clock latency` test had its budget raised from 500ms to 2000ms (4×) to stabilize it under concurrent suite load (48-task parallelism). As-written the comment concedes catastrophic regressions are "hundreds of ms each"; a ~10ms/keystroke per-hot-path regression (firmly into perceptibly laggy territory) would slip through silently.

Reviewer-suggested fixes that keep the guard meaningful:
- **(a) Serialize this test.** Move it to its own file run with `--pool=forks --poolOptions.forks.singleFork`, or put it in a `describe.sequential` block, or tag with Vitest's serial pool config. Then restore a tight 500–800ms budget.
- **(b) Switch to median-per-keystroke.** Record each `view.dispatch(...)` cost, assert `median(perKeystroke) < 15ms`. Medians are robust to tail latency spikes from parallel suites, so the threshold can stay tight without flakes.

Either approach is acceptable; the current 2000ms total budget is not. Source: review of task 1902 (esc-1902-177).

tasks.title: Tighten geometry dispatch registry cross-check: reduce comment duplication and close reverse-direction gap
tasks.description:
Two review suggestions from Task 1821 on `crates/reify-compiler/src/geometry.rs` (the `all_dispatch_functions_accounted_for` test block around lines 603-755):

1. **Reduce breakdown-comment duplication (lines 657-665).** The doc comment on `EXPECTED_DISPATCH_COUNT` re-enumerates every function name already in `GEOM_ARG_FUNCTIONS` / `NO_GEOM_ARG_FUNCTIONS` / boolean_ops / loft, creating a fourth place to keep in sync. The maintenance rule only mentions updating one list and the constant, so the parenthetical names will silently drift. Fix: drop the parenthetical enumerations and keep only per-category counts (e.g. `- GEOM_ARG_FUNCTIONS   14`), or delete the breakdown entirely — the lists are the single source of truth.

2. **Close the reverse-direction gap (lines 748-754).** The revised assertion still only cross-validates the lists against themselves plus a hand-maintained constant. Nothing touches the actual match arms in `compile_geometry_call`, so an arm added there without a corresponding list entry remains undetected — which is the failure mode the module-level comment (lines 603-609) and the test's doc (lines 714-718) explicitly motivate catching. Fix: add a companion test that scans the source of `compile_geometry_call` via `include_str!("geometry.rs")` plus a simple regex over `"name" =>` patterns in the match block, then cross-checks the extracted dispatch set against the four lists. This closes the gap without a procedural macro.

Both suggestions come from reviewer_comprehensive on Task 1821.

tasks.title: Log release-mode warning on duplicate template names in get_entity_tree
tasks.description:
In gui/src-tauri/src/engine.rs `get_entity_tree` (around lines 496-504), the template-name uniqueness invariant is currently enforced only via `debug_assert!`. In release builds, duplicates silently fall back to first-match behavior via `build_sub_node`'s lookup, which could produce silently inconsistent entity trees if a future refactor (macros, synthetic templates, module merging) breaks the compiler's well-formed-module guarantee.

Add a release-mode `tracing::warn!` (log-and-skip / log-and-continue) on first duplicate detection so the misbehaviour surfaces in logs rather than producing silently wrong trees. Keep the existing `debug_assert!` as well, or replace it with the runtime check.

Source: reviewer_comprehensive on task 1783 (suggestion, optional graceful-degradation hardening).

tasks.title: Short-circuit prelude sentinel in offset_to_line_col_fast + doc accuracy
tasks.description:
Follow-up from task 765 review.

Problem: `gui/src-tauri/src/engine.rs:1413` `offset_to_line_col_fast` does NOT short-circuit the `SourceSpan::prelude()` sentinel (offset == u32::MAX). It's the first-label renderer for every diagnostic (engine.rs:1360–1372). For cross-prelude collision warnings the first/only label IS the prelude sentinel, so this path computes `line = line_offsets.len() + 1` (past-last-line) — not an EOF position as `SourceSpan::prelude`'s rustdoc currently claims (`crates/reify-types/src/diagnostics.rs:48-51`). `byte_offset_to_line_col` was fixed in the task-765 diff but the fast variant was not, breaking the "two convergent implementations agree" invariant (see `engine_tests.rs:1386 matches_original_every_offset`).

Fix (preferred): Add the same early return at the top of `offset_to_line_col_fast`, e.g.
```
if offset == u32::MAX as usize { return (1, 1); }
```
and extend the cross-validation test so the sentinel is included in the "two functions agree" invariant.

Also update the rustdoc on `SourceSpan::prelude` (`crates/reify-types/src/diagnostics.rs:48-51`) to match actual behaviour once the fast path short-circuits — both paths now return (1,1).

Alternative (not recommended): short-circuit in the caller at `engine.rs:1364` via `label.span.is_prelude()`. This duplicates logic and diverges from the library-level contract.

Acceptance:
- `offset_to_line_col_fast(..., u32::MAX as usize)` returns (1, 1).
- Cross-validation test covers the sentinel offset.
- Rustdoc on `SourceSpan::prelude` describes the actual behaviour of GUI/LSP renderers.

tasks.title: Guard cross-prelude collision warning against intra-module dups
tasks.description:
Follow-up from task 765 review.

Problem: The cross-prelude collision check at `crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs:278-292` fires per-unit using `unit_registry.lookup`. If a single (malformed) prelude module contained two `pub unit foo` declarations, the warning would read `declared in both 'mod_a' and 'mod_a'` — nonsense. In practice `compile()` rejects intra-module duplicates earlier with proper spans, but the diagnostic is still incorrect when it does fire.

Fix: Guard the warning emission with
```
if existing.source_module.as_deref() != Some(&module_display[..]) { ... }
```
so only genuine cross-module collisions emit this warning. Intra-module dups have been (or should have been) reported at module-compile time.

Acceptance:
- Add a regression test where a single prelude module declares the same pub unit name twice; assert no `declared in both 'X' and 'X'` warning is emitted from `compile_with_prelude`'s seeding loop.
- Existing cross-module collision tests continue to pass.

tasks.title: Prelude collision tests: extract helper + pin single-collision count
tasks.description:
Follow-up from task 765 review. Two small test-quality improvements in `crates/reify-compiler/tests/unit_registry_tests.rs`:

1. **De-duplicate boilerplate** (lines 1722-1932): `prelude_module_unit_collision_emits_warning` and `three_prelude_collision_emits_two_chained_warnings` share ~20 lines of boilerplate per prelude module (parse / assert no parse errors / compile / assert no compile errors). Extract a helper like
   ```rust
   fn prelude_module(name: &str, factor: f64) -> CompiledModule { ... }
   ```
   either in the test file or in `tests/common/mod.rs` (matches the style of existing `common::` helpers), and reuse it across both tests.

2. **Pin the count in the single-collision case** (lines 1775-1785): `prelude_module_unit_collision_emits_warning` currently asserts that *some* warning mentions 'foo', but never pins the *count*. A future refactor that accidentally duplicates warning emission (double-push) would still pass. The three-prelude test already pins the count to 2. Add:
   ```rust
   assert_eq!(
       warnings.iter().filter(|w| w.message.contains("foo")).count(),
       1,
       "expected exactly 1 collision warning, got: {:?}", warnings
   );
   ```

Acceptance: both tests still pass, boilerplate reduced, single-collision count is pinned.

tasks.title: Consolidate overlapping Editor debounce tests
tasks.description: The new 'Editor debounce constant' describe block in gui/src/__tests__/Editor.test.tsx:71-89 overlaps with the existing 'calls bridge.updateSource after 300ms debounce' test at line 110 — both dispatch an edit and assert updateSource fires after the debounce. The unique value of the new test is the (EDITOR_DEBOUNCE_MS-1)ms negative-boundary assertion. Fold the negative-boundary assertion into the existing test and remove the duplicate describe block, OR keep the new test and delete/retitle the older one. Also update the older test's title and inline '// After 300ms debounce' comment to reference EDITOR_DEBOUNCE_MS for consistency.

tasks.title: Strengthen make_poison_literal anti-cascade enforcement
tasks.description:
Follow-up to task 448 review. Two related improvements to the Type::Error poison helper in crates/reify-compiler/src/expr.rs:

1) The current `make_poison_literal` debug_assert (lines 21-32) only checks that SOME Severity::Error exists in `diagnostics` — buffer-wide, not call-site-scoped. A forgotten `diagnostics.push(...)` at a new poison producer still passes in debug mode whenever any earlier compile step already emitted an error. Either (a) change the API so callers pass `len_before` and assert `diagnostics[len_before..].iter().any(|d| d.severity == Error)`, or (b) simplify to a plain constructor and drop the misleading invariant claim.

2) Several sibling error-producing arms in MemberAccess/port/meta still emit `CompiledExpr::literal(Value::Undef, Type::Real)` (or Type::String) after pushing a Severity::Error — specifically lines ~867 (unknown member on sub), ~890 (port has no member), ~918 (unknown member on collection sub), ~931 (invalid collection index), ~983 (no meta block), ~992 (unknown meta key). These are exactly the producer sites the new Type::Error contract is meant to cover. Route them through `make_poison_literal(diagnostics)` (or equivalent post-strengthening API) so e.g. `self.sub.unknown + 5.0` stops cascading a type-mismatch, matching the treatment given to `self.member`.

Add regression tests that assert no second "type mismatch" diagnostic fires on expressions consuming these poisoned producers.

tasks.title: Broaden find_node test helper to cover all recursive CompiledExprKind variants
tasks.description:
Follow-up to task 448 review. The `find_node` helper at crates/reify-compiler/tests/type_error_propagation_tests.rs:12-44 only recurses into BinOp, UnOp, FunctionCall, Conditional, MethodCall, and IndexAccess — returning None for everything else (Quantifier.predicate, ListLiteral elements, SetLiteral elements, Lambda body, Let/Block, etc.). The quantifier test currently works because it pattern-matches the top-level Quantifier out first, but any future refactor that wraps the quantifier or embeds the predicate inside an unhandled variant would cause the `Type::Error` search to silently return None and the assertion would fail in a misleading way.

Either:
- Extend `find_node` to recurse into all CompiledExprKind variants that contain sub-expressions (Quantifier.predicate, list/set element exprs, Lambda body, Let bindings/body, Block stmts, etc.), OR
- Document the restricted scope at the top of the helper and panic on unknown recursive kinds so coverage gaps surface loudly instead of silently missing.

Preferred: the extend-coverage approach so anti-cascade tests stay robust against AST/IR restructuring.

queries: resolution for: Broaden find_node test helper to cover all recursive CompiledExprKind variants

done_provenance.note: All 5 steps completed by implementer. Tests pass: 1152/1152. find_node now covers all 21 CompiledExprKind variants with no _ wildcard.

tasks.title: Add conformance-layer regression test for Type::Error wildcard in type_compat
tasks.description:
Follow-up to task 448 review. `implicitly_converts_to` and `type_compatible` in crates/reify-compiler/src/type_compat.rs (lines 9-11, 94-96) now return `true` unconditionally when either side is `Type::Error`. This is correct for anti-cascade, but these predicates are also consumed by trait-conformance and field-composition checks — meaning a structure with a Type::Error-poisoned field will silently satisfy any trait signature. Today this is safe because the root-cause diagnostic was emitted at the producer site, but if that diagnostic is ever downgraded/suppressed elsewhere, conformance could silently go green with no visible error.

Add an integration test: compile a structure with a poisoned field (e.g. `x: self.unknown_member` or another known Type::Error producer) that implements a trait expecting a specific field type, and assert:
1. Conformance is not reported as a clean/green success, OR
2. The module's diagnostic buffer contains at least one `Severity::Error` whenever the conformance check succeeds on a Type::Error operand.

Pin this invariant so that future changes to the Type::Error wildcard behavior in type_compat.rs can't silently hide trait-conformance regressions.

tasks.title: Add conformance regression test for Type::Error wildcard in type_compat
tasks.description:
Follow-up to task 448 review. `implicitly_converts_to` and `type_compatible` in crates/reify-compiler/src/type_compat.rs (lines 9-11, 94-96) return `true` unconditionally when either side is `Type::Error`. Correct for anti-cascade, but these predicates are also consumed by trait-conformance and field-composition checks — a structure with a Type::Error-poisoned field will silently satisfy any trait signature. Safe today because the producer diagnostic exists, but if that diagnostic is ever downgraded/suppressed, conformance could silently go green with no visible error.

Add integration test: compile a structure with a poisoned field (e.g. `x: self.unknown_member`) implementing a trait expecting a specific field type, and assert: (1) conformance isn't reported as clean success, OR (2) the diagnostic buffer contains a Severity::Error whenever conformance succeeds on a Type::Error operand. Pin the invariant so future changes to the wildcard can't silently hide trait-conformance regressions.

queries: resolution for: Add conformance regression test for Type::Error wildcard in type_compat

tasks.title: validate_tasks_json.py: multi-tag support + remove dead context param
tasks.description:
Review follow-up from Task 1888. scripts/validate_tasks_json.py hardcodes `data.get('master', {}).get('tasks', [])` and silently ignores any other top-level tags. Taskmaster's schema supports sibling tags alongside `master`; if/when this repo ever uses them, drift would go unchecked.

Fix: iterate every top-level key whose value is a dict with a `tasks` list, running `_validate_tasks` per tag with `context=tag_name` (the currently-dead `context` parameter already anticipates this). At minimum, warn if unexpected top-level keys exist so regressions are loud rather than silent.

Secondary: the `_validate_tasks(..., context: str)` parameter is currently dead — it is always called with `""` and the `prefix` interpolation adds zero value today. Either wire it to real tag names (preferred, per the primary fix) or drop the parameter and `prefix` machinery until needed.

Files: scripts/validate_tasks_json.py (lines 56, 68, 76, 81).

tasks.title: validate_tasks_json.py: harden detection of malformed top-level schema
tasks.description:
Review follow-up from Task 1888. scripts/validate_tasks_json.py silently returns exit 0 when given a malformed tasks.json — the opposite of the validator's stated purpose.

Two concrete gaps:
1. Line 47 (`tasks = data.get("master", {}).get("tasks", [])`): if the top-level JSON is not an object, or `master` is not a dict, or `tasks` is not a list, the `.get()` chain silently yields `[]` and the validator prints no errors. Assert `isinstance(data, dict)`, `isinstance(data.get('master'), dict)`, and `isinstance(data['master'].get('tasks'), list)`, printing an explicit schema error and exiting 1 otherwise.
2. Line 76 (`collections.Counter(t["id"] for t in tasks if "id" in t)`): Counter raises TypeError uncaught if an id is unhashable (list/dict due to corruption). Filter the Counter input to hashable scalars, or wrap the call and report an explicit invariant-3 error for unhashable ids.

Files: scripts/validate_tasks_json.py.

Note: if the multi-tag follow-up lands first, the same assertions should apply to every tag dict, not just `master`.

tasks.title: Add non-zero step_offset companion test for chamfer non-geometry target fallback
tasks.description: The renamed test `compile_modify_op_chamfer_non_geometry_target_fallback` (crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs:2395) compiles at the top level with step_offset=0, so asserting `GeomRef::Step(0)` is indistinguishable from the old bug-preserving behavior. Add a companion test that places `chamfer(scalar_param, dist)` in a context where step_offset > 0 (e.g. nested inside a boolean op / let chain after prior ops in the same realization) and assert the target is `GeomRef::Step(step_offset)`, not `Step(0)`. This will catch a future regression where geom_ref fell back to `Step(0)` unconditionally. Follow-up from task 1823 review.

tasks.title: Add chained-modifier test for chamfer(shell(body, t), d) end-to-end resolution
tasks.description: The new `chamfer_let_bound_target_ops` / `fillet_let_bound_target_ops` tests in crates/reify-compiler/tests/let_scope_tests.rs:975-1018 cover the happy path (let-bound cylinder target resolves to Step(0)) but do not exercise an inline (non-let) geometry arg or a chained modifier. Add a test compiling `let result = chamfer(shell(body, 0.5mm), 2mm)` and assert the op sequence `[Cylinder, Modify(Shell, 0), Modify(Chamfer, 1)]` to cover nested modifier resolution and verify chamfer/fillet participate correctly in recursive geometry resolution (sub_ops extension). Follow-up from task 1823 review.

tasks.title: validate_tasks_json.py: document multi-tag scope + test coverage for metadata skip and cross-tag id reuse
tasks.description:
Review follow-up from Task 1938. After the multi-tag iteration landed in scripts/validate_tasks_json.py, three loose ends remain:

1. Module docstring (scripts/validate_tasks_json.py line ~10) still says invariant 3 is "No duplicate `id` values across `master.tasks[]`". Update to reflect that invariants apply per-tag and tags are independent namespaces (e.g., "No duplicate `id` values within any tag's `tasks[]`; tags are independent namespaces.").

2. Module docstring should also describe the top-level convention used by the loop at scripts/validate_tasks_json.py:64-66 — tags are top-level keys whose names do not start with `_`; any non-tag metadata must be underscore-prefixed. Alternative: replace the loose prefix check with an explicit allow-list of known metadata keys. Prefer documenting the underscore convention since it matches the existing code and will not noisily warn if tm-core adds an `_schemaVersion`-style key; but explicitly call out in the docstring why non-tag metadata must be underscored so future maintainers understand the invariant.

3. Add test coverage in tests/infra/test_tasks_json_schema.sh (around lines 177-219 where unexpected-key WARN tests live) for two untested behavioral promises:
   - A `_meta` (underscore-prefixed) sibling of `master` should produce exit 0 and NO "WARN" on stderr (i.e. underscore keys are silently skipped).
   - The same task id (e.g. `"1"`) appearing in both `master` and a sibling tag (e.g. `feature-x`) must NOT be flagged as a duplicate (tags are independent namespaces — the positive counterpart of the existing cross-tag-orphan-dep test).

Files:
- scripts/validate_tasks_json.py
- tests/infra/test_tasks_json_schema.sh

Memory hints: search for "validate_tasks_json multi-tag", get_task 1938 for the originating merged change. Relates to sibling task 1939 (malformed-schema hardening) — that task covers type-assertion gaps, not docstring/coverage gaps, so these are independent.

tasks.title: Tighten make_poison_literal debug_assert with per-call diagnostic-count snapshot
tasks.description: Follow-up from task 448 review (esc-448-218, S3): the current `make_poison_literal` debug_assert in crates/reify-compiler/src/expr.rs only checks "at least one Severity::Error exists in the diagnostics buffer". Because `diagnostics` is a module-wide Vec that accumulates across the whole compile, this invariant goes near-vacuous after the first error — a poison path that forgot its own push is silently accepted whenever any unrelated earlier error exists. Refactor callers to snapshot `diagnostics.len()` (or a pre-call error-severity count) at the start of each producing function and pass that snapshot into the helper so the assert can verify a NEW error was pushed on this path specifically. Alternatively, document the "best-effort smoke check" framing more prominently. Scope-expanding because it touches the helper signature + every caller.
tasks.details:
Suggested by reviewer_comprehensive during task-448 review. Deferred (not folded) because S3 requires signature changes across every make_poison_literal caller and is larger than the trivial-fold threshold. Files: crates/reify-compiler/src/expr.rs (helper + 3+ call sites). Reviewer's exact wording preserved in escalation esc-448-218.

Dependency 448 added. Also note task 1921 is in-progress on a parallel branch auditing expr.rs producers; if it lands first, the call-site audit for this task must be revalidated against the expanded producer set before planning re-runs.

tasks.title: editor-perf / median() polish: richer failure message, non-finite guard, try/finally cleanup
tasks.description:
Three post-merge review suggestions on the task 1926 work (median-per-keystroke guard). All small, local, and touch only the editor-perf test and its shared test-utils helper.

1) gui/src/__tests__/editor-perf.test.tsx:162 — enrich the Vitest second-arg failure message so CI triage doesn't require mentally computing the median from the raw sample list. Replace the current `samples=${JSON.stringify(perKeystroke)}` with something like:
   `` `median=${median(perKeystroke).toFixed(2)}ms min=${Math.min(...perKeystroke).toFixed(2)} max=${Math.max(...perKeystroke).toFixed(2)} samples=${JSON.stringify(perKeystroke.map(v => +v.toFixed(2)))}` ``

2) gui/src/__tests__/test-utils.ts:26 — `median()` currently rejects NaN but silently accepts ±Infinity. For an even-length array containing Infinity in a middle position the result is `(Infinity + x)/2 = Infinity`, which would fail a `toBeLessThan` with a misleading message (same NaN-propagation rationale the docstring already calls out). Replace `values.some(Number.isNaN)` with `values.some(v => !Number.isFinite(v))` and update the error text and docstring. Add a matching unit test in test-utils.test.ts for ±Infinity inputs.

3) gui/src/__tests__/editor-perf.test.tsx:149 — wrap the measurement loop in `try { ... } finally { unmount(); }` so the 300ms debounce cleanup runs regardless of in-loop failures. Low-risk but makes the cleanup invariant explicit.

Source: review of task 1926 (esc-1926-9).

tasks.title: Fix stale line-number doc reference and restore arg-side variant coverage in type_compat tests
tasks.description:
Two cleanups to the task-1918 error-wildcard tightening artifact:

1. In `crates/reify-compiler/src/type_compat.rs` (~line 117), the doc comment for `type_compatible` references "the internal call on line 139 (implicitly_converts_to(param_ty, arg_ty))". After the debug_assert, short-circuit, identity check, and Int→Real widening branch, the actual call is now at line 142 — line-number references rot quickly. Replace with a symbolic reference such as "the internal call to `implicitly_converts_to(param_ty, arg_ty)` at the end of this function".

2. In `crates/reify-compiler/tests/implicit_conversion_tests.rs`, the producer-side (arg-side) `type_compatible_error_wildcard_mirror_*` coverage was narrowed to just `real` and `list` during the 1918 rewrite. The prior commit pinned nine variants (real, int, bool, string, scalar, list, option, vector, matrix, error_error). Restore the full variant set (matching the established `type_compatible_error_wildcard_mirror_*` naming convention) so the legitimate producer-side anti-cascade path — which the tightened contract explicitly preserves — is pinned against the same variant set as before.

Review followup from task 1918 (suggestions S1 & S3 of esc-1918-13).

tasks.title: Ensure release-mode belt-and-braces tests for Type::Error wildcard actually execute in CI
tasks.description:
In `crates/reify-compiler/tests/implicit_conversion_tests.rs` (lines ~662-792), the belt-and-braces release-mode pins — `error_wildcard_implicit_to_error_release_returns_true_*` and `type_compatible_param_error_release_returns_true_*` — are gated on `#[cfg(not(debug_assertions))]` and therefore only run under `cargo test --release` (or a profile with `debug-assertions = false`). Standard `cargo test` (debug profile) skips them entirely.

The release short-circuit (`if from.is_error() || to.is_error() { return true; }`) is the cascade-safety net called out as the main task-448 rationale preserver by task 1918. If someone regresses by removing that line, debug tests still pass (they only assert the panic) and release tests don't run by default — the regression ships.

Pick one of:
  (a) Wire up a CI job that runs `cargo test --release -p reify-compiler` so these pins execute, and document the requirement in a README or test module header.
  (b) Factor the guard into a private helper (e.g. `is_error_wildcard_short_circuit(from, to)`) testable without the debug_assert, so both paths run under normal `cargo test`.

Review followup from task 1918 (suggestion S2 of esc-1918-13).

tasks.title: Refactor trait_conformance_type_error_tests: extract shared assertion helper & fix severity-filter robustness
tasks.description:
Follow-up to task 1936 review. Two related improvements to crates/reify-compiler/tests/trait_conformance_type_error_tests.rs:

1. DRY / code_reuse (lines 74-103, 143-157): The three-assertion invariant block (errors non-empty, no "type mismatch for trait" cascade, at least one "unknown member") is duplicated verbatim between Scenario A (`poisoned_structure_let_preserves_root_cause_error`) and Scenario B (`poisoned_trait_default_preserves_root_cause_error`) — ~30 lines repeated. Extract a private helper `fn assert_poisoned_conformance_invariant(module: &CompiledModule)` that filters errors and runs the three assertions. Each test becomes `source + helper call`; invariant message text lives in one place.

2. Test robustness (lines 86, 145): The cascade-suppression assertion (b) currently filters `module.diagnostics` by `Severity::Error` before searching for "type mismatch for trait". If a future refactor ever downgrades the conformance-layer cascade to `Severity::Warning` (e.g. during a deprecation period), the test silently passes even though the cascade reappeared — the opposite of the invariant. Fix: for check (b) only, search `module.diagnostics` across all severities for the "type mismatch for trait" substring. Keep checks (a) and (c) on the pre-filtered `errors` vector since they intentionally pin severity.

Acceptance:
- Single helper contains all three assertion messages; both scenarios call it.
- Check (b) examines diagnostics regardless of severity; checks (a) and (c) remain severity-pinned.
- All tests in `cargo test -p reify-compiler --test trait_conformance_type_error_tests` still pass.

tasks.title: Tighten PRELUDE_SENTINEL_OFFSET usage + convergence test assertion
tasks.description:
Two review follow-ups from task 1930:

(1) Code reuse: Replace the raw `u32::MAX as usize` literal at `crates/reify-types/src/source_location.rs:45` with `crate::SourceSpan::PRELUDE_SENTINEL_OFFSET` (both symbols live in the same `reify-types` crate, so the migration is trivial). tasks.title: Extend doc-invariant test to all seven derived FieldSourceKind variants
tasks.description: The `field_source_kind_gradient_doc_describes_provenance_only` test in crates/reify-types/src/value.rs (~line 6976) enforces the "no impl-detail leakage" rule only for `FieldSourceKind::Gradient`. A future author rewording any of those docs could silently reintroduce `lambda slot` / `sample handler` / `central-difference` language.
tasks.details:
Parameterize (or loop) the Gradient doc-invariant test over all seven derived-field variants: Gradient, Divergence, Curl, Laplacian, VonMises, PrincipalStresses, MaxShear, SafetyFactor. For each variant assert (a) the doc block omits the forbidden impl tokens ("lambda slot", "sample handler", "central-difference") and (b) mentions the variant's operator name (e.g. `gradient()`, `divergence()`, `curl()`, `laplacian()`, `von_mises()`, `principal_stresses()`, `max_shear()`, `safety_factor()`).

The `enum_variant_doc_region` helper already supports locating any variant; pass `"FieldSourceKind"` and the per-variant line (e.g. `"    Divergence,\n"`). Only the variants table and per-variant operator name strings need to be added.

Source: review escalation esc-1002-30, suggestion 1.

tasks.title: conformance.rs: fix pass2_skipped phantom `(name, Let) -> Type::Real` advertisement
tasks.description:
In `crates/reify-compiler/src/conformance.rs` the `available_defaults` map still emits a `(name, Let) -> Type::Real` entry when Pass 2 recorded the name in `pass2_skipped` (an annotated Param/Let won the slot). The rationale comment at ~324-330 asserts "the requirement is structurally unsatisfied anyway (kind mismatch)" — but a Let requirement looking up `(name, Let)` gets a kind MATCH and will emit a spurious `requirement expects <T>, available default has Real` type-mismatch when "missing required member" would be clearer.

Scenario: trait X requires `let x : Length`; trait Y provides `param x : Length`; trait Z provides unannotated `let x = 5.0`. The Let from Z survives `collect_all_requirements` dedup, Pass 2 skips registering it because Y's Param claimed the slot, and the requirement in X is evaluated against a phantom `Let -> Real` advertisement.

Memory hints: search "task 1907 pass2_skipped available_defaults phantom Let entry"; entities: DefaultKind, AvailableDefaultKind, Type.
tasks.details:
Two viable fixes (reviewer proposed both):

Option A — In Pass 2's conflict branch (`if let Some(ignored_ty)` arm), still insert the compiled expression into `inferred_let_exprs` (or a parallel map) so `available_defaults` can advertise the inferred type for the `(name, Let)` slot. This preserves the Let requirement-matching path even when Pass 1 won the scope slot.

Option B — In the `available_defaults` builder, skip emitting the `(name, Let)` entry entirely when `pass2_skipped.contains(name)` — since no Let cell is actually injected, advertising one is misleading. Update the rationale comment at ~324-330 to reflect this.

Option B is simpler and keeps the invariant "only one default satisfies a given (name, kind)"; Option A preserves the old shape but requires reasoning about why the skipped expression is still useful. Prefer Option B unless the Let-requirement-survives-slot-claim behavior is intentional.

Add regression test in `crates/reify-compiler/tests/trait_merge_tests.rs` covering the 3-trait scenario: structure bound to traits X (requires `let x : Length`), Y (provides `param x : Length`), Z (provides unannotated `let x = 5.0`). Assert ONE diagnostic ("missing required Let member `x`" or "kind mismatch" or the chosen wording) — NOT a secondary "available default has Real" type-mismatch.

Verify: `cargo test -p reify-compiler` green; `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` clean; `cargo fmt --check`.

Origin: reviewer_comprehensive on task 1907 (esc-1907-20, suggestion 1). Complements but distinct from task 1914 item 1 (compile_expr error-cascade), which addresses the Type::Real fallback when inference FAILS; this task addresses the fallback when Pass 2 deliberately DISCARDS the inferred expression.

tasks.title: conformance.rs: restore Pass 1 ↔ Pass 2 symmetry for annotated-Let + Param cell-injection collisions
tasks.description:
The two-pass pre-register split in `crates/reify-compiler/src/conformance.rs` (task 1834/1907) suppresses duplicate Let-cell injection only when an *unannotated* Let loses to a Param/annotated-Let in Pass 1 (via `pass2_skipped`). The symmetric case — an annotated Let whose name was already claimed in Pass 1 by a Param registered earlier in the same Pass 1 loop — still causes the injection loop to emit both a Param cell and an annotated-Let cell for the same `(entity, member)` pair.

The `cross_kind_pre_registration_preserves_first_type` test (updated in commit 7a7c7127d) pins 1 cell in the Param + unannotated-Let case, but a Param + annotated-Let case is not covered by any test and still produces 2 cells. Pre-existing behavior, but inconsistent with the design comment at ~197-215 that frames the split as restoring symmetry.

Memory hints: search "task 1907 pass2_skipped pass1_skipped annotated-Let Param duplicate cells"; entities: DefaultKind, AvailableDefaultKind.
tasks.details:
Two options (reviewer proposed both):

Option A (symmetric fix) — Extend Pass 1's Occupied path to mark annotated-Let conflicts in a new `pass1_skipped: HashSet<String>` set consumed by the injection loop the same way `pass2_skipped` is. This makes the two arms (Pass 1 / Pass 2) behaviorally identical: first-seen wins, and the loser's cell injection is suppressed regardless of kind.

Option B (documented asymmetry) — Explicitly call out in the design comment at ~197-215 that annotated-Let + Param name collisions still emit duplicate cells. Add a follow-up task link. Pin current behavior with a test so future refactors don't silently flip it.

Recommendation: Option A. The injection loop already reads `pass2_skipped`; adding a parallel `pass1_skipped` consumed at the same checkpoint is a small surgical change and removes a reviewer-accessible surprise. Option B effectively documents "we know this is inconsistent" and adds no mechanical guarantee.

Add test in `crates/reify-compiler/tests/trait_merge_tests.rs` mirroring `cross_kind_pre_registration_preserves_first_type` but using annotated-Let + Param (both with explicit types). Assert exactly 1 cell for the shared name (whichever default Pass 1 sees first wins).

Verify: `cargo test -p reify-compiler` green; `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` clean; `cargo fmt --check`.

Origin: reviewer_comprehensive on task 1907 (esc-1907-20, suggestion 3).

tasks.title: Tighten handleSave errorSpy assertion to match sibling-test pattern
tasks.description:
Replace the substring `some(...)` check in gui/src/__tests__/App.test.tsx (around lines 2448-2453) with the sibling-test pattern used at lines 1283-1286 and 1323-1326:

```
expect(errorSpy).not.toHaveBeenCalledWith('Save failed:', expect.any(Error));
```

This is both stricter (asserts exact call shape rather than substring match on a stringified arg) and more intention-revealing (explicitly names the forbidden logging path). It matches the convention already established by the setParameter and re-evaluate sibling tests in the same file.

Context: Task 1892 narrowed the original `expect(errorSpy).not.toHaveBeenCalled()` to tolerate incidental console.error noise, but chose a substring-match approach. Reviewer_comprehensive flagged two concerns:
1. test_coverage_weakening: substring match tolerates other Save-path regressions whose log strings don't contain the exact tokens
2. assertion_precision: relies on `String(err)` producing 'Error: disk full' — fragile if handleSave ever logs a different format

Additionally, leave a code comment at the incidental-noise source (or at the assertion) so future readers know why a targeted assertion is preferred over `not.toHaveBeenCalled()`.

Note: handleSave in gui/src/App.tsx currently does not call console.error at all (only `showToast(\`Save failed: ...\`, 'error')`), so the targeted assertion would pass trivially today and serve purely as a regression guard — which matches the sibling-test intent.

Source: Task 1793 review (reviewer_comprehensive), re-surfaced during Task 1892 review.

tasks.title: WP-A RECOVERY: Audit task 1901 DesignTree wiring and restore missing multi-selection bindings (from lost task 1959)
tasks.description: Recovery audit task. Task 1959 was silently overwritten on 2026-04-17 during a fused-memory DB-lock storm (escalation esc-1959-52). Its original purpose was to act on a task-1744 review finding that DesignTree was still not instantiated in App.tsx as of 2026-04-17, despite task 1901 having been marked done earlier. This task (a) audits whether the 1901 wiring actually landed and (b) if not, restores it with the multi-selection bindings that were added by task 1744.
tasks.details:
Origin: preserved content from the overwritten task 1959 — see /home/leo/src/dark-factory/data/backups/2026-04-18-wp-a/esc-1959-52.json for the full original spec.

## Audit step

2. If DesignTree IS imported and rendered in App.tsx with selection state wired — mark this task done and re-verify task 1901 completion evidence.
3. If DesignTree is NOT imported in App.tsx (the state task 1744 review observed) — proceed with implementation below.

## Implementation scope (if audit finds wiring missing)
Render <DesignTree> in gui/src/App.tsx inside the panel area, connected to:
- tree from the current design-tree source
- viewStateStore (existing binding)
- selectedEntity (primary selection)
- selectedEntities + anchorEntity from selectionStore state
- onSelect(path, mods): mods.ctrl → selectionStore.toggleSelect(path); mods.shift → selectionStore.rangeSelect(path); else → existing handleViewportSelect(path, mods)
- onRangeSelect(paths) → selectionStore.rangeSelect variant
- onSelectAll(paths) → selectionStore.selectAll(paths)

## Prerequisite (may need to be a separate task)
Original 1959 spec included a prerequisite: "Plumb entity-tree bridge + engineStore.entityTree to feed DesignTree"
- gui/src/bridge.ts — add getEntityTree() invoke wrapper around Tauri get_entity_tree command (backend: gui/src-tauri/src/main.rs:233, commands.rs:106; returns Vec<EntityTreeNode>)
- gui/src/stores/engineStore.ts — add entityTree: EntityTreeNode[] state + setEntityTree mutation; call bridgeGetEntityTree() inside initFromState and after mesh/value/constraint mutations
- tests for the bridge wrapper and engineStore field+refresh
If present state shows engineStore.state.entityTree already populated, skip this.

## Acceptance
- Audit outcome documented (wired or not).
- If wiring missing: user can click, Ctrl+click, Shift+click, Ctrl+A inside rendered DesignTree with selection highlight + viewport sync.
- App.test.tsx continues to pass; add smoke test for DesignTree render + click → selectionStore update.

## Context
From review of task 1744 (reviewer_comprehensive): "grep for DesignTree outside test files shows only the panels/index.ts export. App.tsx does not instantiate it." Task 1901 had been marked done but is suspected incomplete or reverted.
<info added on 2026-04-19T20:55:17.064Z>
## Update 2026-04-19 — predecessor 1901 cancelled, this task is authoritative

Task 1901 was cancelled on 2026-04-19 (not "suspected incomplete" — confirmed: the 2026-04-17 merge resolved only tasks.json and App.tsx was never touched). The dependency on 1901 has been removed; this task is fully unblocked.

### Concrete current-main state (audit short-circuit)

- `grep -n 'DesignTree' gui/src/App.tsx` returns **nothing** — wiring is definitively missing. Skip the audit branch and go straight to implementation.
- Backend `get_entity_tree` Tauri command is already registered (gui/src-tauri/src/main.rs:233, commands.rs:106). Do NOT add it.
- `getEntityTree()` / `onEntityTreeUpdate()` bridge wrappers are NOT in gui/src/bridge.ts. They must be added here — do NOT treat this as a separate prerequisite task.
- Backend does NOT emit an `entity-tree-update` event. Do NOT add event plumbing here; instead refresh the tree client-side on engine updates (piggyback on the existing `engineStore.subscribeToEvents` callback or a `createEffect` keyed on `evalStatus.phase === 'idle'` transitions). Never poll on a timer.
- `viewStateStore` (stores/viewStateStore.ts) is on main with full view/auto-view management (`regenerateAutoViews`, `getAllEffective`). Use it as the source of truth for visibility.
- `DesignTree` already supports `selectedEntities`, `anchorEntity`, `onSelect(path, mods)`, `onRangeSelect(paths)`, `onSelectAll(paths)`, Ctrl+A, bulk visibility cycling.

### Additional required step (missing from original spec)

**Visibility → viewport propagation.** After `viewStateStore` state changes, push `viewStateStore.getAllEffective()` into `meshManager.setVisibility(path, state)` per entry. The meshManager is owned by `Viewport`; expose a new ref hook on `Viewport` mirroring the existing `flyToEntityRef`/`fitToViewRef` pattern, and drive it from a `createEffect` in App.tsx that reads `viewStateStore.state.explicit` and `viewStateStore.state.activeViewId`. Without this step the eye-icon clicks in DesignTree will mutate the store but the viewport will not respond — this is the defining end-to-end failure mode.

### Scope discipline

Do NOT edit: `DesignTree.tsx`, `DesignTreeContextMenu.tsx`, `viewStateStore.ts`, `autoViewGenerator.ts`, any Rust backend file. If you find yourself in those files, stop — you've drifted out of scope.

### engineStore.entityTree note

The original spec suggests adding `entityTree` to `engineStore`. That's optional — keeping the tree as a local `createSignal` in App.tsx is equally fine and less invasive. Choose whichever fits best after reading current `engineStore.ts`. The test expectations should not assume either layout.
</info added on 2026-04-19T20:55:17.064Z>

tasks.title: viewStateStore: atomic regenerateAutoViews and user-view explicit write-back
tasks.description: regenerateAutoViews performs two sequential setState calls (setTree prunes explicit, then a second produce() block replaces explicit for auto:* active views). Each setState triggers reactive subscribers independently, exposing an intermediate state and wasting the initial prune. Separately, for user:* active views, edits made via setVisibility are written to state.explicit but never mirrored back into state.views[activeViewId].visibility, so the view's stored visibility drifts from the live edits — a later "restore view"/"export view" feature would surprise the user.
tasks.details:
## Scope

gui/src/stores/viewStateStore.ts

### 1. Atomicity (lines 264-330)

Fold the setTree pruning into the same produce() pass as the view regeneration, OR skip the explicit-prune inside setTree when called from regenerateAutoViews (since explicit is replaced moments later on the auto:* branch anyway). Rebuild nodeByPath/parentByPath in the same step. Goal: one setState, one reactive notification.

### 2. User-view write-back (lines 305-322)

Pick and document one semantic:
- (a) `ViewDefinition.visibility` is a seed/template; live state lives only in `state.explicit`. Add a doc comment on ViewDefinition and seedView explaining this.
- (b) Mirror setVisibility edits back into `state.views[activeViewId].visibility` when active view is `user:*` (and possibly stamp `modified=true` on auto views — see Task XXXX for `modified` cleanup).

Prefer (b) so user views remain self-contained, but either is acceptable as long as it's documented.

### Tests

Add a regression test that calls setVisibility while a user:* view is active, then asserts that either (a) state.views[activeViewId].visibility is unchanged and the store doc says so, or (b) state.views[activeViewId].visibility reflects the edit.

- search: "ViewDefinition visibility explicit"

tasks.title: autoViewGenerator: replace substring type-name matching with anchored checks
tasks.description: Geometry and material detection in autoViewGenerator.ts uses bare `type_name.includes('Solid' | 'Surface' | 'Curve' | 'Material')`. Any type whose name happens to contain those tokens (e.g. a hypothetical 'MySolidarity', 'MaterialReference') will be misclassified. This is the sole distinction between 'hidden' and 'show' in the default view, so misclassification silently hides (or unhides) geometry.
tasks.details:
## Scope

gui/src/stores/autoViewGenerator.ts

### Changes

- `isLetGeometryType` (lines 38-46): replace `type_name.includes('Solid' | 'Surface' | 'Curve')` with anchored matching. Options:
  - Equality against a known-type enum / set: `GEOMETRY_TYPES = new Set(['Solid', 'Surface', 'Curve'])` with `GEOMETRY_TYPES.has(type_name)`.
  - If generics/wrappers must be tolerated (e.g. `Option<Solid>`), use a regex anchored on word boundaries or parse the type_name.

- `manufacturingReadyVisibilityFor` (lines 130-144): same fix for `'Material'` check.

### Tests

gui/src/__tests__ — add regression tests with node `type_name` values that contain the substrings but should NOT match (e.g. 'Solidarity', 'MaterialReference', 'SurfaceTreatment', 'CurveBall'). Assert they render via defaultVisibilityFor / manufacturingReadyVisibilityFor as non-geometry / non-material.

tasks.title: Tighten should_panic substring in helper_flags_cascade_at_warning_severity
tasks.description:
Follow-up to task 1947 review. In crates/reify-compiler/tests/trait_conformance_type_error_tests.rs:154, the regression test `helper_flags_cascade_at_warning_severity` uses `#[should_panic(expected = "type mismatch for trait")]`. That substring appears in multiple places that could satisfy the predicate spuriously: (1) in check (b)'s assertion prose ("unexpected 'type mismatch for trait' cascade"), (2) in the `{:?}` dump of `module.diagnostics` that check (b) appends, and (3) potentially in the `errors: {:?}` dump of check (c) if the errors vector ever held such a message. If a regression caused check (a) or (c) to panic first with a diagnostics-quoting message, the should_panic would accidentally pass — obscuring which check actually caught the cascade.

Fix: change the expected substring to something distinctive to check (b)'s own prose, e.g. `expected = "unexpected 'type mismatch for trait' cascade"`. That phrase appears only in check (b)'s assertion message, so a regression in (a)/(c) ordering cannot satisfy the predicate.

Acceptance:
- should_panic expected substring is unique to check (b)'s prose.
- `cargo test -p reify-compiler --test trait_conformance_type_error_tests` passes.

tasks.title: constraint_def cleanup: demote format_shadow_warning to fn and drop redundant type-param guard
tasks.description:
Two small cleanups in crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs flagged during task 872 review: (1) format_shadow_warning is pub with a rationale ("so tests can build the expected string without duplicating the format literal") but no test actually calls it — the shadow-warning test uses substring matches. Demote to `fn` (crate-private) and drop the pub justification from the doc; if a test ever needs it, promote with `#[cfg(test)] pub(crate)`. (2) In compile_constraint_def, the param-type check has `resolve_type_expr_with_aliases(...).is_none() && ... && !type_param_names.contains(name.as_str())`. resolve_type_expr_with_aliases internally returns Some(Type::TypeParam(..)) when the name is a type param, so the explicit `!type_param_names.contains(...)` clause is dead — drop it.</description>
<parameter name="details">Locations: crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs lines ~60-73 (format_shadow_warning) and ~116-124 (param-type check).

tasks.title: Broaden or reword constraint-def param "unknown type" diagnostic to avoid false positives on struct/entity/trait names
tasks.description:
The new "unknown type" diagnostic in compile_constraint_def (crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs ~113-132) only whitelists builtins (via resolve_type_expr_with_aliases), type parameters, aliases, and enums. It does NOT recognize structure/entity/trait names in scope. If a user writes `constraint def Foo { param s: MyStructure ... }`, they now get an error at def-compile time even though previously the raw ConstraintDef was just cloned into the registry and no such check ran.

Options: (a) broaden the whitelist to also accept structure/entity/trait names in scope; (b) keep the current strictness but reword the error to explicitly list acceptable categories ("expected a builtin scalar, type param, alias, or enum") so users see the restriction isn't arbitrary. Pick one and add a regression test.
tasks.details:
Analogue precedent: compile_function has the same blind spot. If (a) is chosen, consider whether compile_function should be updated in parallel.

memory_hints: search "constraint def unknown type param diagnostic" for task 872 review context; search "compile_function unknown type" for the analogue.

tasks.title: Wire view persistence load-on-open and debounced-save into App.tsx orchestration
tasks.description: Connect Task 1749's persistence primitives to the app reactive graph: load sidecar (priority) or localStorage on file open before auto view generation; run the 500ms debounced saver after any viewStateStore change. Requires 1746/1747 UI producers to be real before it's useful end-to-end.
tasks.details:
Task 1749 ships `viewPersistence.ts` (localStorage + debounced saver), `sidecarPersistence.ts` (priority loader), `fuzzyPathMatcher.ts`, and `viewReconciliation.ts`. The orchestration glue — load on file open, re-evaluate entity tree through `viewReconciliation.partition()`, subscribe debounced saver to viewStateStore — lives at the App.tsx layer and was deferred because the UI producers (1746/1747) were not actually present on the base branch.

### Steps
1. On file open (after entity tree is built), call the priority loader; if present, feed into viewStateStore.hydrate(persistedState).
2. Before auto view generation, gate on whether hydration occurred; if yes, skip fresh defaults and instead reconcile against the new entity tree.
3. Subscribe the debounced localStorage saver to viewStateStore changes.
4. On entity tree rebuild, call viewReconciliation to partition kept / new / missing, moving missing-path explicit entries into staleExplicit.
5. Run fuzzy recovery and surface non-blocking notification for rename matches.

- search: "viewPersistence priority loader"
